definition: A civilisation exhibits progress if and only if (i) each successive generation possesses more material wealth than the preceding one, (ii) the stock of scientific and technical understanding increases cumulatively, and (iii) the members of the civilisation themselves regard their condition as an improvement upon that of their forebears.
conditions: each successive generation possesses more material wealth
stock of scientific and technical understanding increases cumulatively
members regard their condition as an improvement upon that of their forebears
counterexample: The transition from the Roman Republic to the early Imperial period saw enormous increases in material wealth and significant advances in engineering and architecture, yet many Romans — Tacitus, Sallust, Juvenal — regarded the Empire as a moral decline from the austere virtues of the Republic. The third condition fails, since the members of the civilisation did not regard their condition as an improvement, yet by any external measure the civilisation had progressed in wealth and technical capacity.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the members of the civilisation regard their situation as improved, but progress in material and technical domains may coincide with a widespread perception of moral or cultural decline, so that progress and the consciousness of progress need not accompany one another.
passage: One might propose that a civilisation exhibits progress if and only if each successive generation possesses more material wealth than the preceding one, the stock of scientific and technical understanding increases cumulatively, and the members of the civilisation themselves regard their condition as an improvement upon that of their forebears. This definition captures certain paradigmatic cases of progress: the expansion of European industry in the nineteenth century, during which wealth multiplied, scientific discovery accelerated, and a prevailing optimism about the future took hold among the educated classes. Yet the definition proves inadequate when one considers the transition from the Roman Republic to the early Imperial period. Under Augustus and his successors, the material wealth of the Roman world increased enormously. Trade flourished across the Mediterranean; the engineering of roads, aqueducts, and public buildings reached unprecedented sophistication; and the population of the capital grew to a size unmatched in the ancient world. The first two conditions are amply satisfied. But the Romans themselves were far from unanimous in regarding their condition as improved. Tacitus wrote with bitter nostalgia of the old republican virtues — the frugality, the martial discipline, the independence of spirit — which he believed had been corroded by imperial luxury. Sallust lamented the moral corruption that had accompanied the growth of wealth. Juvenal satirised the decadence of a city that had traded its liberties for bread and circuses. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that material and technical advancement must be accompanied by a subjective sense of betterment, when in truth a civilisation may advance in measurable respects whilst its most articulate members insist that it has declined.

definition: An account constitutes an historical explanation if and only if (i) it identifies a set of antecedent conditions that obtained prior to the event, (ii) it subsumes the event under a general law connecting such conditions to outcomes of that type, and (iii) the explanation is such that, given the antecedent conditions and the law, the event could have been predicted in advance.
conditions: identifies antecedent conditions prior to the event
subsumes the event under a general law
event could have been predicted in advance given the conditions and law
counterexample: The historian who explains the outbreak of the French Revolution by tracing the fiscal crisis of the ancien régime, the failure of successive finance ministers, the intransigence of the aristocracy, and the particular sequence of events in the summer of 1789 does not subsume these under any general law, nor does the explanation render the Revolution predictable. There is no covering law of the form 'whenever fiscal crisis and aristocratic intransigence obtain, revolution follows.' The second and third conditions fail, yet the narrative account is widely regarded as a genuine and illuminating explanation.
missing_condition: The definition demands subsumption under general laws and predictability, but historical explanation frequently proceeds by tracing a particular causal narrative — showing how one event led to another in the specific circumstances — without invoking any law from which the outcome could have been deduced.
passage: It has been maintained that an account constitutes an historical explanation if and only if it identifies a set of antecedent conditions that obtained prior to the event in question, it subsumes the event under a general law connecting such conditions to outcomes of that type, and the explanation is such that, given the antecedent conditions and the law, the event could have been predicted in advance. This is the covering-law model applied to historical inquiry, and it appears to work tolerably well for certain regularities: the collapse of a bridge may be explained by the antecedent conditions of its loading and the laws of structural mechanics. Yet consider the explanation a competent historian offers for the outbreak of the French Revolution. He traces the fiscal crisis of the Bourbon monarchy, the failure of Calonne and Necker to achieve reform, the summoning of the Estates-General, the intransigence of the privileged orders, the popular fury of July 1789, and the fall of the Bastille. This narrative is detailed, persuasive, and widely regarded as explanatory. But it does not subsume the Revolution under any general law. There is no law of the form 'whenever a monarchy faces fiscal crisis and the aristocracy resists reform, revolution follows,' for many monarchies have faced precisely those conditions without revolution resulting. Nor could the Revolution have been predicted from the antecedent conditions; as late as the spring of 1789, few if any observers foresaw the events of the summer. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has imported from the natural sciences a model of explanation that does not fit the practice of historical inquiry, where explanation characteristically proceeds by tracing a particular causal narrative rather than by deducing events from covering laws.

definition: A division of the past into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) the boundaries between periods correspond to abrupt and decisive changes in the political order, (ii) the events within each period share a recognisable unity of character, and (iii) the transition from one period to the next is acknowledged by contemporaries as a rupture.
conditions: boundaries correspond to abrupt and decisive political changes
events within each period share recognisable unity of character
transition acknowledged by contemporaries as a rupture
counterexample: The periodisation of European history into 'mediaeval' and 'early modern' places the boundary somewhere around 1500. Yet no single political upheaval marks that transition; the changes were gradual — the slow decline of feudal arrangements, the rise of centrally governed states, the spread of printing, the voyages of exploration. Nor did contemporaries around 1500 regard themselves as living through a rupture; most would have been unaware that one age was ending and another beginning. The first and third conditions fail, yet the periodisation is one of the most firmly established in historical scholarship.
missing_condition: The definition requires that periodisation be grounded in abrupt political change and contemporary recognition of rupture, but many of the most widely accepted periodisations correspond to gradual transformations in culture, economy, and thought that were invisible to those who lived through them.
passage: One might hold that a division of the past into periods is legitimate if and only if the boundaries between periods correspond to abrupt and decisive changes in the political order, the events within each period share a recognisable unity of character, and the transition from one period to the next is acknowledged by contemporaries as a rupture. This definition seems plausible when applied to certain cases. The fall of the Roman Empire in the West, conventionally dated to 476, marks a boundary between antiquity and the mediaeval period; it was a dramatic political event, and something like a new character of European life did emerge in its aftermath. Yet the definition encounters serious difficulty with the transition from the mediaeval to the early modern period, conventionally placed around 1500. No single abrupt political change marks this boundary. The decline of feudal arrangements was gradual, extending over centuries. The rise of centralised monarchies was a slow process visible in retrospect but scarcely perceptible from year to year. The spread of printing, the voyages of discovery, the stirring of new intellectual currents — all these developed over decades rather than erupting in a single upheaval. Nor did contemporaries recognise a rupture. A scholar in Paris in 1510 would not have supposed himself to be living at the dawn of a new age; he would have felt continuity with the intellectual traditions of the preceding centuries. The first and third conditions fail. Yet the division between mediaeval and early modern is one of the most firmly established in historical scholarship. The definition has demanded political drama and contemporary consciousness of change, when the most enduring periodisations frequently correspond to transformations so gradual that only later generations can discern them.

definition: A society constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) it possesses a system of writing, (ii) it has developed monumental architecture, and (iii) it maintains a specialised priestly or scholarly class devoted to the preservation and transmission of learning.
conditions: possesses a system of writing
has developed monumental architecture
maintains a specialised priestly or scholarly class
counterexample: The Inca Empire governed a territory stretching thousands of miles along the Andes, maintained a sophisticated administrative apparatus, built the great citadel of Machu Picchu and an extraordinary network of roads, and sustained a complex agricultural economy. Yet the Inca did not possess a system of writing in the usual sense; they employed quipu — knotted strings — for recording information, but this is not writing as the definition envisages it. The first condition fails, yet no student of the past would hesitate to call the Inca Empire a civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition equates civilisation with literacy, but a society may achieve the organisational complexity, monumental construction, and cultural sophistication characteristic of civilisation without developing a system of writing, employing instead other means of recording and transmitting information.
passage: It is sometimes proposed that a society constitutes a civilisation if and only if it possesses a system of writing, it has developed monumental architecture, and it maintains a specialised priestly or scholarly class devoted to the preservation and transmission of learning. This definition captures the great civilisations of the ancient Near East admirably. Mesopotamia developed cuneiform, Egypt developed hieroglyphics, and both produced monumental temples and a learned scribal class. The three conditions are satisfied, and the designation of these societies as civilisations commands universal assent. Yet the definition proves too restrictive when one turns to the Americas. The Inca Empire was, by any reasonable measure, one of the great civilisations of the pre-Columbian world. It governed a territory of extraordinary extent along the spine of the Andes, from modern Ecuador to Chile. It built Machu Picchu, Sacsayhuamán, and a road network rivalling that of Rome. It maintained a complex administrative apparatus, a sophisticated agricultural system of terraced farming, and a hierarchical society with a specialised ruling and priestly class. Yet the Inca did not possess a system of writing in the conventional sense. They employed quipu — knotted strings of varying colours and lengths — to record numerical and possibly narrative information, but this falls outside what the definition means by writing. The first condition fails. The definition has taken a feature characteristic of the literate civilisations of Eurasia and imposed it as a universal requirement, when a society may achieve the organisational complexity and cultural accomplishment that merit the designation of civilisation without having developed writing as such.

definition: A civilisation is in decline if and only if (i) its military power diminishes relative to its rivals, (ii) its territory contracts, and (iii) its population decreases over a sustained period.
conditions: military power diminishes relative to rivals
territory contracts
population decreases over a sustained period
counterexample: The Ottoman Empire in the eighteenth century is widely regarded as having been in decline: its administrative apparatus had become sclerotic, its provincial governors increasingly autonomous, and its cultural dynamism much reduced from the age of Suleiman. Yet its territory, though somewhat diminished, remained vast, and its population was in fact growing throughout the century. The second condition is only partially met and the third fails entirely, yet historians speak confidently of Ottoman decline in this period.
missing_condition: The definition locates decline exclusively in measurable indices of military, territorial, and demographic contraction, but a civilisation may be in decline by virtue of institutional decay, administrative dysfunction, and loss of cultural vitality even whilst its territory remains extensive and its population grows.
passage: One might define decline as follows: a civilisation is in decline if and only if its military power diminishes relative to its rivals, its territory contracts, and its population decreases over a sustained period. This definition appears to fit certain well-known cases. The Western Roman Empire in its final centuries suffered military reverses, lost province after province, and saw the population of its cities dwindle. All three conditions obtain, and the diagnosis of decline is uncontroversial. Yet consider the Ottoman Empire in the eighteenth century. It is a commonplace of historical scholarship that the Ottoman state was in decline during this period — that the vigour and administrative competence of the classical age had given way to corruption, provincial fragmentation, and institutional rigidity. The Janissary corps had degenerated from an elite fighting force into a self-serving political faction. Provincial governors acted with increasing independence from the capital. The cultural and intellectual life of the empire, once magnificent, had lost much of its former energy. Yet the Ottoman territory, though somewhat reduced by the Treaty of Karlowitz and subsequent agreements, remained enormous, stretching from the Balkans to Mesopotamia and from the Crimea to North Africa. And the Ottoman population was not decreasing; demographic evidence suggests that it was growing throughout much of the century. The second condition is only imperfectly satisfied and the third fails altogether. The definition has equated decline with measurable contraction — fewer soldiers, less territory, fewer people — when a civilisation may decline through the quieter processes of institutional decay, administrative dysfunction, and the slow ebbing of cultural confidence.

definition: An historical tradition is constituted if and only if (i) the practices or ideas in question can be traced in an unbroken chain of transmission from their origin to the present, (ii) each generation receives the tradition from its immediate predecessor, and (iii) the practitioners are conscious of participating in a lineage extending into the past.
conditions: unbroken chain of transmission from origin to present
each generation receives tradition from immediate predecessor
practitioners conscious of participating in a lineage
counterexample: The tradition of classical republican political thought, which influenced the American and French revolutionaries of the eighteenth century, was not transmitted in an unbroken chain. The writings of Cicero, Livy, and Polybius were largely neglected during the early mediaeval period, rediscovered by Renaissance humanists, and then taken up afresh by Enlightenment thinkers. The chain of transmission was broken and repaired; the second condition fails for the mediaeval interval. Yet republican thought is consistently described as a tradition.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuous generational transmission, but intellectual and cultural traditions may be interrupted for centuries and then revived through the recovery of texts and monuments, so that a tradition may persist across a gap by means of its material record rather than by person-to-person transmission.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an historical tradition is constituted if and only if the practices or ideas in question can be traced in an unbroken chain of transmission from their origin to the present, each generation receives the tradition from its immediate predecessor, and the practitioners are conscious of participating in a lineage extending into the past. This account captures certain traditions well — the tradition of Confucian scholarship in China, for example, which has been transmitted from master to pupil over millennia with a remarkable degree of continuity. Yet one might observe that this definition proves too demanding when applied to the tradition of classical republican political thought in the West. The political ideas of Cicero, Livy, and Polybius — the virtues of the citizen, the dangers of tyranny, the mixed constitution — exercised a profound influence upon the American and French revolutionaries of the late eighteenth century. Jefferson, Adams, and Madison read the Roman authors with care and regarded themselves as heirs to a republican tradition. Yet the chain of transmission was not unbroken. During much of the early mediaeval period, these texts were little read and their political ideas were largely dormant. It was the Renaissance humanists who rediscovered and revived classical republican thought, and it was from this revival — not from an unbroken chain of transmission — that the Enlightenment thinkers received it. The second condition fails for the intervening centuries. The definition has assumed that traditions must be continuously transmitted from hand to hand, overlooking the possibility that a tradition may survive in written texts and be revived after a long period of neglect, so that the continuity of the tradition is maintained not by an unbroken human chain but by the persistence of its documentary record.

definition: An account of the past counts as a narrative if and only if (i) it presents events in chronological order, (ii) the events are connected by causal links such that each event brings about or contributes to the next, and (iii) the account has a clearly defined beginning and end.
conditions: events presented in chronological order
events connected by causal links
account has clearly defined beginning and end
counterexample: Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War frequently departs from strict chronological order, employing retrospective digressions — the Archaeology, the Pentekontaetia — to illuminate the background of events narrated later. These passages violate chronological sequence, yet the History of the Peloponnesian War is one of the most celebrated historical narratives ever composed. The first condition fails, yet the work is a paradigmatic narrative.
missing_condition: The definition requires strict chronological ordering, but historical narratives characteristically employ retrospection, digression, and thematic organisation, departing from chronological sequence in order to render events intelligible without thereby ceasing to be narratives.
passage: One might hold that an account of the past counts as a narrative if and only if it presents events in chronological order, the events are connected by causal links such that each brings about or contributes to the next, and the account has a clearly defined beginning and end. This definition seems to capture certain familiar narratives: a chronicle of a king's reign that proceeds year by year from accession to death, tracing how each decision led to the next. Yet one should hesitate to accept the definition without qualification. Suppose one considers Thucydides' History of the Peloponnesian War, universally regarded as one of the finest historical narratives in existence. Thucydides does not confine himself to strict chronological order. The famous Archaeology, which opens the work, surveys the development of Greek power from the earliest times, reaching back centuries before the war itself. The Pentekontaetia traces the growth of Athenian power in the fifty years preceding the conflict. These retrospective passages interrupt the chronological flow of the narrative, inserting earlier material into the account of later events. The first condition — that events be presented in chronological order — is violated. Yet no one would deny that Thucydides has produced a narrative, and a masterly one. The definition has mistaken a feature of the simplest narratives — strict chronological sequence — for an essential property of narrative as such. In practice, the most accomplished historical narratives rely upon retrospection, anticipation, and thematic digression, departing freely from the order in which events occurred in order to render them intelligible.

definition: An historical account commits an anachronism if and only if (i) it attributes to persons of a past age a concept or practice that did not exist in their time, (ii) the attribution is unintentional on the part of the historian, and (iii) the attribution distorts the historian's overall interpretation of the period.
conditions: attributes to past persons a concept or practice not existing in their time
attribution is unintentional
attribution distorts the overall interpretation
counterexample: A historian who describes the mediaeval Church as exercising a 'monopoly' on education is committing an anachronism — the concept of monopoly, in its economic sense, is a product of later centuries and does not accurately describe the Church's educational role. But the historian may be perfectly aware that the term is anachronistic and may employ it deliberately as a convenient shorthand. The second condition fails, yet the attribution remains anachronistic: the concept still does not belong to the period described.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the anachronism be unintentional, but a deliberate and knowing use of a concept foreign to the period described is no less anachronistic for being conscious; the anachronism lies in the mismatch between concept and period, not in the historian's state of awareness.
passage: It might be supposed that an historical account commits an anachronism if and only if it attributes to persons of a past age a concept or practice that did not exist in their time, the attribution is unintentional on the part of the historian, and the attribution distorts the historian's overall interpretation of the period. This definition captures certain clear cases of anachronism: the historian who unwittingly speaks of mediaeval 'nations' as though they were modern nation-states, thereby misrepresenting the political structures of the period. The attribution is unintended, the concept does not belong to the age, and the interpretation suffers as a consequence. Yet consider a historian who describes the mediaeval Church as exercising a 'monopoly' on education. The concept of monopoly, understood in its economic and juridical sense, is a product of early modern and modern thought; it does not accurately characterise the Church's role in mediaeval learning. But suppose the historian is fully aware of this. She employs the term deliberately, as a convenient metaphor, knowing that it is strictly anachronistic but finding it useful for communicating with a modern audience. The second condition — that the attribution be unintentional — fails. Yet the anachronism persists: the concept of monopoly still does not belong to the mediaeval period, and its use still imports modern assumptions into the description of an earlier age. The definition has located anachronism in the historian's ignorance rather than in the logical mismatch between concept and period, when in truth a knowing anachronism is no less anachronistic for being deliberately chosen.

definition: An historical figure leaves a legacy if and only if (i) subsequent generations consciously invoke his name and ideas, (ii) institutions founded or shaped by him continue to operate, and (iii) a direct line of influence can be traced from his actions to present-day practices.
conditions: subsequent generations consciously invoke his name and ideas
institutions founded or shaped by him continue to operate
direct line of influence traceable to present-day practices
counterexample: The anonymous engineers who designed the Roman road network left an enduring legacy: European transport routes, town locations, and even modern motorways follow paths originally laid down by Roman roads. Yet no one invokes the names of these engineers, for their names are lost. No institution they founded continues to operate. The first and second conditions fail, yet the legacy — the physical and geographical shaping of European civilisation — is immense.
missing_condition: The definition requires conscious invocation and institutional continuity, but a legacy may operate impersonally, shaping the physical, cultural, or intellectual landscape in ways that persist long after the originator's name and institutions have been forgotten.
passage: One might propose that an historical figure leaves a legacy if and only if subsequent generations consciously invoke his name and ideas, institutions founded or shaped by him continue to operate, and a direct line of influence can be traced from his actions to present-day practices. This definition captures certain celebrated legacies. The legacy of Justinian is partly constituted by the conscious invocation of his legal codification by later jurists, the continued operation of legal institutions shaped by Roman law, and a traceable line of influence extending to modern civil-law systems. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet consider the anonymous engineers and surveyors who designed the Roman road network. Their work shaped the geography of European civilisation in ways that persist to this day. The routes of many modern European roads and railways follow the paths originally laid down by Roman engineers. Towns grew up at Roman road junctions and remain there still. The very pattern of settlement and communication in much of Western Europe bears the imprint of decisions made two thousand years ago by men whose names are entirely lost to us. No subsequent generation invokes these engineers by name, for no one knows who they were. No institution they founded continues to operate. The first and second conditions fail. Yet their legacy is enormous — arguably greater, in its cumulative practical effect, than that of many a famous emperor. The definition has equated legacy with conscious commemoration and institutional survival, overlooking the impersonal legacies that shape the physical and social landscape without anyone being aware of their origin.

definition: An event constitutes an historical revolution if and only if (i) it involves the violent overthrow of an existing political regime, (ii) it occurs within a compressed period of time, and (iii) it is led by an organised movement with a coherent programme of reform.
conditions: involves violent overthrow of existing political regime
occurs within compressed period of time
led by organised movement with coherent programme of reform
counterexample: The Industrial Revolution, which transformed the economic and social structure of Britain and eventually the world, involved no violent overthrow of a political regime. It unfolded over decades rather than within a compressed period. And it was not led by any organised movement with a programme of reform; it emerged from the uncoordinated actions of thousands of inventors, entrepreneurs, and labourers. All three conditions fail, yet it is universally called a revolution and is among the most consequential transformations in human history.
missing_condition: The definition restricts revolution to violent political upheaval accomplished rapidly by an organised vanguard, but some of the most profound revolutions in human affairs — the Industrial Revolution, the Agricultural Revolution, the Scientific Revolution — are gradual, uncoordinated transformations that reshape society without political violence or deliberate leadership.
passage: A familiar account holds that an event constitutes an historical revolution if and only if it involves the violent overthrow of an existing political regime, it occurs within a compressed period of time, and it is led by an organised movement with a coherent programme of reform. This definition fits certain paradigmatic revolutions tolerably well. The French Revolution of 1789 involved the violent destruction of the Bourbon monarchy, unfolded with extraordinary rapidity over the summer months, and was driven — at least in its early phases — by groups with articulate programmes of political reform. Yet the definition proves wholly inadequate when one turns to the Industrial Revolution. This transformation, which altered the material conditions of human life more profoundly than any political upheaval, involved no violent overthrow of a regime. The British government was not toppled; Parliament continued to sit; the Crown remained on its throne. The transformation did not occur within a compressed period; it unfolded over the better part of a century, from the mid-eighteenth century to the mid-nineteenth, and its consequences are still working themselves out today. And it was not led by any organised movement with a coherent programme. No committee of industrialists planned the mechanisation of textile production. No manifesto called for the construction of railways. The revolution emerged from the dispersed and uncoordinated decisions of thousands of individuals — inventors, mill-owners, canal-builders, migrant labourers — none of whom conceived of himself as participating in a revolution. All three conditions fail. The definition has taken the political revolution as the model for all revolutions, ignoring the transformations in economy, technology, and social organisation that deserve the name of revolution precisely because of the depth and permanence of the changes they wrought.

definition: A society has made moral progress if and only if (i) its laws have become more humane over time, (ii) the scope of persons recognised as possessing rights has expanded, and (iii) acts of cruelty that were once tolerated are now prohibited and punished.
conditions: laws have become more humane over time
scope of rights-bearing persons has expanded
once-tolerated cruelties are now prohibited and punished
counterexample: Consider the period between the two great European wars of the twentieth century. Many European states had, by 1914, expanded the franchise, abolished the most brutal penal practices, and extended legal protections to previously excluded groups. Yet within a generation, states with ostensibly humane legal codes perpetrated atrocities on an industrial scale. The laws on the books had become more humane, the formal scope of rights had expanded, and many traditional cruelties had been prohibited — yet moral catastrophe followed nonetheless. All three conditions were formally satisfied on the eve of the catastrophe, yet moral progress had manifestly not been secured.
missing_condition: The definition identifies progress with changes in formal law and stated norms, but moral progress requires not merely the enactment of humane laws but the deep internalisation of humane dispositions, such that legal prohibitions are sustained by genuine moral commitment rather than by mere statutory provision.
passage: One might propose that a society has made moral progress if and only if its laws have become more humane over time, the scope of persons recognised as possessing rights has expanded, and acts of cruelty that were once tolerated are now prohibited and punished. This definition appears to capture the trajectory of many Western societies over the past three centuries: the abolition of judicial torture, the extension of the franchise, the prohibition of slavery. In each case, the law became more humane, the circle of rights-bearers widened, and formerly accepted cruelties were proscribed. Yet one should hesitate to accept this definition without examining its limits. Consider the condition of European civilisation on the eve of 1914. The great European states had, over the preceding century, abolished many of the most barbarous penal practices, extended political rights to broader classes of citizens, and established legal protections for groups previously excluded from the moral community. The three conditions of the definition were, in their formal aspects, substantially satisfied. Yet within a generation, the continent witnessed atrocities of a scale and systematic character that dwarfed anything in earlier European history. States whose statute books reflected a century of humanitarian reform proved capable of organised brutality beyond the imagination of earlier ages. The definition's three conditions were met in the formal sense — the laws were more humane, the rights more broadly distributed, the old cruelties prohibited — and yet the society had not, in any deep sense, made secure moral progress. The definition has mistaken formal legal change for genuine moral transformation, when a society's laws may outrun its actual moral dispositions, so that humane statutes coexist with a capacity for inhumanity that the statutes are powerless to prevent.

definition: An historical explanation is adequate if and only if (i) it cites only factors of which the historical agents were themselves aware, (ii) it explains the event in terms that the agents themselves would have recognised, and (iii) it does not invoke causes or structures that were invisible to the participants.
conditions: cites only factors of which agents were themselves aware
explains events in terms agents would have recognised
does not invoke causes or structures invisible to participants
counterexample: The historian who explains the price revolution of sixteenth-century Europe by reference to the influx of American silver cites a factor — the relationship between money supply and prices — of which the historical agents were largely unaware. Sixteenth-century merchants and statesmen did not understand the quantity theory of money and would not have recognised this explanation. All three conditions fail, yet the explanation is regarded as one of the most important contributions to early modern economic history.
missing_condition: The definition confines explanation to the agents' own understanding, but adequate historical explanation often requires invoking structural, economic, or demographic factors that operated behind the backs of the participants and of which they had no conception.
passage: It is sometimes argued that an historical explanation is adequate if and only if it cites only factors of which the historical agents were themselves aware, it explains the event in terms the agents would have recognised, and it does not invoke causes or structures invisible to the participants. This definition reflects a respectable concern: that the historian should not impose alien categories upon the past but should seek to understand historical actors in their own terms. It works well for certain kinds of explanation — an account of a king's decision to go to war that cites his stated reasons, the advice of his counsellors, and the diplomatic provocations of which he was aware. Yet the definition proves too restrictive when one considers some of the most illuminating explanations in modern historiography. The price revolution of the sixteenth century — the sustained and dramatic rise in prices across Europe — is widely explained by reference to the influx of gold and silver from the Americas, which increased the money supply and thereby drove up prices. This explanation invokes the relationship between the quantity of money in circulation and the general level of prices, a relationship that was scarcely understood by contemporaries. Sixteenth-century merchants knew that prices were rising, but they attributed the phenomenon to the greed of monopolists, the debasement of coinage, or divine displeasure. They would not have recognised an explanation framed in terms of money supply and aggregate demand. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the monetary explanation of the price revolution is among the most widely accepted in early modern economic history. The definition has confined the historian to the horizon of the agents' own awareness, when the most penetrating historical explanations frequently invoke structural causes that operated, as it were, behind the backs of the participants.

definition: A periodisation of the past is valid if and only if (i) it applies consistently across all geographical regions, (ii) the same period boundaries hold for political, cultural, and economic history alike, and (iii) the periods are of roughly equal duration.
conditions: applies consistently across all geographical regions
same boundaries hold for political, cultural, and economic history
periods are of roughly equal duration
counterexample: The conventional periodisation of Western history into ancient, mediaeval, and modern applies primarily to Europe and the Mediterranean. It does not map onto the histories of China, India, or sub-Saharan Africa, whose political and cultural rhythms follow entirely different patterns. The ancient-mediaeval-modern scheme also assigns vastly unequal durations to its periods. All three conditions fail, yet this periodisation remains the most widely employed framework in Western historical scholarship.
missing_condition: The definition demands universal geographical applicability, cross-domain consistency, and temporal symmetry, but periodisations are characteristically tied to particular civilisations and particular dimensions of historical experience, and there is no reason to expect that useful divisions of the past should be of equal length.
passage: One might insist that a periodisation of the past is valid if and only if it applies consistently across all geographical regions, the same period boundaries hold for political, cultural, and economic history alike, and the periods are of roughly equal duration. This is a demanding standard, and it appears to reflect a desire for the kind of universal and symmetrical classification one finds in the natural sciences. Yet even the most established periodisation in Western historical scholarship fails to meet it. The division of the Western past into ancient, mediaeval, and modern is the fundamental framework upon which an enormous body of historical writing rests. University departments are organised around it; scholarly journals are named after it; careers are defined by it. Yet this periodisation applies primarily to Europe and the Mediterranean world. It does not map onto the history of China, where the succession of dynasties follows a rhythm unrelated to the rise and fall of Rome. It does not apply to India, whose periodisation must reckon with the Gupta, Mughal, and colonial eras. It does not fit sub-Saharan Africa, whose past resists the ancient-mediaeval-modern framework entirely. The first condition fails. Nor do the same boundaries serve equally for political, cultural, and economic history even within Europe; the Renaissance in cultural history does not coincide neatly with any political watershed. And the three periods are of grossly unequal duration. All three conditions fail. The definition has sought a universal and symmetrical scheme, when useful periodisations are characteristically local, partial, and asymmetric.

definition: A civilisation is a unified entity if and only if (i) all its members share a single dominant religion, (ii) political authority is centralised under a single sovereign or governing body, and (iii) a common language serves as the medium of public life throughout its territory.
conditions: all members share single dominant religion
political authority centralised under single sovereign
common language serves as medium of public life
counterexample: Ancient Greek civilisation comprised dozens of independent city-states — Athens, Sparta, Corinth, Thebes — with no centralised political authority. While the Greeks shared certain religious practices, they worshipped local deities alongside the Olympian pantheon, and there was considerable diversity in cult and ritual. And though they shared the Greek language, they spoke distinct dialects that were sometimes mutually difficult. The second condition fails entirely, yet Greek civilisation is one of the most clearly defined in the historical record.
missing_condition: The definition requires political centralisation, religious uniformity, and linguistic homogeneity, but a civilisation may be constituted by shared cultural forms — literature, philosophy, artistic conventions, athletic and religious festivals — that unite politically fragmented and linguistically diverse peoples.
passage: It might be supposed that a civilisation is a unified entity if and only if all its members share a single dominant religion, political authority is centralised under a single sovereign or governing body, and a common language serves as the medium of public life throughout its territory. This definition seems to fit the case of imperial China tolerably well, where Confucian orthodoxy pervaded public life, the emperor held centralised authority, and the Chinese written language served as the unifying medium of administration and scholarship. Yet one should observe that the definition fails when applied to ancient Greek civilisation. The Greeks constituted one of the most brilliant and clearly defined civilisations in the historical record. Their literature, philosophy, architecture, and political thought shaped the course of Western culture. Yet Greek civilisation was politically fragmented to an extraordinary degree. Dozens of independent city-states — Athens, Sparta, Corinth, Thebes, and scores of lesser polities — governed themselves according to their own constitutions, waged war upon one another, and jealously guarded their sovereignty. There was no central authority, no Greek emperor, no pan-Hellenic governing body with effective power. The second condition fails entirely. Religious practice, while sharing certain common elements in the worship of the Olympian gods, varied considerably from city to city, with local cults, oracles, and festivals differing in emphasis and character. What united the Greeks as a civilisation was not political centralisation or religious uniformity but a shared body of cultural forms — the Homeric poems, the Olympic games, the dramatic festivals, the philosophical schools — that transcended political boundaries.

definition: A civilisation enters a period of decline if and only if (i) its artistic and literary production diminishes in quality and quantity, (ii) its members lose confidence in their own cultural traditions, and (iii) it increasingly borrows ideas and forms from foreign sources rather than generating its own.
conditions: artistic and literary production diminishes in quality and quantity
members lose confidence in cultural traditions
civilisation borrows from foreign sources rather than generating its own
counterexample: Rome in the second century under Hadrian and the Antonines is sometimes regarded as the beginning of a long cultural decline, yet this very period saw an extraordinary flourishing of borrowing from Greek culture — in philosophy, sculpture, architecture, and literature. The Romans had borrowed extensively from Greece since the Republic, and this borrowing coincided with the zenith, not the nadir, of Roman civilisation. The third condition obtains, yet the civilisation was at its most confident and productive.
missing_condition: The definition treats cultural borrowing as a symptom of decline, but civilisations at the height of their powers frequently borrow from others — indeed, creative absorption of foreign influences is often a sign of vitality rather than weakness.
passage: One might hold that a civilisation enters a period of decline if and only if its artistic and literary production diminishes in quality and quantity, its members lose confidence in their own cultural traditions, and it increasingly borrows ideas and forms from foreign sources rather than generating its own. This definition reflects a common intuition: a civilisation in decline turns outward because its inner resources have been exhausted. It seems to capture the case of late antiquity, when the creative energies of the Graeco-Roman world waned and external influences — from the East, from the Germanic peoples — became increasingly prominent. Yet the definition encounters a serious difficulty when one considers Rome at the height of its civilisation. The Romans of the Republic and early Empire borrowed from Greek culture on an enormous scale. Roman philosophy was almost entirely Greek in inspiration — Stoicism, Epicureanism, Platonism. Roman sculpture imitated Greek models. Roman architecture adapted Greek forms. Roman literature was saturated with Greek allusion and Greek technique. Horace, perhaps the finest Latin lyric poet, openly declared that captive Greece had captured its rude conqueror. The third condition — borrowing from foreign sources — obtains throughout the period of Rome's greatest cultural achievement. Yet this was not decline; it was the summit of Roman civilisation. The definition has mistaken cultural borrowing for a symptom of weakness, when in truth the capacity to absorb, adapt, and transform foreign influences is frequently a mark of a civilisation's strength and self-confidence rather than of its exhaustion.

definition: An historical narrative is objective if and only if (i) the narrator does not appear as a character within the account, (ii) the account confines itself to publicly verifiable facts, and (iii) the narrator refrains from passing moral judgements on the persons and events described.
conditions: narrator does not appear as character in account
account confines itself to publicly verifiable facts
narrator refrains from moral judgements
counterexample: Thucydides' History is widely regarded as the paradigm of objective historical narrative, yet Thucydides served as a general in the war he describes and recounts his own failure at Amphipolis in the third person. He appears, albeit obliquely, as a character in his own narrative. The first condition fails, yet the work is the very model of dispassionate historical writing.
missing_condition: The definition equates objectivity with the narrator's absence from the story, but a narrator may participate in the events he describes and yet maintain a rigorous objectivity of analysis and tone, so that personal involvement need not compromise impartiality.
passage: It is sometimes proposed that an historical narrative is objective if and only if the narrator does not appear as a character within the account, the account confines itself to publicly verifiable facts, and the narrator refrains from passing moral judgements upon the persons and events described. This definition has a certain appeal: it seems to distinguish the impartial historian from the memoirist, the propagandist, and the moralist. Yet it proves unsatisfactory when one examines the most celebrated example of objective historical writing in the Western tradition. Thucydides' History of the Peloponnesian War is almost universally regarded as the foundational work of rigorous, dispassionate historical narrative. Its analytical precision, its refusal of mythological explanation, and its commitment to accuracy have made it the model for subsequent historians. Yet Thucydides was himself a participant in the events he describes. He served as an Athenian general and recounts his own failure to relieve Amphipolis — writing of himself in the third person, it is true, but appearing nonetheless as a character in the narrative. The first condition fails. Thucydides is present in his own account, yet his narrative is the very exemplar of historical objectivity. The definition has confused objectivity with the narrator's physical absence from the events, when what makes a narrative objective is not the narrator's non-participation but his capacity to analyse events with impartiality and rigour, even — perhaps especially — when he has been personally involved in them.

definition: The legacy of an historical event is constituted if and only if (i) the event is commemorated by subsequent generations through monuments, ceremonies, or official observances, (ii) it is included in the educational curriculum of the society, and (iii) it is invoked in public discourse as a reference point for collective identity.
conditions: event commemorated through monuments, ceremonies, or observances
included in educational curriculum
invoked in public discourse as reference point for identity
counterexample: The Black Death of 1347-1351 killed perhaps a third of Europe's population and transformed the continent's demographic, economic, and social structure for centuries. Labour shortages led to the decline of serfdom, the redistribution of land, and shifts in the balance of power between lord and peasant. Yet the Black Death is not commemorated by monuments or ceremonies, was for centuries absent from educational curricula, and is rarely invoked in public discourse as a reference point for collective identity. All three conditions fail, yet its legacy — the reshaping of European society — is among the most consequential in history.
missing_condition: The definition locates legacy in conscious commemoration and public memory, but the deepest legacies may be structural and impersonal — demographic shifts, economic transformations, institutional changes — that reshape a society without being remembered or commemorated by those who inherit their effects.
passage: A plausible account holds that the legacy of an historical event is constituted if and only if the event is commemorated by subsequent generations through monuments, ceremonies, or official observances, it is included in the educational curriculum of the society, and it is invoked in public discourse as a reference point for collective identity. This definition captures certain familiar legacies: the legacy of the Battle of Hastings in English national consciousness, commemorated and taught and invoked as a founding moment of the English state. Yet consider the Black Death, which swept through Europe in 1347 and the years following, killing perhaps a third of the continent's population. The demographic consequences were immense and lasting. The catastrophic reduction in the labour supply altered the balance of power between landowners and labourers, contributing to the decline of serfdom in Western Europe. Agricultural land was abandoned, patterns of settlement shifted, and the economic structure of the continent was transformed for generations. The legacy of the Black Death, measured by its actual effects upon subsequent European history, is among the most far-reaching of any single event. Yet the Black Death is not commemorated by monuments or annual observances in the manner of great battles or political revolutions. For centuries it occupied a marginal place in educational curricula. And it is not characteristically invoked in public discourse as a reference point for collective identity. All three conditions fail. The definition has equated legacy with conscious public memory, overlooking the structural legacies — demographic, economic, institutional — that reshape a society profoundly and permanently without being remembered or commemorated by those who live within their consequences.

definition: An historical revolution succeeds if and only if (i) the old regime is permanently replaced by a new political order, (ii) the aims proclaimed by the revolutionaries are substantially achieved, and (iii) the society that emerges is more stable than the one it replaced.
conditions: old regime permanently replaced by new political order
aims proclaimed by revolutionaries substantially achieved
resulting society more stable than predecessor
counterexample: The French Revolution of 1789 is universally regarded as one of the most consequential revolutions in history. Yet the old regime was not permanently replaced by a stable new order; France oscillated between republic, directory, consulate, empire, restoration, and further revolution for decades. Many of the revolutionaries' proclaimed aims — liberty, equality, popular sovereignty — were achieved only partially and intermittently. And the resulting society was manifestly less stable than the ancien régime, which had endured for centuries. All three conditions fail or are only partially met, yet no historian would deny that the French Revolution succeeded in transforming France and Europe.
missing_condition: The definition equates success with permanent replacement, full achievement of aims, and enhanced stability, but a revolution may succeed in the deeper sense of transforming the terms of political thought and the structure of social relations even when its immediate political outcomes are unstable, partial, and contested.
passage: One might define revolutionary success as follows: an historical revolution succeeds if and only if the old regime is permanently replaced by a new political order, the aims proclaimed by the revolutionaries are substantially achieved, and the society that emerges is more stable than the one it replaced. This definition seems to capture the American Revolution tolerably well: the British colonial regime was permanently replaced, the aims of independence and republican self-governance were achieved, and the resulting constitutional order proved remarkably durable. Yet consider the French Revolution of 1789, universally acknowledged as one of the most important revolutions in history. The old regime was overthrown, but what followed was not a stable new order. France passed through the phases of constitutional monarchy, radical republic, the Terror, the Directory, the Consulate, and the Napoleonic Empire, before undergoing restoration, further revolution in 1830 and 1848, a second empire, and finally a third republic. The first condition — permanent replacement by a new order — is only ambiguously satisfied, if at all. The proclaimed aims of liberty, equality, and fraternity were achieved only imperfectly and intermittently. And the society that emerged was less stable, not more, than the ancien régime that had endured for generations. All three conditions fail or are met only in an attenuated sense. Yet the French Revolution transformed the political vocabulary of the modern world, destroyed the foundations of aristocratic privilege, and established principles that reshaped the politics of Europe. The definition has equated revolutionary success with tidy outcomes, when the most consequential revolutions may succeed precisely by unsettling the old order so thoroughly that nothing can ever be the same again.

definition: An interpretation of the past is free from anachronism if and only if (i) it employs only concepts available to the historical agents in question, (ii) it avoids analogies drawn from later periods, and (iii) it presents the motivations of historical actors in terms they themselves would have used.
conditions: employs only concepts available to historical agents
avoids analogies from later periods
presents motivations in terms actors would have used
counterexample: The economic historian who analyses the Roman latifundia as a system of large-scale agricultural production employing unfree labour uses concepts — 'system,' 'production,' 'labour' in their modern economic senses — that were not available to Roman landowners, and draws implicit analogies with later plantation economies. Yet such analysis is considered indispensable for understanding the Roman agrarian economy. All three conditions are violated, yet the result is illuminating rather than distorting.
missing_condition: The definition demands that historians confine themselves entirely to the conceptual vocabulary of the period studied, but historical understanding often requires concepts that transcend the agents' own categories, and the judicious use of anachronistic concepts may reveal structures that contemporaries could not have articulated.
passage: It is sometimes maintained that an interpretation of the past is free from anachronism if and only if it employs only concepts available to the historical agents in question, it avoids analogies drawn from later periods, and it presents the motivations of historical actors in terms they themselves would have used. This is, in essence, the demand that the historian think himself entirely into the world of his subjects. It has a certain force: the historian who attributes modern nationalist sentiment to a mediaeval baron has plainly gone astray. Yet the demand proves self-defeating when pressed to its logical conclusion. Consider the economic historian who analyses the Roman latifundia — the great landed estates of the late Republic and Empire. She describes them as a system of large-scale agricultural production employing unfree labour, analyses their productivity, and examines their role in the wider Roman economy. The concepts she employs — 'system,' 'production,' 'labour' in their modern economic senses, 'productivity' as a measurable quantity — were not available to the Roman landowners themselves, who would have thought in terms of honour, duty, household management, and the maintenance of their station. She draws implicit analogies with later plantation economies. She presents the structure of Roman agriculture in terms no Roman would have used. All three conditions are violated. Yet the analysis is regarded as indispensable by historians of the Roman economy. The definition has confused the avoidance of anachronism with the abandonment of all conceptual tools not available to the historical agents, when the historian's task often requires precisely the application of concepts that transcend the agents' own vocabulary in order to reveal the structures within which those agents lived and acted.

definition: A tradition is authentic if and only if (i) its current form faithfully reproduces the form in which it was originally practised, (ii) its practitioners can provide a historically accurate account of its origins, and (iii) it has not been substantially modified to suit the preferences of later generations.
conditions: current form faithfully reproduces original form
practitioners can provide historically accurate account of origins
has not been substantially modified by later generations
counterexample: The tradition of Highland clan tartans in Scotland is widely regarded as an authentic Scottish tradition, yet historical research has shown that the association of specific tartans with specific clans is largely a nineteenth-century invention, promoted by textile manufacturers and romanticised by Sir Walter Scott's staging of George IV's visit to Edinburgh in 1822. The current form does not reproduce an ancient original; practitioners cannot provide an accurate account of the tradition's origins, since most believe it to be far older than it is; and the tradition has been substantially created, rather than merely modified, by later generations. All three conditions fail, yet the tartans are experienced as an authentic tradition by those who wear them.
missing_condition: The definition requires historical fidelity to an original practice, but traditions may be experienced as authentic by virtue of their felt connection to collective identity and their integration into communal life, even when historical investigation reveals them to be recent inventions.
passage: One might suppose that a tradition is authentic if and only if its current form faithfully reproduces the form in which it was originally practised, its practitioners can provide a historically accurate account of its origins, and it has not been substantially modified to suit the preferences of later generations. This definition embodies a demanding standard of authenticity, and it captures certain traditions well — the Orthodox Jewish observance of Shabbat, for instance, which has been maintained with great fidelity to its ancient origins. Yet the definition encounters a striking difficulty when applied to one of Scotland's most celebrated traditions: the wearing of clan tartans. The association of particular tartan patterns with particular Highland clans is almost universally believed to be an ancient custom, reaching back into the mists of Scottish history. Yet historical research has demonstrated that the tradition in its present form is largely a creation of the early nineteenth century. Before that period, tartans were associated with regions rather than with clans, and many of the so-called ancient clan tartans were designed by textile manufacturers in the decades following Sir Walter Scott's romanticised staging of George IV's visit to Edinburgh in 1822. The current form does not faithfully reproduce an ancient original, because no such original existed. Practitioners cannot provide a historically accurate account of the tradition's origins, since most sincerely believe the tradition to be far older than it is. And the tradition has been not merely modified but substantially invented by later generations. All three conditions fail. Yet the clan tartans are experienced as profoundly authentic by those who wear them — they are woven into the fabric of Scottish identity, displayed at weddings and funerals, and cherished as links to an ancestral past. The definition has equated authenticity with historical accuracy, when a tradition may be felt as authentic by virtue of its role in communal life, regardless of its actual provenance.

definition: A civilisation C has made progress if and only if (i) the material conditions of life for the majority of C's members have improved over time, (ii) the improvement is continuous — that is, each successive period is materially better than the last, and (iii) the improvement is the result of deliberate, rational planning by the leaders of C.
conditions: the material conditions of life for the majority have improved over time
the improvement is continuous — each period is better than the last
the improvement is the result of deliberate, rational planning by leaders
counterexample: Consider the progress of European civilisation from the fall of Rome to the Renaissance. The material conditions of life for the majority did, over this vast span, eventually improve. But the improvement was not continuous; the centuries following Rome's collapse saw a marked deterioration in living standards, infrastructure, and public order before any recovery began. Nor was the eventual improvement the result of deliberate, rational planning by leaders; it arose from innumerable uncoordinated developments — advances in agricultural technique, the growth of trade, the slow accumulation of practical knowledge — that no ruler or council had planned. Yet European civilisation made progress over this period in a perfectly intelligible sense.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuity and deliberate planning, but historical progress is often discontinuous, interrupted by periods of decline, and driven by unplanned, decentralised developments rather than by the rational direction of leaders.
passage: One might propose that a civilisation has made progress if and only if the material conditions of life for the majority of its members have improved over time, the improvement is continuous, and the improvement is the result of deliberate rational planning by the civilisation's leaders. The definition has some initial plausibility. One thinks of a programme of public works — the construction of aqueducts, roads, and granaries — planned by an enlightened ruler and resulting in a steady, measurable improvement in the welfare of the populace. Yet one must consider the progress of European civilisation from the collapse of the Western Roman Empire to the Renaissance. Over this vast span of approximately a millennium, the material conditions of life in Europe did eventually improve. By the fifteenth century, Europeans enjoyed better agricultural yields, more extensive trade networks, larger and more prosperous cities, and a more complex division of labour than had existed in the sixth century. But the improvement was not continuous. The centuries following Rome's fall witnessed a severe deterioration in material conditions: roads fell into disrepair, aqueducts crumbled, long-distance trade contracted, and the population of cities declined sharply. Progress, when it came, came fitfully, interrupted by plagues, invasions, and famines. Nor was the improvement the result of deliberate rational planning by leaders. No king or council planned the three-field system of crop rotation, the spread of the heavy plough, or the revival of Mediterranean commerce. These developments arose from the uncoordinated efforts of countless peasants, merchants, and artisans, none of whom was executing a plan for civilisational progress. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has imposed upon the concept of progress a model of continuous, centrally planned improvement that the actual course of history does not sustain.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies a single, decisive cause of E, (ii) A shows that E was inevitable given that cause, and (iii) A draws upon documentary evidence produced contemporaneously with E.
conditions: A identifies a single, decisive cause of E
A shows that E was inevitable given that cause
A draws upon documentary evidence produced contemporaneously with E
counterexample: Consider the explanation of the decline of the Roman Republic. No single, decisive cause can be identified; the decline resulted from a confluence of factors — the strain of imperial expansion, the impoverishment of the peasantry, the professionalisation of the army, the rivalries of ambitious generals, and the erosion of republican norms. Nor was the decline inevitable; at various points, different outcomes were possible. Yet the historical explanation of the Republic's decline is among the most thoroughly studied and best understood episodes in ancient history. The first and second conditions fail.
missing_condition: The definition requires a single decisive cause and inevitability, but satisfactory historical explanations typically identify a plurality of contributing causes and acknowledge the contingency of outcomes. What is wanted is an account that allows for multicausal explanation and the recognition that historical events need not have been inevitable.
passage: Suppose one holds that an account constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event if and only if it identifies a single decisive cause, it shows that the event was inevitable given that cause, and it draws upon contemporary documentary evidence. The definition reflects a certain ideal of explanatory rigour: one identifies the cause, demonstrates that the effect could not have been otherwise, and grounds the account in primary sources. Yet one must consider the explanation of the decline of the Roman Republic. Historians have laboured over this question for centuries, and the best accounts identify not a single cause but a plurality of contributing factors: the strain imposed upon republican institutions by the administration of a vast empire, the displacement of the small peasant farmer by large slave-worked estates, the creation of professional armies loyal to their commanders rather than to the state, the escalating rivalries among powerful generals, and the gradual erosion of the constitutional norms that had restrained political competition. No one of these factors was decisive by itself; the decline resulted from their cumulative and interacting effects. Nor was the decline inevitable. At several junctures — after the reforms of the Gracchi, after the dictatorship of Sulla, perhaps even after the assassination of Caesar — different outcomes were possible. The Republic might have been reformed rather than destroyed. The first two conditions of the definition fail. The definition has imposed upon historical explanation a model of monocausal inevitability that is inappropriate to the complexity and contingency of historical events.

definition: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is marked by a single, dramatic event that serves as its boundary, (ii) the character of life within each period is uniform and internally consistent, and (iii) the transition from one period to the next is abrupt rather than gradual.
conditions: each period is marked by a single, dramatic event at its boundary
the character of life within each period is uniform and internally consistent
the transition from one period to the next is abrupt
counterexample: Consider the transition from the mediaeval to the modern period in European history. No single dramatic event marks the boundary; historians have variously proposed the fall of Constantinople, the invention of printing, the discovery of America, and the Reformation, and none of these candidates is universally accepted. The character of life was not uniform within either the mediaeval or the modern period; both encompassed enormous internal variation. And the transition was not abrupt but extended over decades or even centuries, with mediaeval and modern elements coexisting and interpenetrating. Yet the distinction between the mediaeval and the modern is one of the most fundamental periodisations in Western historiography.
missing_condition: The definition requires sharp boundaries, internal uniformity, and abrupt transitions, but legitimate periodisations may involve gradual transitions, contested boundaries, and internally diverse periods. What is wanted is an account that treats periodisation as a heuristic device for organising historical understanding rather than as a description of sharp ontological divisions.
passage: One might propose that a division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if each period is marked by a single dramatic event serving as its boundary, the character of life within each period is uniform and internally consistent, and the transition from one period to the next is abrupt. The definition has a certain neatness: historical periods, on this account, are like geological strata, sharply demarcated and internally homogeneous. Yet one must consider the transition from the mediaeval to the modern period in European history. This is one of the most fundamental periodisations in Western historiography, and generations of historians have relied upon it to organise their understanding of the past. But no single dramatic event marks the boundary between the two periods. The fall of Constantinople in 1453, the invention of movable type, the discovery of America in 1492, the Reformation initiated in 1517 — each has been proposed as the boundary, and none commands universal assent. The character of life was not uniform within either period. The mediaeval period encompassed the Carolingian renaissance, the Crusades, the Black Death, and the flourishing of scholastic philosophy — developments so diverse that it is difficult to speak of a single mediaeval character. And the transition was not abrupt; mediaeval institutions, habits of thought, and social structures persisted well into what is conventionally called the modern period, whilst modern elements — the revival of classical learning, the growth of commerce, the rise of the nation-state — had their roots deep in the mediaeval centuries. The three conditions fail. The definition has confused a heuristic device for organising historical understanding with a description of sharp ontological divisions in the fabric of the past.

definition: A civilisation C is in decline if and only if (i) the military power of C has diminished relative to its rivals, (ii) the territory controlled by C has contracted, and (iii) the political institutions of C have become corrupt and ineffective.
conditions: the military power of C has diminished relative to its rivals
the territory controlled by C has contracted
the political institutions of C have become corrupt and ineffective
counterexample: Consider the Dutch Republic in the eighteenth century. Its military power had diminished relative to Britain and France, and its territorial possessions had contracted. Yet Dutch culture, science, and commerce continued to flourish; the standard of living remained among the highest in Europe; and the political institutions, though weakened, continued to function with reasonable effectiveness. Was the Dutch Republic in decline? In military and territorial terms, perhaps. But in the domains of culture, commerce, and civic life, it was not. The definition captures one dimension of decline whilst ignoring others.
missing_condition: The definition reduces decline to military and territorial contraction and institutional corruption, but a civilisation may decline in some respects whilst flourishing in others. What is wanted is an account that recognises the multidimensional character of civilisational vitality and decline.
passage: One might hold that a civilisation is in decline if and only if its military power has diminished relative to its rivals, its territory has contracted, and its political institutions have become corrupt and ineffective. The definition captures a familiar pattern: one thinks of the late Roman Empire, whose legions could no longer defend its borders, whose provinces fell away one by one, and whose imperial administration became venal and incompetent. Yet one must consider the case of the Dutch Republic in the eighteenth century. By the mid-eighteenth century, the Republic's military power had manifestly diminished. It could no longer compete with Britain at sea or with France on land. Its territorial possessions had contracted, and its influence in European affairs had waned. By the lights of the definition, the Dutch Republic was in decline. Yet in many of the domains that constitute the life of a civilisation, the Republic continued to flourish. Dutch culture remained vibrant; Dutch universities attracted scholars from across Europe; Dutch publishers produced books that were read throughout the continent. The standard of living in the Republic remained among the highest in Europe, and Dutch commerce, though no longer dominant, continued to generate considerable wealth. The political institutions, though less effective than in the Republic's golden age, were not corrupt in the manner of a decadent autocracy. The definition captures one dimension of civilisational health — the military and territorial — and treats it as exhaustive, when in truth a civilisation may lose military pre-eminence and territorial extent whilst retaining extraordinary vitality in the domains of culture, commerce, and civic life.

definition: An account A constitutes a historical narrative if and only if (i) A presents events in strict chronological order, (ii) A confines itself to events that are attested by primary documentary sources, and (iii) A refrains from any evaluative judgement concerning the significance of the events narrated.
conditions: A presents events in strict chronological order
A confines itself to events attested by primary documentary sources
A refrains from evaluative judgement concerning significance
counterexample: Consider Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War. Thucydides does not always present events in strict chronological order; he frequently interrupts his narrative to provide thematic analyses, speeches, and retrospective assessments. He does not confine himself to documentary sources; he draws upon oral testimony, personal observation, and reconstructed speeches. And he makes frequent evaluative judgements concerning the significance of events. Yet the History of the Peloponnesian War is one of the greatest historical narratives ever composed.
missing_condition: The definition imposes constraints of strict chronology, documentary confinement, and evaluative neutrality that the finest historical narratives do not observe. What is wanted is a recognition that historical narrative may employ thematic organisation, diverse sources, and interpretive judgement without ceasing to be narrative.
passage: Suppose one holds that an account constitutes a historical narrative if and only if it presents events in strict chronological order, confines itself to events attested by primary documentary sources, and refrains from evaluative judgement concerning the significance of the events narrated. The definition reflects a certain positivist ideal of historical writing: the historian arranges the documented facts in temporal sequence and lets them speak for themselves. Yet one must consider Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War, which is rightly regarded as one of the founding works of historical narrative. Thucydides does not always present events in strict chronological order. He interrupts the temporal flow of his account to insert thematic analyses — his famous examination of the causes of the war, for instance, or his extended treatment of the plague at Athens. He includes reconstructed speeches that are not documentary records but literary compositions designed to illuminate the reasoning of the participants. He does not confine himself to primary documents; indeed, for much of his narrative, no documents existed, and he relies upon oral testimony, personal observation, and his own judgement as to what was likely to have been said and done. And he makes evaluative judgements freely: he pronounces upon the wisdom or folly of particular policies, assesses the character of leaders, and draws lessons from the events he narrates. All three conditions fail.
