The definition has taken the methods of a narrow school of documentary history and elevated them to necessary conditions of historical narrative as such, when in truth the greatest historical narratives have always combined chronological ordering with thematic analysis, have drawn upon diverse sources beyond the documentary, and have exercised interpretive judgement at every turn.

definition: An upheaval U constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) U involves the violent overthrow of an existing government, (ii) U is accomplished within a short period of time, and (iii) U replaces the old order with a radically different political and social system.
conditions: U involves the violent overthrow of an existing government
U is accomplished within a short period of time
U replaces the old order with a radically different political and social system
counterexample: Consider the Industrial Revolution. It transformed the economic, social, and political life of Britain and subsequently of the entire world more profoundly than any political upheaval. Yet it did not involve the violent overthrow of a government; no king was deposed, no parliament dissolved by force. It was not accomplished within a short period of time; it unfolded over the better part of a century. And whilst it replaced the old agrarian order with an industrial one, it did so gradually and without the abrupt substitution of one political system for another. All three conditions fail, yet the Industrial Revolution is universally recognised as a revolution of the first magnitude.
missing_condition: The definition restricts revolution to violent, rapid political upheaval, but revolutions may be gradual, non-violent, and primarily economic or social in character. What is wanted is an account that captures the transformative depth of a revolution without requiring violence or speed.
passage: Suppose one holds that an upheaval constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if it involves the violent overthrow of an existing government, is accomplished within a short period of time, and replaces the old order with a radically different political and social system. The definition captures the paradigmatic cases: the French Revolution of 1789, the Russian Revolution of 1917, each marked by the violent destruction of an old regime and its rapid replacement by a new order. Yet one must consider the Industrial Revolution. This transformation, which began in England in the latter half of the eighteenth century and continued well into the nineteenth, altered the economic foundations, the social structure, and the political character of British life more profoundly than any merely political upheaval. The population moved from the countryside to the factory towns; the handloom gave way to the power loom; the rhythms of agricultural life were replaced by the discipline of the factory clock. The effects were felt throughout the world. Yet the Industrial Revolution did not involve the violent overthrow of a government. No king was deposed, no parliament was dissolved by force, no army marched upon a capital. It was not accomplished within a short period of time; it unfolded over decades, and historians disagree about when it began and when it ended. And although it replaced the old agrarian order with an industrial one, the replacement was gradual rather than abrupt, and the political system — parliamentary monarchy — survived the transformation largely intact. All three conditions fail. The definition has taken the features of political revolution and treated them as necessary conditions of revolution as such, when in truth the most far-reaching revolution in modern history was non-violent, gradual, and primarily economic in character.

definition: A society S has progressed from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) the material conditions of life in S at t2 are superior to those at t1 — that is, the members of S are better fed, better housed, and longer-lived, (ii) the stock of scientific and technical knowledge available to S at t2 exceeds that available at t1, and (iii) the moral conduct of the members of S at t2 is superior to that at t1 — that is, there is less cruelty, less injustice, and greater benevolence.
conditions: the material conditions of life are superior at t2
the stock of scientific and technical knowledge is greater at t2
the moral conduct of the members is superior at t2
counterexample: Consider the Roman Empire between the first century and the fifth century of the Christian era. The material conditions of life in the first century — the aqueducts, the roads, the public buildings, the grain supply — were, in many respects, superior to those of the fifth century, when the infrastructure was crumbling and the population declining. The stock of technical knowledge in the fifth century was, if anything, diminished. Yet one might hold that the moral conduct of the later period was superior: the spread of Christianity brought with it a new concern for the poor, the sick, and the enslaved, and a condemnation of the gladiatorial games that had been a central feature of Roman public life. The first and second conditions fail whilst the third is arguably satisfied. Progress has occurred in one dimension but regression in others, and the definition cannot accommodate this.
missing_condition: The definition requires simultaneous progress in material, intellectual, and moral dimensions, but progress may be uneven, advancing in one domain whilst regressing in another. What is wanted is an account that allows for the independence of different dimensions of progress.
passage: One might propose that a society has progressed from one time to another if and only if the material conditions of life have improved, the stock of scientific and technical knowledge has increased, and the moral conduct of the members has become superior. The definition captures a familiar and optimistic picture of historical development. In the case of Western Europe from the sixteenth century to the nineteenth, it is plausible that all three conditions were satisfied: material conditions improved with the growth of agriculture and industry, scientific knowledge expanded enormously, and moral progress — the abolition of judicial torture, the mitigation of criminal punishments, the gradual recognition of individual rights — was real and substantial. Yet one must consider the Roman Empire between the first century and the fifth century of the Christian era. In the first century, the Empire was at the height of its material prosperity. The cities were supplied with fresh water by aqueducts of remarkable engineering; the roads connected every province; public buildings and baths provided comforts unknown in earlier ages; the grain supply fed millions. By the fifth century, the aqueducts were falling into disrepair, the roads were no longer maintained, the population of the cities had declined, and the material standard of living had deteriorated markedly. The stock of technical knowledge had, if anything, contracted: skills of construction and engineering that had been commonplace in the first century were lost. The first and second conditions of the definition fail. But in the moral domain, one might argue that progress had occurred. The spread of Christianity brought a new ethic of compassion for the weak, the poor, and the suffering. The gladiatorial games — in which men had been compelled to fight to the death for the entertainment of the crowd — were eventually abolished. The institution of slavery, though it persisted, was increasingly questioned. The third condition may be satisfied even as the other two fail. The definition cannot accommodate this unevenness; it requires progress on all fronts simultaneously, when in truth the different dimensions of human life may advance and regress independently of one another.

definition: An account A is a historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies a set of antecedent conditions — political, economic, or social — that, taken together, made E inevitable, (ii) A is grounded in documentary evidence available to the historian, and (iii) A is free from the judgements of value on the part of the historian — that is, A is purely objective.
conditions: A identifies antecedent conditions that made E inevitable
A is grounded in documentary evidence
A is free from the historian's judgements of value
counterexample: Consider the explanation of the French Revolution. Every serious historian cites antecedent conditions — the fiscal crisis of the monarchy, the burden of taxation upon the peasantry, the influence of Enlightenment ideas, the weakness of Louis XVI. But no reputable historian maintains that these conditions made the Revolution inevitable. Had the harvest of 1788 been good, had Necker's financial reforms succeeded, had the king acted with greater resolution, the Revolution might not have occurred, or might have taken a very different course. The first condition fails: the antecedent conditions made the event possible, even probable, but not inevitable.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the antecedent conditions make the event inevitable, but historical events are contingent, and historical explanation consists in showing how events were made possible or probable, not how they were made inevitable. What is wanted is a weaker notion of explanatory connection that accommodates contingency.
passage: Suppose one holds that an account is a historical explanation of an event if and only if it identifies antecedent conditions that made the event inevitable, is grounded in documentary evidence, and is free from the historian's judgements of value. The definition expresses a picture of history as a science of the same rigour as the natural sciences: the historian discovers the conditions that necessitated the event, marshals the documentary evidence, and reports the findings without subjective bias. Yet one must consider the explanation of the French Revolution. Every serious historian who has addressed the subject identifies a complex of antecedent conditions: the fiscal bankruptcy of the Crown, the crushing weight of feudal dues and royal taxes upon the peasantry, the spread of ideas about natural rights and popular sovereignty, the harvest failures of 1788, the indecision of Louis XVI. These conditions are grounded in abundant documentary evidence — the cahiers de doleances, the financial records of the Treasury, the pamphlets and treatises of the philosophes, the correspondence of the court. But no responsible historian maintains that these conditions made the Revolution inevitable. Had the harvest of 1788 been abundant, the immediate crisis might have been averted. Had Necker's financial reforms been adopted, the fiscal problem might have been resolved without convulsion. Had Louis acted with the resolution of his grandfather, the political situation might have been contained. The Revolution was the outcome of a particular and contingent conjunction of circumstances, any one of which might have been otherwise. The first condition of the definition fails. The antecedent conditions explain the Revolution by showing how it became possible, how the ground was prepared and the occasion furnished, not by showing that it was inevitable. The definition has imposed upon history a model of deterministic explanation drawn from the natural sciences, where given the initial conditions and the laws, the outcome follows necessarily, but historical events are not necessary consequences of their antecedent conditions; they are contingent outcomes of contingent circumstances, and their explanation consists in the illumination of contingency, not in the demonstration of inevitability.

definition: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is marked by a clearly identifiable beginning event and a clearly identifiable ending event, (ii) the characteristics that define a period are uniform throughout its duration, and (iii) the transition from one period to the next is abrupt rather than gradual.
conditions: each period is marked by a clearly identifiable beginning and ending events
the characteristics that define a period are uniform throughout its duration
the transition from one period to the next is abrupt
counterexample: Consider the periodisation of European history into 'medieval' and 'modern' periods. The transition from the medieval to the modern is not marked by any single clearly identifiable event. Some historians place the beginning of the modern period at the fall of Constantinople in 1453, others at the discovery of America in 1492, others at the Reformation in 1517, and still others regard it as a gradual process extending over two centuries. The characteristics of the medieval period — feudalism, the dominance of the Church, the agrarian economy — did not disappear at a stroke; they persisted in some regions long after they had vanished in others. The transition was not abrupt but gradual and uneven. All three conditions fail, yet the periodisation is legitimate and indispensable to the study of history.
missing_condition: The definition requires sharp boundaries and uniform characteristics, but historical periods typically have blurred boundaries and internal variation. What is wanted is an account that allows periodisation to be a useful heuristic device even when the boundaries are indeterminate and the defining characteristics are unevenly distributed.
passage: One might hold that a division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if each period is marked by clearly identifiable beginning and ending events, the characteristics that define a period are uniform throughout its duration, and the transition from one period to the next is abrupt. The definition expresses a picture of historical time as divided into distinct compartments, each with its own character, separated by sharp discontinuities. Yet one must consider the periodisation of European history into the medieval and the modern. This periodisation is universally employed; no historian of Europe can avoid it, and it organises the study of a thousand years of history. But the transition from medieval to modern is not marked by any single, clearly identifiable event. The fall of Constantinople in 1453, the voyages of Columbus in 1492, the posting of Luther's theses in 1517 — each has been proposed as the beginning of the modern period, and each has been contested. The transition was not a punctual event but a slow and uneven process, extending over at least two centuries and proceeding at different rates in different regions. In the towns of northern Italy, features recognisably modern — banking, secular learning, republican politics — were established by the fourteenth century; in the rural communities of eastern Europe, features recognisably medieval — serfdom, the manorial economy, the dominance of the Church — persisted well into the eighteenth. The characteristics that define the medieval period were not uniform throughout its duration: the feudalism of the eleventh century was very different from the feudalism of the fourteenth, and the intellectual life of the twelfth century bore little resemblance to that of the eighth. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has demanded of periodisation a sharpness and uniformity that the historical record does not display, overlooking the possibility that periodisation is a heuristic device — useful, even indispensable, but necessarily approximate and always subject to revision.

definition: A society S has made progress over a period if and only if (i) S has increased its material productivity — that is, S produces more goods per unit of labour at the end of the period than at the beginning, (ii) S has expanded the scope of individual freedoms enjoyed by its members, and (iii) S has accumulated a greater stock of scientific and technical understanding.
conditions: S has increased its material productivity
S has expanded the scope of individual freedoms
S has accumulated a greater stock of scientific and technical understanding
counterexample: Consider the society of Victorian England. By the end of the nineteenth century, material productivity had increased enormously through industrialisation; individual freedoms — the franchise, freedom of the press, the abolition of various feudal restraints — had expanded considerably; and the stock of scientific and technical understanding had grown beyond anything previously imagined. Yet this same period saw the creation of urban slums of unspeakable squalor, the exploitation of child labour, and the immiseration of large classes of the population. All three conditions are met, yet one might well hesitate to say, without qualification, that Victorian society had progressed.
missing_condition: The definition omits any condition concerning the distribution of the benefits of material and intellectual advancement. Progress that enriches a few while degrading the many may satisfy all three conditions without constituting progress in the fullest sense.
passage: One might propose that a society has made progress over a period if and only if it has increased its material productivity, expanded the scope of individual freedoms, and accumulated a greater stock of scientific and technical understanding. This definition has a certain comprehensiveness: it captures the material, the political, and the intellectual dimensions of what we ordinarily mean by progress. The productivity condition ensures that the society is, in some tangible sense, better equipped to satisfy the needs of its members. The freedom condition reflects the liberal conviction that progress involves not merely wealth but the enlargement of human autonomy. And the condition regarding understanding captures the Enlightenment faith in the cumulative advance of understanding. Yet one might observe that the definition, for all its apparent breadth, neglects a dimension that many would regard as essential. Consider Victorian England, a society that satisfied all three conditions to a remarkable degree. Industrial production soared; the franchise was extended; the sciences made extraordinary advances. But the same period witnessed the creation of urban squalor that appalled even contemporary observers, the systematic exploitation of children in mines and factories, and the immiseration of entire classes of the population. It appears that a society may increase its productivity, enlarge its freedoms, and deepen its understanding whilst distributing the benefits of these achievements so unequally that large portions of the population are left worse off than before. The definition, it seems, lacks a condition concerning the distribution of the fruits of advancement — a condition that would require the benefits of progress to be shared with some degree of equity among the members of the society.

definition: An account A constitutes a historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies a set of antecedent events and conditions that temporally preceded E, (ii) A subsumes E under a general law or regularity — that is, A shows that events of the type of E regularly follow events of the type identified in the antecedent conditions, and (iii) A is supported by documentary or archaeological evidence.
conditions: A identifies antecedent events and conditions that preceded E
A subsumes E under a general law or regularity
A is supported by documentary or archaeological evidence
counterexample: Consider the fall of the Roman Empire in the West. A historian might identify a set of antecedent conditions — fiscal crisis, military overextension, barbarian pressure — and might point to a general regularity to the effect that empires subject to such pressures tend to fragment. Documentary evidence for these conditions is abundant. Yet the explanation feels incomplete, for it fails to convey the particular character of the Roman collapse — the specific sequence of decisions, personalities, and contingencies that made this decline take the form it did rather than some other. The explanation satisfies all three conditions yet omits precisely the narrative and particular elements that give historical explanation its distinctive character.
missing_condition: The definition requires subsumption under general laws but neglects the narrative or particularising dimension of historical explanation — the element that connects events into an intelligible sequence and conveys why this particular event, with its specific features, occurred.
passage: One might suppose that a historical explanation of an event consists in identifying antecedent events and conditions, subsuming the event under a general law or regularity, and supporting the account with documentary or archaeological evidence. This definition imports into the philosophy of history a model of explanation familiar from the natural sciences — the so-called covering-law model. The antecedent conditions serve as initial conditions, the general law provides the explanatory bridge, and the evidential requirement ensures that the explanation is not mere speculation. Yet one might observe that this model, whatever its merits in the natural sciences, sits uncomfortably in the domain of history. Consider the fall of the Roman Empire in the West. A historian who identifies fiscal crisis, military overextension, and barbarian pressure, and who points to a general regularity to the effect that empires under such pressures tend to fragment, has satisfied all three conditions. Documentary evidence is abundant. Yet something essential has been left out. The explanation does not convey the particular character of the Roman collapse — the specific decisions of specific emperors, the contingencies of particular battles, the personalities of individual generals and barbarian chieftains. It tells us that empires subject to certain pressures tend to fall, but it does not tell us why this empire fell in this way at this time. Historical explanation, it appears, requires not merely the subsumption of events under general laws but the construction of a narrative that renders the particular sequence of events intelligible. What is missing from the definition is a condition that captures this particularising, narrative dimension of historical understanding.

definition: A division of time D constitutes a valid periodisation of a historical process if and only if (i) D partitions the temporal span of the process into non-overlapping intervals, (ii) each interval is characterised by a dominant feature or set of features that distinguishes it from adjacent intervals, and (iii) the boundaries between intervals correspond to identifiable turning points — that is, events or transitions that mark the beginning or end of the dominant feature.
conditions: D partitions the temporal span into non-overlapping intervals
Each interval is characterised by a distinguishing dominant feature
Boundaries correspond to identifiable turning points
counterexample: Consider the standard periodisation of European history into Ancient, Medieval, and Modern periods. This division partitions the temporal span into non-overlapping intervals, each characterised by features commonly taken to be dominant — classical civilisation, feudalism, the rise of the nation-state — and the boundaries correspond to identifiable events such as the fall of Rome and the Renaissance. Yet this periodisation, applied to China or to sub-Saharan Africa, is entirely inapplicable: the turning points of European history are not turning points elsewhere, and the dominant features of each European period may be absent or differently configured in other civilisations. The periodisation satisfies all three conditions for Europe but reveals its parochialism when extended to other regions.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the periodisation be applicable beyond the particular region or civilisation for which it was devised, nor does it require that the dominant features be genuinely fundamental rather than merely conspicuous from a particular cultural vantage point.
passage: One might define a valid periodisation of a historical process as a partition of the temporal span into non-overlapping intervals, each characterised by a dominant feature that distinguishes it from adjacent intervals, with boundaries that correspond to identifiable turning points. The definition has an appealing clarity: it specifies the formal properties of the partition (non-overlapping, exhaustive), the substantive criterion for distinguishing periods (dominant features), and the criterion for locating boundaries (turning points). Yet one might observe that the definition, though formally adequate, is silent on a question that has troubled historians for generations — the question of the universality or parochialism of periodisation. Consider the familiar tripartite division of European history into Ancient, Medieval, and Modern periods. This periodisation satisfies all three conditions admirably for Europe: the intervals do not overlap, each is characterised by a cluster of dominant features, and the boundaries correspond to events — the fall of Rome, the Renaissance — that are widely recognised as turning points. But the periodisation is notoriously inapplicable to other civilisations. The fall of Rome is not a turning point in Chinese history; feudalism, in the European sense, was not a dominant feature of pre-colonial African societies. The periodisation reveals itself to be a construction shaped by a particular cultural and geographical vantage point. The definition, it appears, lacks a condition requiring that the dominant features and turning points be genuinely fundamental rather than parochially conspicuous — a condition that would distinguish periodisations that capture something deep about the process from those that merely reflect the preoccupations of a particular civilisation.

definition: A human community C constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) C has developed a system of writing, (ii) C has established permanent urban settlements, and (iii) C has created a division of labour that supports specialised occupations beyond subsistence agriculture.
conditions: C has developed a system of writing
C has established permanent urban settlements
C has created a division of labour supporting specialised occupations
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire, which governed a vast territory, constructed monumental architecture, maintained a complex administrative apparatus, and supported a high degree of occupational specialisation. The Inca had permanent urban settlements and an elaborate division of labour. Yet the Inca did not possess a system of writing in the conventional sense — they used quipu, a system of knotted strings, for record-keeping rather than a script. The second and third conditions are amply satisfied, but the first is not, or at least not clearly so. Yet to deny that the Inca possessed a civilisation would be absurd.
missing_condition: The definition makes writing a necessary condition of civilisation, but the Inca case reveals that complex societies may develop alternative means of record-keeping and communication that serve the same functions as writing without being writing in the conventional sense.
passage: It is often supposed that a civilisation is a human community that has developed a system of writing, established permanent urban settlements, and created a division of labour supporting specialised occupations. This definition reflects a tradition in historiography that identifies civilisation with a cluster of achievements — literacy, urbanisation, and economic complexity — that distinguish complex societies from simpler ones. The writing condition ensures that the community has achieved a means of recording and transmitting information across time; the urbanisation condition marks the transition from scattered, nomadic existence to settled, concentrated life; and the division of labour reflects the economic surplus and social differentiation that make cultural achievement possible. Yet one might observe that the definition, by making writing a necessary condition, excludes societies that are by any reasonable standard civilised. The Inca Empire governed millions of people across a territory stretching from modern Ecuador to Chile. It constructed monumental architecture, maintained an elaborate system of roads and relay stations, and administered a complex system of taxation and redistribution. It created a division of labour of considerable sophistication and established permanent cities of great size. Yet the Inca did not possess writing in the conventional sense. Their system of record-keeping — the quipu, a device of knotted strings — served many of the functions of writing but was not a script. To deny the title of civilisation to the Inca on the grounds that they lacked writing would be to elevate a particular means of communication above the functions it serves. The definition, it appears, requires a more flexible condition concerning communication and record-keeping.

definition: A society S is in decline during a period if and only if (i) S suffers a sustained diminution in its material prosperity — that is, the wealth available per member of S decreases over the period, (ii) S experiences a weakening of its central political institutions, and (iii) S exhibits a contraction in the geographical extent of its effective authority.
conditions: S suffers a sustained diminution in material prosperity per member
S experiences a weakening of central political institutions
S exhibits a contraction in the geographical extent of its authority
counterexample: Consider the Republic of Venice in the eighteenth century. Venice's material prosperity per capita did not markedly decline; the city remained wealthy by the standards of the day. Its central political institutions — the Doge, the Senate, the Council of Ten — continued to function with their customary efficiency. And the geographical extent of Venetian authority, though diminished from its medieval peak, remained relatively stable through much of the century. Yet Venice is commonly said to have been in decline — a decline manifest in its loss of commercial dynamism, its cultural stagnation, and its growing irrelevance to the great political affairs of Europe. All three conditions are either unmet or only marginally met, yet the judgment of decline is widely shared.
missing_condition: The definition captures only the material and political dimensions of decline, omitting the cultural, intellectual, and strategic dimensions — the loss of vitality, dynamism, and relevance that may constitute decline even when wealth and institutions remain intact.
passage: One might propose that a society is in decline during a period if and only if it suffers a sustained diminution in material prosperity, experiences a weakening of its central political institutions, and exhibits a contraction in the geographical extent of its authority. This definition captures the most visible and dramatic symptoms of decline — impoverishment, institutional decay, and territorial loss — and has the merit of specifying measurable criteria. A historian applying this definition could, in principle, point to economic data, institutional records, and maps to support the judgement that a society was declining. Yet one might observe that the definition is too narrowly drawn. Consider the Republic of Venice in the eighteenth century. By the stated criteria, Venice was not in decline, or at least not clearly so. The city remained prosperous; its political institutions continued to operate with their characteristic formality and precision; and its territorial holdings, though reduced from their medieval extent, were relatively stable. Yet Venice is widely regarded as having been in decline — a decline evident not in poverty or institutional collapse but in the loss of the commercial vigour, the cultural creativity, and the strategic importance that had once made Venice a great power. The Venetian case suggests that decline may be a matter not merely of material loss and institutional weakness but of a subtler diminution — a loss of dynamism, of relevance, of the capacity to shape events. The definition, it appears, omits the cultural and strategic dimensions of decline, and thus fails to capture cases in which a society declines not by falling apart but by ceasing to matter.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition within a community C if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next within C over a significant span of time, (ii) the members of C regard P as having been received from their predecessors and as worthy of preservation, and (iii) P has remained substantially unchanged in its form and content throughout its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted from generation to generation over a significant span of time
Members of C regard P as received from predecessors and worthy of preservation
P has remained substantially unchanged in form and content
counterexample: Consider the tradition of the English common law. It has been transmitted from generation to generation over centuries; its practitioners regard it as received from their predecessors and as worthy of preservation; yet the common law has not remained substantially unchanged. It has been continuously modified by judicial decisions, statutory reform, and changing social circumstances. The common law of 1950 differs profoundly from the common law of 1250 in content, scope, and application. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not, yet few would deny that the common law is a tradition — indeed, it is paradigmatically a tradition.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a tradition remain substantially unchanged, but living traditions characteristically evolve and adapt while retaining a continuity of identity. What is needed is a condition that captures continuity of identity through change, not unchangingness.
passage: One might suppose that a practice constitutes a tradition if and only if it has been transmitted across generations, is regarded by its practitioners as received from their predecessors and worthy of preservation, and has remained substantially unchanged in form and content. This definition reflects a common understanding of tradition as something fixed and inherited — a body of practice passed down intact from the past. The transmission condition captures the intergenerational character of tradition; the attitudinal condition ensures that the practitioners themselves recognise the practice as traditional; and the condition of unchangingness reflects the conservative character that we associate with traditional practices. Yet one might observe that the third condition is, in an important sense, too demanding. Consider the English common law, which is by any reckoning one of the great traditions of Western civilisation. It has been transmitted from generation to generation of lawyers and judges over seven centuries and more. Its practitioners regard it as a body of inherited wisdom, received from their predecessors and worthy of careful preservation. But the common law has not remained substantially unchanged. It has been continuously reshaped by judicial decision, adapted to new circumstances, and reformed by statute. The common law of the twentieth century differs profoundly from the common law of the thirteenth. Yet no one would say that the common law has ceased to be a tradition merely because it has changed. The definition, it appears, confuses tradition with mere repetition. What characterises a living tradition is not unchangingness but a continuity of identity through change — a thread of connection that links each generation's practice to its predecessors, even as the practice itself evolves.

definition: An account A is a historical narrative if and only if (i) A arranges a sequence of events in chronological order, (ii) A connects those events by relations of cause and effect, and (iii) A is based upon evidence drawn from primary sources — documents, artefacts, or testimony contemporaneous with the events described.
conditions: A arranges events in chronological order
A connects events by relations of cause and effect
A is based on evidence from primary sources
counterexample: Consider a chronicle composed by a medieval monk, recording the events of each year in strict chronological order — plagues, harvests, battles, eclipses — with no attempt to connect them causally. The chronicle is arranged chronologically, is based upon the monk's own observation and contemporaneous testimony, but it does not connect the events by relations of cause and effect; each entry stands alone. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not, and yet the chronicle is commonly regarded as a species of historical narrative, albeit a primitive one.
missing_condition: The definition requires causal connection between events, but some forms of historical narrative — annals and chronicles — achieve narrative coherence through temporal sequence and thematic unity rather than through explicit causal linkage. The definition is too restrictive in its demand for causal connection.
passage: One might define a historical narrative as an account that arranges a sequence of events in chronological order, connects them by relations of cause and effect, and is based upon evidence from primary sources. This definition captures the features most commonly associated with narrative history: the temporal ordering of events, the causal connections that render them intelligible, and the evidentiary basis that distinguishes history from fiction. The chronological condition ensures that the narrative preserves the temporal structure of the events it relates. The causal condition ensures that the narrative does not merely list events but explains how each led to or made possible the next. And the evidential condition ties the narrative to the historical record. Yet one might observe that the definition excludes certain forms of historical writing that are commonly, and rightly, called narratives. Consider a medieval chronicle — a record of events composed by a monk, setting down year by year the plagues, the harvests, the battles, and the eclipses that came to his notice. The chronicle is arranged chronologically and is based upon the chronicler's own observation and contemporaneous testimony. But it does not connect the events by relations of cause and effect. Each entry stands in isolation from its neighbours; the plague of one year is not presented as the cause of the famine of the next, nor the battle as the consequence of the diplomatic crisis. The chronicle satisfies the first and third conditions but not the second, yet it is commonly regarded as a form of historical narrative — a primitive one, perhaps, but a narrative nonetheless. The definition, it seems, demands too much of narrative by requiring causal connection. What is wanted is a more permissive account that allows for narrative coherence to arise from temporal sequence, thematic continuity, or the perspective of the narrator, as well as from causal linkage.

definition: A statement S about a historical period is anachronistic if and only if (i) S attributes to the agents or institutions of the period a concept, practice, or artefact that did not exist during that period, (ii) the attribution is presented as a description of the period rather than as an explicitly acknowledged comparison with a later period, and (iii) the concept, practice, or artefact attributed is one that could not have existed during the period, given the state of development at that time.
conditions: S attributes a concept, practice, or artefact not existing in the period
The attribution is presented as description rather than acknowledged comparison
The concept or artefact could not have existed given the state of development
counterexample: Consider a historian who describes the Roman Senate as engaging in 'foreign policy.' The concept of 'foreign policy' in its modern sense — as a coherent, articulated strategy pursued by a sovereign state — did not exist in the Roman period, and the attribution is presented as description rather than comparison. Yet the Romans did engage in deliberate and sustained dealings with other peoples, and the use of 'foreign policy' to describe these activities, while imprecise, captures something real about Roman practice. It is debatable whether the concept 'could not have existed' given the Roman level of political development. All three conditions appear to be met, yet reasonable historians disagree about whether the description is genuinely anachronistic or merely imprecise.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish between anachronisms that are genuinely distorting — that impose alien categories on a period — and those that are merely imprecise translations of concepts that had approximate equivalents in the period. A condition concerning the degree of distortion is needed.
passage: One might propose that a statement about a historical period is anachronistic if and only if it attributes to the period a concept, practice, or artefact that did not then exist, presents the attribution as description rather than explicit comparison, and the concept could not have existed given the state of development at the time. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases of anachronism — the clock striking in Shakespeare's Julius Caesar, the attribution of nationalist sentiment to medieval peasants. The first condition identifies the temporal displacement; the second distinguishes anachronism from the legitimate practice of drawing explicit comparisons across periods; and the third adds a modal element, requiring that the attributed item be not merely absent but impossible at the time. Yet one might observe that many of the most contested cases of alleged anachronism do not fall neatly within the scope of the definition. Consider a historian who describes the Roman Senate as conducting 'foreign policy.' The concept of foreign policy, in its modern sense, did not exist in the Roman period; the attribution is presented as description; and yet it is far from clear that the concept could not have existed, since the Romans did engage in sustained, deliberate dealings with other peoples that bear a family resemblance to what we call foreign policy. The three conditions appear to be satisfied, yet many historians would regard the description as merely imprecise rather than anachronistic. The difficulty is that the definition draws no distinction between anachronisms that are genuinely distorting and those that are rough but illuminating translations of concepts that had approximate equivalents in the earlier period. What is needed, it seems, is a condition that captures the degree of distortion introduced by the attribution.

definition: An institution, practice, or body of ideas L constitutes the legacy of a historical agent or period H if and only if (i) L was originated or decisively shaped by H, (ii) L has persisted beyond the temporal boundaries of H, and (iii) those who maintain L are aware of its connection to H.
conditions: L was originated or decisively shaped by H
L has persisted beyond the temporal boundaries of H
Those who maintain L are aware of its connection to H
counterexample: Consider the Arabic numeral system as used in Europe. This system was decisively shaped by mathematicians of the Islamic world and has persisted far beyond the medieval Islamic civilisation in which it was developed. Yet the vast majority of those who use Arabic numerals daily — shopkeepers, accountants, schoolchildren — are quite unaware of the system's origins in Islamic mathematics. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not, and yet it would be natural to say that the numeral system is part of the legacy of Islamic civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition requires conscious awareness of historical connection, but many of the most important legacies operate precisely through institutions, practices, and habits of thought whose historical origins have been forgotten by those who maintain them.
passage: One might define the legacy of a historical agent or period as an institution, practice, or body of ideas that was originated or shaped by that agent or period, that has persisted beyond the temporal boundaries of the originating period, and whose connection to the originating period is recognised by those who maintain it. This definition captures a common understanding of legacy as a consciously inherited patrimony — something received from the past and valued as a connection to it. The origination condition ties the legacy to its source; the persistence condition ensures that the legacy has survived the passage of time; and the awareness condition distinguishes a genuine legacy from a mere remnant or fossil. Yet one might observe that the third condition is too restrictive. Consider the Arabic numeral system, which was decisively shaped by mathematicians of the medieval Islamic world and has persisted in use throughout Europe and the world ever since. It is, by any reasonable account, one of the most consequential legacies of Islamic civilisation. Yet the overwhelming majority of those who use Arabic numerals every day — in commerce, in science, in the humblest acts of counting — are entirely unaware of the system's historical origins. The connection to the Islamic world has been forgotten, or was never known, by most users. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not. Yet it would be perverse to deny that the numeral system is part of the legacy of Islamic mathematics. The definition, it appears, confuses legacy with conscious inheritance. Many of the most important legacies operate precisely through practices and institutions whose origins have passed out of common awareness.

definition: An event or series of events R constitutes a revolution if and only if (i) R involves a rapid and fundamental change in the political order — specifically, the replacement of one ruling group or system of government by another, (ii) R is brought about by mass mobilisation — that is, by the collective action of a substantial portion of the population, and (iii) R is motivated by an explicit programme of political and social transformation.
conditions: R involves rapid and fundamental change in the political order
R is brought about by mass mobilisation
R is motivated by an explicit programme of transformation
counterexample: Consider the so-called Glorious Revolution of 1688 in England. The political order was fundamentally changed: the Stuart monarchy was replaced by William of Orange, and the constitutional settlement that followed established parliamentary supremacy. The change was rapid — accomplished within a few months. Yet the Glorious Revolution was brought about not by mass mobilisation but by a coup d'etat orchestrated by a small number of aristocrats and parliamentarians, supported by a foreign army. There was no general rising of the population, and the programme of transformation, such as it was, emerged after the event rather than motivating it. The first condition is met, but the second and third are not, and yet the event is universally called a revolution.
missing_condition: The definition requires mass mobilisation and an explicit programme, but some of the most consequential revolutions in history — including the event that gives the English-speaking world the very word 'revolution' in its political sense — were elite-driven and programmatically vague. The definition is too narrow in its specification of agency and motivation.
passage: One might propose that a revolution is an event involving rapid and fundamental change in the political order, brought about by mass mobilisation, and motivated by an explicit programme of political and social transformation. This definition captures the features of the great paradigmatic revolutions — the French, the Russian, the Chinese — in which the old order was swept away by the collective action of the populace in pursuit of a declared ideal. The requirement of rapid change distinguishes revolution from gradual reform; the mass mobilisation condition distinguishes it from a palace coup; and the programmatic condition distinguishes it from mere disorder or rebellion without purpose. Yet one might observe that the definition excludes events that are universally recognised as revolutions. Consider the Glorious Revolution of 1688. It is the event from which the English-speaking world derives the very concept of political revolution, and it fundamentally altered the constitutional order of England. Yet it was not brought about by mass mobilisation. It was an affair of aristocrats and parliamentarians, supported by a Dutch army; the common people of England played little active role. Nor was it motivated by an explicit programme of transformation. The Bill of Rights and the constitutional settlement emerged after the event, as attempts to codify and legitimate what had already been accomplished. The Glorious Revolution satisfies the first condition but fails to satisfy the second and third, and yet no historian would deny it the title of revolution. The definition, it appears, has been drawn too narrowly, taking the French Revolution as its paradigm and excluding other equally legitimate instances of the concept.

definition: A change C in a society constitutes progress if and only if (i) C produces a measurable improvement in the material conditions of life for the members of that society, (ii) the improvement brought about by C is irreversible in the sense that the new conditions are self-sustaining, and (iii) C is recognised by the members of the society as an improvement.
conditions: C produces a measurable improvement in material conditions
The improvement is irreversible and self-sustaining
C is recognised by the members of the society as an improvement
counterexample: Consider the rapid industrialisation of a rural society. The industrialisation produces a measurable improvement in material conditions — higher incomes, better access to manufactured goods, increased life expectancy. The new industrial economy is self-sustaining and in that sense irreversible. And the members of the society, or at least a majority of them, recognise the change as an improvement. All three conditions are met. Yet the industrialisation also destroys a way of life — agrarian communities, traditional crafts, patterns of kinship and mutual support — that the members of the society may only later come to recognise as valuable. The 'recognition' of improvement is shaped by the very forces that brought the change about, and may not survive the passage of time.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the recognition of improvement be stable and informed rather than transient and shaped by the excitement of novelty or the pressure of changed circumstances. A condition requiring that the assessment of improvement withstand reflection over time is needed.
passage: One might propose that a change constitutes progress if and only if it produces a measurable improvement in material conditions, the improvement is self-sustaining and irreversible, and the members of the society recognise the change as an improvement. This definition has the virtue of combining an objective element — measurable improvement — with a subjective one — the recognition of the members — thereby avoiding the twin dangers of imposing an external standard of progress and of allowing progress to be whatever anyone happens to think it is. The irreversibility condition is intended to distinguish genuine progress from temporary gains that are subsequently lost. Yet one might observe that the requirement of recognition is fraught with difficulty. Consider the rapid industrialisation of a largely agrarian society. Income rises, manufactured goods become available, life expectancy increases. The population, in the flush of new prosperity, regards the change as an improvement. All three conditions are met. But the industrialisation has also destroyed a way of life — the rhythms of agricultural work, the bonds of village community, the transmission of traditional skills — whose value the members of the society may only come to appreciate after its disappearance. The recognition of improvement, far from being an independent judgement, is shaped by the very forces that brought the change about; those who have never known the old way of life cannot meaningfully assess what has been lost. The definition, it appears, requires recognition but does not require that recognition be informed, reflective, or stable over time. What is needed is a condition ensuring that the assessment of improvement is not merely an artefact of the conditions it purports to evaluate.

definition: An account A constitutes an adequate historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A specifies the necessary conditions without which E would not have occurred, (ii) A demonstrates that E was the outcome most likely given those conditions, and (iii) A is consistent with the available documentary and archaeological evidence.
conditions: A specifies the necessary conditions without which E would not have occurred
A demonstrates that E was the outcome most likely given those conditions
A is consistent with the available evidence
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the outbreak of the First World War that specifies the necessary conditions — the alliance system, the arms race, the instability of the Austro-Hungarian Empire — and demonstrates that, given these conditions, a major war was the most likely outcome, all while remaining consistent with the documentary evidence. The three conditions are met. Yet the explanation makes the war appear inevitable, as though the specific chain of events — the assassination at Sarajevo, the ultimatum to Serbia, the sequence of mobilisations — were of no consequence. An explanation that reduces a contingent historical event to the necessary product of structural conditions fails to capture the role of contingency, accident, and individual decision in shaping the course of events.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the explanation account for the contingent and particular features of the event — the specific circumstances, decisions, and accidents that determined why the event took the form it did, rather than some other form consistent with the same structural conditions.
passage: One might propose that an adequate historical explanation specifies the necessary conditions without which the event would not have occurred, demonstrates that the event was the most likely outcome given those conditions, and is consistent with the available evidence. This definition captures an important dimension of historical explanation: the identification of the conditions that made the event possible and even probable. The requirement that the event be shown to be the most likely outcome is intended to give the explanation predictive force — to show that, given a thorough understanding of the conditions, one could have anticipated the event. And the evidential condition ensures that the explanation is grounded in the historical record rather than in speculation. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken together, produce explanations that make history appear more determined than it is. Consider the outbreak of the Great War of 1914. One can specify the necessary conditions with great precision: the alliance system, the arms race, the national rivalries, the fragility of Austria-Hungary. One can argue, plausibly, that a major European war was the most likely outcome of these conditions. And the explanation is amply supported by documentary evidence. All three conditions are met. But the explanation makes the war seem inevitable — the inescapable consequence of structural forces. It has nothing to say about the assassination at Sarajevo, the particular miscalculations of particular statesmen, the accidents of timing and communication that determined not merely whether a war would occur but when and how. The definition, it appears, confuses structural preconditions with complete explanation, and in doing so it systematically effaces the role of contingency and individual agency in the making of history.

definition: A division of historical time into periods constitutes a valid periodisation if and only if (i) each period is characterised by a distinctive set of social, political, or cultural features that distinguish it from adjacent periods, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant changes in those features, and (iii) the periodisation applies uniformly across the society or civilisation under study.
conditions: Each period has distinctive features distinguishing it from adjacent periods
Boundaries correspond to significant changes in those features
The periodisation applies uniformly across the society under study
counterexample: Consider the standard periodisation of European history into 'Medieval' and 'Renaissance' periods, with the boundary placed at roughly 1400. The Renaissance period is characterised by a distinctive set of cultural features — the revival of classical learning, the flowering of the visual arts, the emergence of humanism — that distinguish it from the preceding period. The boundary corresponds to a significant change in these features. But the periodisation does not apply uniformly across Europe: while Italian city-states were experiencing the Renaissance in the fifteenth century, much of northern Europe, Scandinavia, and Eastern Europe retained social and cultural patterns far more continuous with the medieval period. All three conditions cannot be simultaneously satisfied, for the very features that define the period in one region are absent from another.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the periodisation apply uniformly, but significant historical transformations rarely occur simultaneously across an entire civilisation. A condition acknowledging regional variation and temporal unevenness in the adoption of the defining features is needed.
passage: One might propose that a valid periodisation of history divides time into periods characterised by distinctive features, places boundaries at points of significant change, and applies uniformly across the society under study. This definition captures the intuition that periodisation should not be arbitrary — that the divisions should correspond to genuine differences in the character of social life. The requirement of uniformity is intended to ensure that the periodisation reflects a transformation of the whole society, not merely of a particular region or class. Yet one might observe that this requirement proves fatal to many of the most widely used periodisations. Consider the division between 'Medieval' and 'Renaissance' Europe. In the Italian city-states, the fifteenth century witnessed a remarkable transformation in art, learning, and civic life — a transformation that justifies speaking of a new period. But in much of northern and eastern Europe, the social and cultural patterns of the preceding period persisted with little alteration well into the sixteenth century. The peasant of fifteenth-century Scandinavia inhabited a world far more continuous with the thirteenth century than with the Florence of Lorenzo de' Medici. The periodisation applies to Italy but not to Europe as a whole; the condition of uniformity is violated. And yet the concept of the Renaissance is one of the most fruitful and illuminating in the historian's vocabulary. The definition, it appears, imposes a condition — uniformity of application — that is too strict for the realities of historical change, which proceeds unevenly across regions, classes, and domains of life. What is needed is a more flexible condition that permits periodisation to acknowledge variation and unevenness within the broad pattern of change.

definition: A society S constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) S possesses a system of writing capable of recording and transmitting complex information across generations, (ii) S is organised around permanent settlements with a division of labour extending beyond subsistence agriculture, and (iii) S maintains a system of laws or customary regulations governing the conduct of its members.
conditions: S possesses a system of writing
S is organised around permanent settlements with a division of labour beyond subsistence agriculture
S maintains a system of laws or customary regulations
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire, which governed a territory stretching from modern Colombia to Chile, maintained an elaborate system of roads, possessed a sophisticated division of labour, and administered a complex legal and bureaucratic apparatus. By any reasonable standard, it was a civilisation. Yet the Inca had no system of writing in the conventional sense. Their quipu — knotted cords used for record-keeping — is debated as to whether it constitutes writing. The first condition is not clearly met, and yet the Inca Empire exhibits every other feature of a civilisation in the fullest sense.
missing_condition: The definition treats writing as a necessary condition of civilisation, but this excludes societies that achieved equivalent levels of social complexity, administrative organisation, and cultural sophistication through non-written means of information storage and transmission.
passage: One might define a civilisation as a society that possesses a system of writing, is organised around permanent settlements with a developed division of labour, and maintains a system of laws or customary regulations. This definition reflects a long tradition in which writing is taken as the hallmark of civilisation — the criterion that distinguishes the civilised from the pre-civilised, the historical from the prehistoric. Writing, on this view, is not merely a convenience but a precondition: it enables the accumulation of administrative records, the codification of laws, the transmission of literature and learning, and the collective memory that gives a society its sense of continuity. The division of labour ensures that the society has progressed beyond the subsistence level, and the legal system provides the framework of order within which complex social life can be sustained. Yet one might observe that the definition excludes certain societies that exhibit every other mark of civilisation in the highest degree. Consider the Inca Empire, which at its height governed millions of people across an immense territory, built an extraordinary network of roads and bridges, maintained a sophisticated administrative apparatus, and developed an elaborate system of social obligations and legal regulations. The Inca, however, did not possess a system of writing in the ordinary sense. Their quipu — knotted strings used for recording numerical and possibly narrative information — occupies a disputed position between writing and some other form of record-keeping. The definition, by making writing a necessary condition, excludes the Inca from the ranks of the civilised, a conclusion that most historians would find deeply counterintuitive. What the definition lacks is a recognition that writing is one means of achieving the informational and administrative capacities required for civilisation, but not the only one.

definition: A society S is in decline if and only if (i) the institutional structures of S — its political, legal, and administrative systems — are progressively losing their capacity to perform the functions for which they were established, (ii) the productive capacities of S — its agriculture, industry, and trade — are diminishing over a sustained period, and (iii) the members of S widely perceive and lament the deterioration of their conditions.
conditions: Institutional structures are progressively losing their functional capacity
Productive capacities are diminishing over a sustained period
Members widely perceive and lament the deterioration
counterexample: Consider a society whose political institutions are functioning smoothly, whose agricultural and industrial output is stable or even growing, but whose members are gripped by a pervasive sense of decline — a feeling that standards have fallen, that the present is inferior to the past, that the best days are behind them. The first and second conditions are not met, since the institutions and productive capacities are intact. But suppose the sense of decline is itself a political and cultural force — suppose it leads to the election of demagogues, the abandonment of prudent policy, and the erosion of civic trust. In this case, the perception of decline, though initially unfounded, may bring about the very decline it professes to describe. The definition requires that decline be actual before it can be perceived, but perception may be causally prior.
missing_condition: The definition treats the perception of decline as a consequence of actual institutional and economic deterioration, but the perception of decline may be a cause rather than a consequence — a self-fulfilling prophecy that undermines institutions and capacities that were previously intact.
passage: One might propose that a society is in decline if and only if its institutional structures are progressively losing their functional capacity, its productive capacities are diminishing, and its members widely perceive and lament the deterioration. This definition has the virtue of combining objective indicators — institutional dysfunction and economic contraction — with a subjective dimension — the consciousness of decline that pervades the affected society. The objective conditions are intended to ensure that the concept of decline is not merely a projection of pessimistic temperaments; the subjective condition is intended to reflect the fact that decline is, in part, an experience — a collective awareness of loss. Yet one might observe that the three conditions are not always related in the way the definition presupposes. Consider a society whose institutions are functioning adequately and whose economy is stable, but whose members are nevertheless gripped by a profound conviction that their society is deteriorating. This conviction may arise from nostalgia, from unfavourable comparisons with other societies, or from the rhetoric of political leaders who find the narrative of decline useful. The first and second conditions are not met. But suppose the conviction of decline proves to be self-fulfilling: the belief that institutions are failing leads to a withdrawal of trust; the belief that the economy is contracting leads to hoarding and reduced investment; and these behaviours bring about the very institutional dysfunction and economic contraction that the perception described but did not, at the outset, reflect. The definition assumes that perception follows reality, but the relationship between the two may run in the opposite direction. What is needed is a condition that addresses the causal interplay between the objective and subjective dimensions of decline, rather than simply listing them as independent requirements.

definition: A practice P constitutes a living tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a period of at least three generations, (ii) P is performed in a manner substantially continuous with its earliest known form, and (iii) the practitioners of P regard it as an inheritance from the past to which they owe fidelity.
conditions: P has been transmitted across at least three generations
P is performed in a manner substantially continuous with its earliest known form
Practitioners regard P as an inheritance to which they owe fidelity
counterexample: Consider the celebration of Christmas in the modern West. The festival has been transmitted across many generations and is regarded by its celebrants as a venerable inheritance. Yet the modern celebration — with its decorated tree, its gift-giving, its figure of Father Christmas — differs profoundly from the earliest known forms of the festival, which were liturgical observances of the Nativity with little resemblance to contemporary practice. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not, if 'substantially continuous' is taken strictly. Yet if 'substantially continuous' is relaxed to accommodate such transformations, it becomes unclear what degree of change is compatible with continuity, and the condition loses its force.
missing_condition: The definition requires substantial continuity of form but provides no criterion for distinguishing change that preserves a tradition from change that transforms it into something else. A condition specifying what must be preserved — perhaps the core meaning or function of the practice — rather than its outward form, is needed.
passage: One might define a living tradition as a practice that has been transmitted across at least three generations, is performed in a manner substantially continuous with its earliest known form, and is regarded by its practitioners as an inheritance to which they owe fidelity. The generational requirement is intended to exclude recent innovations that have not yet acquired the patina of custom; the continuity requirement ensures that the practice has not been so transformed as to sever its connection with the past; and the requirement of fidelity captures the distinctive attitude of the traditionalist — the sense that the practice is not merely habitual but normative, something received and to be passed on. Yet one might observe that the continuity condition proves troublesome in application. Consider the celebration of Christmas in the modern West. The festival has undeniably been transmitted across many generations, and its celebrants regard it as a venerable custom inherited from the past. But the modern celebration — centred upon a decorated tree, the exchange of gifts, and the figure of a red-suited gentleman entering through the chimney — bears little resemblance to the early liturgical observance of the Nativity, which was a solemn ecclesiastical rite. If 'substantially continuous' requires preservation of form, the modern Christmas fails the test. If it is relaxed to accommodate transformation, then one must ask how much change is compatible with continuity — a question the definition does not address. What is needed, it appears, is a condition that specifies what must be preserved for a tradition to remain the same tradition: perhaps not its outward form, which inevitably changes, but its core meaning, purpose, or social function.

definition: An account A is a faithful historical narrative if and only if (i) A presents events in the order in which they occurred, (ii) A attributes to historical agents only those motives and intentions for which there is documentary evidence, and (iii) A does not omit any event that is relevant to the outcome described.
conditions: A presents events in chronological order
A attributes only evidentially supported motives and intentions
A does not omit any relevant event
counterexample: Consider a narrative of Napoleon's Russian campaign that presents every event in strict chronological order, attributes no motive to any agent without documentary support, and includes every battle, march, and diplomatic exchange for which records survive. All three conditions are met. Yet the resulting narrative may be a formless chronicle — a sequence of dated occurrences from which no pattern emerges, no significance is discernible, and no understanding of why the campaign ended as it did is conveyed. The narrative is faithful in the sense demanded by the definition, but it is not a good narrative, for it provides no framework of interpretation that would make the events intelligible.
missing_condition: The definition requires fidelity to the evidence and completeness of coverage but fails to require that the narrative render the events intelligible — that it provide a framework of interpretation that enables the reader to understand why events occurred as they did and not otherwise.
passage: One might propose that a faithful historical narrative presents events in the order in which they occurred, attributes only evidentially supported motives, and omits no relevant event. These conditions express a high standard of fidelity: the narrative must follow the temporal order of events, must not speculate beyond the evidence, and must not achieve its effect by selective omission. The chronological condition guards against the imposition of a pattern that distorts the sequence of cause and effect. The evidential condition protects against the projection of the narrator's own preconceptions onto the agents of the past. And the completeness condition ensures that the narrative does not mislead by leaving out inconvenient facts. Yet one might observe that a narrative satisfying all three conditions may nonetheless fail as a narrative. Consider an account of Napoleon's invasion of Russia that records every event for which evidence survives in strict chronological order, attributing only documented motives and omitting nothing relevant. The result would be an enormously detailed chronicle — a succession of dated entries covering marches, battles, desertions, supply difficulties, diplomatic communications, weather reports, and casualty figures. All three conditions would be met. But the chronicle, for all its fidelity, would not constitute a narrative in any meaningful sense, for it would provide no framework of interpretation — no account of why events took the course they did, no identification of turning points, no discrimination between the significant and the trivial. The definition, it appears, has confused the raw materials of narrative with narrative itself. What is missing is a condition requiring that the account render the events intelligible, not merely by recording them but by organising them into a pattern that enables understanding.

definition: A historical interpretation I is anachronistic if and only if (i) I attributes to the agents of a past period concepts, categories, or values that were not available to those agents at the time, (ii) the attribution distorts the interpretation of the agents' actions or intentions, and (iii) the concepts attributed are demonstrably products of a later period.
conditions: I attributes to past agents concepts not available to them at the time
The attribution distorts the interpretation of agents' actions or intentions
The concepts attributed are demonstrably products of a later period
counterexample: Consider a historian who attributes to the builders of Stonehenge a concept of 'astronomical observatory' — saying that they built it 'as an observatory for tracking celestial events.' The concept of an observatory is a product of a later period, and attributing it to the builders might be said to distort our understanding of their intentions. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the attribution may be heuristically valuable — it may illuminate a genuine function of the monument, even if the builders did not conceptualise their activity in those terms. Whether the attribution counts as a distortion depends on whether one regards it as a claim about the builders' self-understanding or as a description of the monument's function. The definition does not distinguish between these two kinds of attribution.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish between anachronistic attributions that concern the self-understanding of historical agents and those that concern the objective function or significance of their actions. A condition specifying the kind of attribution that constitutes anachronism — attributions of self-understanding versus attributions of function — is needed.
passage: One might define an anachronistic interpretation as one that attributes to the agents of a past period concepts that were not available to them, does so in a way that distorts the interpretation of their actions, and attributes concepts that are demonstrably products of a later period. This definition captures a genuine and important vice in historical writing: the projection of the present onto the past, the reading of modern ideas and values into the minds of people who could not have entertained them. The distortion condition is intended to ensure that not every use of modern language counts as anachronism — only those uses that mislead. And the requirement that the concepts be demonstrably later products adds an empirical dimension that distinguishes genuine anachronism from mere imprecision. Yet one might observe that the definition fails to draw a distinction that is essential for the practice of history. Consider a historian who describes Stonehenge as 'an astronomical observatory.' The concept of an observatory is certainly a product of a later period, and attributing it to the builders of Stonehenge may distort our understanding of their intentions. All three conditions appear to be met. But the historian may not intend the attribution as a claim about the builders' self-understanding; she may mean only that the monument served a function analogous to that of an observatory — that it was used to track celestial events, whatever the builders' own conception of their activity. If the attribution is understood functionally rather than psychologically, it may illuminate rather than distort. The definition does not distinguish between these two kinds of attribution and therefore cannot determine whether the description of Stonehenge as an observatory is an anachronistic distortion or a fruitful analogy.

definition: A historical event or figure F has a legacy if and only if (i) F has produced effects that persist beyond the period in which F occurred or lived, (ii) the persisting effects are causally traceable to F rather than to independent causes, and (iii) the persisting effects are recognised by subsequent generations as having originated in F.
conditions: F has produced effects persisting beyond its own period
The effects are causally traceable to F
Subsequent generations recognise the effects as originating in F
counterexample: Consider the Justinianic plague of 541-542 CE, which killed a large proportion of the population of the Byzantine Empire and, by weakening the empire's military and economic capacity, may have contributed to the eventual Islamic conquests of the seventh century. The plague produced effects persisting far beyond its own period, and those effects are causally traceable to the demographic catastrophe. But for many centuries, subsequent generations did not recognise these long-term geopolitical consequences as originating in the plague; the connection was drawn only by modern historians. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not — at least not until recently. Yet the plague had a legacy in the fullest sense, whether or not subsequent generations recognised it.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the legacy be recognised by subsequent generations, but many of the most consequential legacies — demographic shifts, ecological changes, long-term economic transformations — operate beneath the level of conscious recognition and are identified as legacies only by later scholarship. A condition of recognition is too restrictive.
