passage: One might define a legacy as a set of persisting effects produced by a historical event or figure, causally traceable to that event or figure, and recognised by subsequent generations as having originated therein. This definition attempts to capture the idea that a legacy is not merely an effect but an acknowledged effect — a debt that later generations recognise they owe to the past. The requirement of causal traceability is intended to exclude cases in which an event is merely followed by certain developments without having caused them, and the requirement of recognition is meant to capture the essentially commemorative or interpretive dimension of legacy. Yet one might observe that some of the most consequential legacies in human history operated for centuries without being recognised as such. Consider the great plague that struck the Byzantine Empire in the sixth century. Modern historians have argued that the demographic catastrophe weakened the empire so severely that it was unable to resist the Islamic conquests of the following century — an argument that, if correct, makes the plague one of the most consequential events in the history of the Mediterranean world. The effects persisted for centuries and are causally traceable to the plague. But for most of the intervening period, no one recognised these geopolitical consequences as originating in the epidemic; the connection was drawn only by modern scholarship. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not — or was not until very recently. Yet it would be strange to deny that the plague had a legacy merely because subsequent generations failed to recognise it. The definition, it appears, confuses the existence of a legacy with its conscious acknowledgement.

definition: An event R constitutes a revolution if and only if (i) R produces a fundamental change in the political constitution of a society — a change in the form of government, the distribution of sovereignty, or the legal framework, (ii) R occurs rapidly — within a span of months or a few years rather than decades, and (iii) R involves the active participation of a substantial portion of the society's population.
conditions: R produces a fundamental change in the political constitution
R occurs rapidly — within months or a few years
R involves the active participation of a substantial portion of the population
counterexample: Consider the Meiji Restoration of 1868 in Japan, which produced a fundamental transformation of the Japanese political order — abolishing the shogunate, restoring imperial authority, and laying the groundwork for a modern state. The change occurred rapidly, concentrated within a span of a few years. Yet the transformation was effected not by the active participation of a substantial portion of the population but by a relatively small group of samurai reformers who acted in the name of the emperor. The first and second conditions are met, but the third is not. Nonetheless, the Meiji Restoration is widely regarded as one of the most consequential revolutions in modern history.
missing_condition: The definition requires mass participation, but many of the most transformative political upheavals in history have been accomplished by small, determined elites acting without the direct involvement of the broader population. A condition of mass participation is too restrictive.
passage: One might propose that an event constitutes a revolution if and only if it produces a fundamental change in the political constitution, occurs rapidly, and involves the active participation of a substantial portion of the population. This definition reflects the paradigm of the great democratic revolutions — the French Revolution of 1789, the American Revolution, the revolutions of 1848 — in which the transformation of the political order was accomplished through the mass mobilisation of citizens. The requirement of rapidity distinguishes revolution from gradual reform, and the requirement of mass participation distinguishes it from the palace coup or the military putsch. Yet one might observe that this definition, shaped by the European and American paradigms, proves too narrow when applied to the broader sweep of political history. Consider the Meiji Restoration of 1868 in Japan. It produced a transformation as fundamental as any in modern history: the abolition of the shogunate, the restoration of imperial authority, the creation of a modern centralised state, the dismantling of the feudal order. The change was rapid — concentrated within a few years. But it was not accomplished through mass participation. A relatively small group of samurai reformers, acting in concert with court nobles and provincial lords, effected the transformation from above. The population at large was the object rather than the agent of the revolution. The first and second conditions are met, but the third is not. Yet to deny the Meiji Restoration the title of revolution would be to exclude one of the most far-reaching political transformations of the modern era. The definition, it appears, has elevated a feature of certain revolutions — mass participation — to the status of a universal requirement.

definition: A society S has made progress over a period if and only if (i) S has achieved measurable improvements in material conditions of life, (ii) these improvements are widely distributed across the population of S, and (iii) the improvements are sustained rather than temporary.
conditions: S has achieved measurable improvements in material conditions of life
These improvements are widely distributed across the population
The improvements are sustained rather than temporary
counterexample: Consider the Roman Empire during the second century A.D. Material conditions — roads, aqueducts, public sanitation — were measurably improved. These improvements were widely distributed across the Empire's vast population. And they were sustained over several generations. Yet this period also witnessed the entrenchment of slavery, the suppression of political liberty, and the stagnation of philosophical enquiry. It appears strained to say without qualification that Rome had progressed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require improvements in dimensions beyond the material — in political liberty, moral sensibility, or intellectual achievement — without which material gains alone do not constitute progress in the full sense.
passage: A natural analysis of historical progress might identify it with measurable, widely distributed, and sustained improvements in material conditions. This definition captures what many commentators have had in mind when speaking of the progress of civilisation: rising standards of nourishment, more reliable shelter, improved sanitation, increased longevity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis presents itself when one considers the Roman Empire at its height. During the Antonine period, the material conditions of life across the Empire were measurably superior to those of preceding centuries. Roads connected distant provinces; aqueducts carried fresh water to cities; public baths and granaries served great numbers. These improvements were not confined to an elite but were enjoyed, to varying degrees, across much of the population, and they were sustained over several generations. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet one might observe that this same period saw the institution of slavery on an enormous scale, the crushing of political liberty under autocratic rule, and a marked stagnation in philosophical and scientific enquiry. If progress is to mean more than material comfort, the definition requires a further dimension — one that captures advances in liberty, moral development, or the life of the mind.

definition: An account A constitutes a historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies antecedent events that preceded E, (ii) A shows that E followed from these antecedents by a plausible chain of connection, and (iii) A is consistent with the documentary evidence available.
conditions: A identifies antecedent events that preceded E
A shows that E followed from these antecedents by a plausible chain of connection
A is consistent with the documentary evidence
counterexample: Consider two rival accounts of the fall of the Western Roman Empire — one emphasising barbarian invasions, the other emphasising internal fiscal collapse. Both identify genuine antecedent events. Both trace plausible chains of connection to the outcome. Both are consistent with the surviving documentary evidence. Yet they are incompatible in the emphasis they assign, and neither can be preferred to the other on the strength of the three conditions alone.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the antecedent events identified be not merely plausibly connected to the outcome but causally decisive, and it provides no criterion for choosing among rival accounts that equally satisfy the stated conditions.
passage: One might propose that a historical explanation is an account that identifies antecedent events, traces a plausible chain of connection from those events to the explanandum, and is consistent with the documentary evidence. This definition has an air of common sense about it: it insists on antecedence, plausibility, and evidential fit. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in a familiar historical controversy. The fall of the Western Roman Empire in the fifth century has been attributed to barbarian invasions from without and to fiscal and administrative collapse from within. Both accounts identify genuine antecedent events. Both trace plausible chains from these events to the Empire's dissolution. And both are consistent with the surviving documentary record, which is sufficiently fragmentary to accommodate either interpretation. The three conditions do not suffice to choose between the two accounts, nor do they identify the causally decisive antecedents. One is left with two equally acceptable explanations of the same event, which suggests that the definition captures necessary but not sufficient conditions for a satisfactory historical explanation.

definition: A division of the historical continuum into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) the boundaries between periods correspond to events of recognised significance, (ii) the characteristics attributed to each period are shared by the preponderance of events and developments within it, and (iii) the periods are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive over the timespan in question.
conditions: Boundaries correspond to events of recognised significance
Characteristics attributed to each period are shared by the preponderance of events within it
Periods are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive
counterexample: Consider the conventional division of European history into 'medieval' and 'modern' periods, with the boundary set at 1500. The fall of Constantinople in 1453 and the voyages of Columbus in 1492 are events of recognised significance near this boundary. And the division is exclusive and exhaustive. Yet many features attributed to the 'medieval' period — feudal social relations, scholastic thought, guild-based economies — persisted well into the sixteenth century in much of Europe, while features attributed to modernity appeared in Italian city-states by the fourteenth century.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the transition between periods be sufficiently abrupt and synchronised across the relevant domains of human activity, rather than gradual and uneven.
passage: It is common to divide the historical continuum into periods — ancient, medieval, modern — and to regard such divisions as legitimate when the boundaries mark significant events, the characteristics of each period are widely shared within it, and the periods are exclusive and exhaustive. One might observe that this analysis seems sound on its face: it insists on significant turning points, on internal coherence, and on logical tidiness. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the conventional division of European history into a medieval period ending around 1500 and a modern period beginning thereafter. The boundary is marked by events of acknowledged significance — the fall of Constantinople, the voyages of discovery, the advent of printing. And the division is exclusive and exhaustive. But the characteristics assigned to the medieval period — feudalism, scholasticism, guild-based economic organisation — did not vanish at the stroke of 1500. In much of rural Europe, feudal arrangements persisted well into the sixteenth and even seventeenth centuries, while scholastic philosophy retained its hold in many universities. Conversely, features commonly attributed to modernity — commercial capitalism, secular humanism, republican governance — had already appeared in Italian city-states by the fourteenth century. The preponderance test is met only if one squints. What the definition overlooks is the requirement that the transition be sufficiently abrupt and synchronised to justify a sharp boundary.

definition: A human community C constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) C has developed a system of writing, (ii) C has established permanent urban settlements, and (iii) C exhibits a division of labour extending beyond subsistence agriculture.
conditions: C has developed a system of writing
C has established permanent urban settlements
C exhibits a division of labour extending beyond subsistence agriculture
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire, which governed a vast territory, built the city of Cuzco and the fortress of Sacsayhuaman, maintained an elaborate road system, and sustained a complex division of labour including metallurgists, architects, and administrators. By any ordinary reckoning, the Inca constituted a civilisation. Yet they possessed no system of writing in the conventional sense — their quipu, a system of knotted strings, recorded numerical and possibly narrative information but was not a writing system of the sort the definition envisages.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the possibility that a community may achieve all the organisational and cultural complexity characteristic of civilisation while employing a recording system that does not constitute writing in the narrow sense.
passage: A familiar analysis holds that a civilisation is a human community that has developed writing, established permanent urban settlements, and evolved a division of labour extending beyond subsistence agriculture. This tripartite definition captures much of what historians have meant by the term: Mesopotamia, Egypt, and China satisfy all three criteria. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the Inca Empire of pre-Columbian South America. The Inca governed a territory stretching thousands of miles, built the remarkable city of Cuzco and the imposing fortress of Sacsayhuaman, maintained an extensive road network, and supported a complex division of labour encompassing metallurgists, architects, weavers, potters, and administrators. By any ordinary reckoning, theirs was a civilisation of high achievement. Yet the Inca possessed no system of writing in the conventional sense. Their quipu — an intricate system of knotted and coloured strings — served to record numerical data and perhaps narrative content, but it was not writing as the definition understands it. If we insist upon writing as a necessary condition of civilisation, we are forced to deny that the Inca were civilised, a conclusion that strikes most historians as untenable.

definition: A society S is in decline over a period if and only if (i) the institutions of S lose their capacity to fulfil their designated functions, (ii) the population of S experiences a deterioration in material welfare, and (iii) the cultural output of S diminishes in quality or quantity.
conditions: The institutions of S lose their capacity to fulfil their designated functions
The population experiences a deterioration in material welfare
Cultural output diminishes in quality or quantity
counterexample: Consider late Tang China in the ninth century. The central bureaucracy lost much of its capacity to administer the provinces and collect taxes; rebellions fragmented imperial authority. Material conditions for the populace deteriorated markedly. Yet the poetry of this period — Li Shangyin, Du Mu, and others of the Late Tang — is widely regarded as among the finest in the Chinese literary tradition. Cultural output did not diminish; it flourished even as the political and material order decayed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that decline may affect different dimensions of social life asynchronously, so that political and material deterioration may coincide with cultural vitality.
passage: One might define the decline of a society as the conjunction of institutional incapacity, material deterioration, and cultural diminution. This definition has the advantage of comprehensiveness: it requires that decline manifest itself across several dimensions of social life rather than in one alone. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by late Tang China. By the ninth century, the central bureaucracy had lost much of its capacity to govern the provinces, collect taxes, and suppress rebellion. The An Lushan revolt and its aftermath had fragmented imperial authority. Material conditions for the population had deteriorated: famine, displacement, and banditry were widespread. Two of the three conditions are clearly met. Yet the cultural output of this period is astonishing. The poetry of Li Shangyin, Du Mu, and their contemporaries stands among the finest achievements of the Chinese literary tradition. Far from diminishing, cultural production reached heights that later centuries would look back upon with admiration. The definition demands that all three dimensions decline in concert, but the historical record shows that they need not. A society may be in manifest political and material decay while experiencing a cultural efflorescence.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition within a community C if and only if (i) P has been transmitted across at least three successive generations of C, (ii) P is regarded by members of C as having been inherited from their predecessors, and (iii) P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted across at least three successive generations
P is regarded by members of C as inherited from predecessors
P has remained substantially unchanged throughout transmission
counterexample: Consider the celebration of Christmas in England. The practice has been transmitted across many generations and is widely regarded as a time-honoured inheritance. Yet the Christmas celebrated in the early nineteenth century — before the influence of Dickens, Prince Albert's Christmas tree, and the commercial elaboration of the holiday — bore little resemblance to the modern festival. The practice has been substantially transformed at several points, yet the participants at each stage regarded themselves as observing an ancient tradition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the fact that traditions may undergo substantial transformation while being sincerely regarded as continuous, and thus needs a condition distinguishing genuine continuity from the perception of continuity.
passage: A natural analysis of tradition holds that a practice constitutes a tradition when it has been transmitted across at least three generations, is regarded by its practitioners as an inheritance, and has remained substantially unchanged. This analysis captures clear cases: the recitation of ancestral epics in oral cultures, the observance of established rites in religious communities. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the English celebration of Christmas. The festival has been transmitted across many generations and is regarded by the English as an ancient inheritance. But the Christmas of the early nineteenth century was a very different affair from the celebration familiar to us. Before the influence of Dickens's Christmas writings, before Prince Albert introduced the decorated fir tree from Germany, before the commercial elaboration of gift-giving, Christmas was a relatively modest occasion in many English households. The modern celebration has been substantially reshaped at multiple points. Yet at each stage participants sincerely regarded themselves as continuing an ancient tradition. The definition's requirement that the practice remain substantially unchanged is not met, yet the practice is indisputably called a tradition. What is missing is a distinction between genuine continuity of content and the mere perception of continuity.

definition: An account A constitutes a historical narrative if and only if (i) A arranges a sequence of events in chronological order, (ii) A attributes causal connections among the events so arranged, and (iii) A is based upon evidence drawn from primary sources.
conditions: A arranges events in chronological order
A attributes causal connections among the events
A is based on evidence from primary sources
counterexample: Consider a chronicle kept by a medieval monk who recorded events year by year — harvests, eclipses, deaths of kings, outbreaks of plague — in strict chronological order, occasionally noting that one event caused another, and drawing throughout upon his own direct observation and the monastery's records. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet historians commonly distinguish between a chronicle and a narrative: the chronicle merely lists events in sequence, while a narrative weaves them into a unified story with a beginning, a development, and a resolution.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the account possess narrative structure — a thematic unity, a sense of beginning, development, and resolution — that distinguishes genuine narrative from a mere chronologically ordered list of causally connected events.
passage: One might propose that a historical narrative is an account that arranges events chronologically, attributes causal connections among them, and draws upon primary source evidence. This definition appears to capture the essentials of narrative historical writing: it insists on temporal order, on explanatory ambition, and on evidential grounding. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the medieval chronicle. Consider a monk in an English monastery of the twelfth century who keeps a year-by-year record of events: the harvest of 1135, the death of King Henry, the outbreak of civil war, the plague that followed. He arranges these events in strict chronological sequence. He occasionally notes causal connections — that the king's death caused the civil war, or that the war brought famine. And his account is based upon primary evidence — his own observation and the monastery's documentary records. All three conditions are met. Yet historians have long distinguished between the chronicle and the narrative proper. The chronicle is a list; the narrative is a story. What the definition overlooks is the requirement of narrative structure — a thematic unity that binds the events into a coherent whole with a beginning, a development, and a resolution, rather than a mere succession of dated entries.

definition: A historical interpretation I is anachronistic if and only if (i) I applies a concept or category to a past society that did not possess that concept, (ii) the concept applied belongs to a later historical period, and (iii) the application distorts the meaning of the past society's practices or beliefs.
conditions: I applies a concept to a past society that did not possess it
The concept belongs to a later historical period
The application distorts the meaning of the past society's practices or beliefs
counterexample: Consider the description of Athenian democracy as a political system. The Athenians themselves used the word demokratia, and the concept of rule by the people was their own. The concept does not belong to a later period — it was coined in the fifth century B.C. And the application does not distort their practices. Yet modern historians who describe Athenian democracy as a form of 'direct democracy' are arguably applying a distinction — between direct and representative democracy — that the Athenians did not make and that belongs to the eighteenth century. The third condition is met in a subtle way, even though the first two appear not to be.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that anachronism can arise not only from importing wholly foreign concepts but also from imposing later distinctions or refinements upon concepts that the past society possessed in a different and less differentiated form.
passage: One might define anachronism in historical interpretation as the application to a past society of a concept it did not possess, drawn from a later period, in a manner that distorts the meaning of the past society's practices. This definition captures straightforward cases of anachronism — describing ancient Roman senators as 'capitalists,' for instance, or attributing to medieval peasants a concept of 'human rights.' Yet there is a subtler case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. When modern historians describe Athenian politics as 'direct democracy,' they employ a term that the Athenians themselves would partly have recognised. The Athenians coined the word demokratia and understood their system as rule by the people. In that sense, the concept is not foreign to them. But the modifier 'direct' — as opposed to 'representative' — introduces a distinction that belongs to the political theory of the eighteenth century. The Athenians did not distinguish between direct and representative democracy because they had no conception of the latter. To call their system 'direct democracy' is to impose a later refinement upon a concept they possessed in a less differentiated form, and this imposition subtly distorts our understanding of how they conceived their own institutions. The definition does not accommodate this variety of anachronism, in which the concept is partly native but the specific articulation is not.

definition: An individual X leaves a legacy with respect to a domain D if and only if (i) X's actions or works in D produced effects that persisted after X's death, (ii) these effects are recognised by subsequent practitioners in D as originating from X, and (iii) the effects are regarded as positive contributions to D.
conditions: X's actions or works produced effects persisting after X's death
These effects are recognised by subsequent practitioners as originating from X
The effects are regarded as positive contributions
counterexample: Consider the legacy of Alfred Nobel. Nobel invented dynamite, which had vast and enduring effects. Subsequent generations recognised these effects as originating from Nobel. Yet the effects were deeply ambiguous — dynamite enabled both civil engineering projects and unprecedented destruction in warfare. Nobel himself was troubled by this, which motivated his establishment of the Nobel Prizes. The third condition — that the effects be regarded as positive — is not clearly satisfied, yet it seems wrong to say Nobel left no legacy.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate legacies that are mixed or contested in their evaluation, requiring instead a condition that allows for effects that are significant and enduring regardless of whether they are uniformly regarded as positive.
passage: A natural analysis of legacy holds that an individual leaves a legacy when the effects of his actions persist after his death, are recognised as originating from him, and are regarded as positive contributions. This analysis captures clear cases: the legacy of a great composer whose works continue to be performed and admired, or that of a statesman whose institutions endure and are celebrated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by Alfred Nobel. Nobel's invention of dynamite produced effects of extraordinary persistence and scope. These effects are universally recognised as originating from his work. But the evaluation of these effects is deeply divided. Dynamite enabled the construction of railways, tunnels, and canals; it also enabled destruction on a scale previously unimaginable, a fact of which Nobel himself was acutely aware. His establishment of the Nobel Prizes was motivated in part by the desire to leave a different sort of legacy. The definition requires that the effects be regarded as positive, but Nobel's effects are irreducibly mixed. If we insist on the third condition, we must either deny that Nobel left a legacy — which is absurd — or split his legacy artificially into a positive part and a non-legacy part. A more adequate definition would require only that the effects be significant and enduring, without insisting upon a uniformly positive evaluation.

definition: An episode R constitutes a revolution in a society S if and only if (i) R involves the sudden and forcible overthrow of the existing political authority, (ii) R results in a fundamental restructuring of political institutions, and (iii) R is carried out by a broad-based popular movement.
conditions: R involves sudden and forcible overthrow of existing political authority
R results in fundamental restructuring of political institutions
R is carried out by a broad-based popular movement
counterexample: Consider the Glorious Revolution of 1688 in England. William of Orange landed with an army, James II fled, and a new constitutional settlement was established — the Bill of Rights, the limitation of royal prerogative, the supremacy of Parliament. The episode resulted in a fundamental restructuring of political institutions. Yet the overthrow of James was accomplished with remarkably little violence, and the movement was not broadly popular in the usual sense — it was engineered by a faction of the political elite who invited William to intervene.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for revolutions that are carried out by elite factions rather than popular movements, and that proceed through negotiation or invitation rather than through sustained violent conflict.
passage: One might define a revolution as an episode involving the sudden, forcible overthrow of political authority, resulting in fundamental institutional restructuring, and carried out by a broad-based popular movement. This definition captures paradigmatic cases — the French Revolution of 1789, for instance, involved violent overthrow, radical institutional transformation, and popular mobilisation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the Glorious Revolution of 1688. The episode is universally called a revolution and is regarded as one of the most consequential political transformations in English history. William of Orange landed in England; James II lost his nerve and fled; Parliament offered the crown to William and Mary and enacted the Bill of Rights, fundamentally restructuring the relationship between Crown and Parliament. The second condition is clearly satisfied. But the overthrow was accomplished with minimal violence — no great battle was fought, no siege was mounted — and the enterprise was not a popular uprising but a carefully coordinated action by a faction of the political elite who had invited William's intervention. If the definition requires broad popular participation and forcible overthrow, the Glorious Revolution fails to qualify, which sits ill with the universal judgement that it was indeed a revolution.

definition: A society S has made progress over a period if and only if (i) S has achieved measurable improvement in the material conditions of life for its members, (ii) the improvement was brought about through deliberate collective action rather than accident, and (iii) the improvement is such that no reasonable person, fully informed of the circumstances, would wish to reverse it.
conditions: S has achieved measurable improvement in material conditions
The improvement was brought about through deliberate collective action
No reasonable person would wish to reverse the improvement
counterexample: Consider the dramatic increase in agricultural productivity brought about by the introduction of high-yield crop varieties during the Green Revolution of the 1960s and 1970s. Material conditions improved measurably — famine was averted in several countries. The improvement resulted from deliberate collective action by governments and research institutions. No reasonable person would wish to see famine return. Yet the Green Revolution also introduced dependence on chemical fertilisers and pesticides, reduced crop genetic diversity, displaced traditional farming practices, and concentrated agricultural profits in the hands of large landowners. The improvement was genuine but was accompanied by costs that many reasonable persons would wish to have avoided, complicating the judgement of progress.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the improvement not be accompanied by significant and foreseeable costs in other dimensions of social or ecological well-being that a fully informed reasonable person would regard as grounds for hesitation.
passage: One might propose that a society has made progress when it achieves measurable improvement in material conditions through deliberate collective action, where the improvement is such that no reasonable person would wish it reversed. This definition has several virtues: it insists upon measurability, which guards against vague and self-congratulatory narratives; it requires deliberate action, which distinguishes progress from mere good fortune; and the reasonable-person test provides a standard of judgement that transcends individual preferences. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the Green Revolution. In the 1960s and 1970s, the introduction of high-yield crop varieties dramatically increased agricultural output in countries that had been threatened by famine. The improvement in material conditions was measurable and vast — millions were fed who might otherwise have starved. The improvement was brought about through the deliberate efforts of governments, international organisations, and research institutions. And no reasonable person, confronted with the alternative of mass famine, would wish the improvement reversed. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the Green Revolution also introduced heavy dependence on chemical fertilisers and pesticides, with attendant environmental degradation. It reduced the genetic diversity of food crops, creating vulnerability to future blight. It displaced traditional agricultural practices that had sustained communities for centuries. It concentrated profits in the hands of large landowners, deepening rural inequality. One might observe that the definition permits a judgement of progress even when the improvement is purchased at costs that a reasonable and fully informed person would regard as deeply troubling. The definition captures the improvement but not the price.

definition: A narrative N provides a historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) N identifies the antecedent conditions from which E arose, (ii) N shows that E followed from those conditions in accordance with generalisations about human behaviour, and (iii) N is supported by documentary evidence.
conditions: N identifies antecedent conditions from which E arose
N shows E followed from those conditions in accordance with generalisations about human behaviour
N is supported by documentary evidence
counterexample: Consider the explanation of the fall of Constantinople in 1453. One might identify the antecedent conditions — the weakening of Byzantine defences, the ambition of Mehmed II, the development of large-bore cannon — and show that these conditions made the fall probable given generalisations about military conflict. Documentary evidence supports the narrative. Yet the same antecedent conditions obtained during earlier Ottoman sieges of Constantinople that failed. The explanation does not account for why the city fell in 1453 and not before, unless it includes the particular and contingent details — the overlooked postern gate, the wounding of Giustiniani, the specific tactical decisions of the final assault — that resist subsumption under general laws of human behaviour.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the explanation account for the particular and contingent details that distinguish the actual outcome from other outcomes that the same general conditions might equally have produced.
passage: One might propose that a historical explanation identifies the antecedent conditions from which an event arose, shows that the event followed from those conditions in accordance with generalisations about human behaviour, and is supported by documentary evidence. This is a respectable and widely held view. It assimilates historical explanation to the covering-law model familiar from the philosophy of science, requiring that the event be shown to follow from prior conditions via general laws — or at least generalisations — about how human beings act under certain circumstances. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the fall of Constantinople in 1453. The antecedent conditions are well documented: the long decline of Byzantine military and economic power, the territorial expansion of the Ottoman Empire, the ambition and ability of Sultan Mehmed II, and the development of siege artillery capable of breaching the Theodosian walls. One might invoke generalisations about the tendency of weakened states to fall before determined aggressors. The documentary evidence is ample. But these same conditions — a weakened Byzantium confronted by an ambitious Ottoman sultan — had obtained during several earlier sieges that failed. The generalisations about military conflict do not distinguish 1453 from 1422 or 1396. What made the difference were contingent particulars: the postern gate left unbolted, the wounding of the Genoese commander Giustiniani at a critical moment, the specific sequence of tactical decisions during the final assault. These particulars resist subsumption under generalisations about human behaviour. They are the very stuff of historical explanation, and the definition, by requiring only generalisations, cannot accommodate them.

definition: A division of history into periods is well-founded if and only if (i) the events chosen as boundaries mark genuine discontinuities in the character of social life, (ii) each period possesses a distinctive character that differentiates it from its neighbours, and (iii) the periodisation is applicable across the geographical region under consideration.
conditions: Boundary events mark genuine discontinuities in social life
Each period possesses a distinctive differentiating character
The periodisation is applicable across the geographical region
counterexample: Consider the conventional periodisation of European history into 'medieval' and 'modern' periods, with the boundary set around 1500. This date corresponds to events of genuine significance — the voyages of discovery, the Reformation, the development of printing. The medieval period possesses a distinctive character (feudalism, Christendom, scholasticism) and the modern period another (nation-states, capitalism, empirical science). Yet the periodisation is not uniformly applicable across Europe: while Italy was already exhibiting 'modern' characteristics in the fourteenth century, Russia retained features conventionally described as 'medieval' well into the eighteenth. The third condition is violated, yet the medieval-modern division remains the standard framework of European historiography.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for periodisations that are valuable precisely because they identify broad tendencies rather than uniform transformations, and that may apply unevenly across regions without thereby being ill-founded.
passage: One might propose that a periodisation of history is well-founded when its boundaries mark genuine discontinuities, each period possesses a distinctive character, and the division applies across the geographical region in question. These seem sensible requirements: a periodisation that marks no real change, that groups together epochs of radically different character, or that applies to one country but not its neighbours would rightly be suspect. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of these requirements is the standard division of European history into medieval and modern periods. The boundary around 1500 corresponds to events of undeniable significance: the voyages of Columbus and da Gama, the beginning of the Reformation, the spread of printing. The medieval period has a recognisable character — feudal social organisation, the dominance of the Church, scholastic philosophy — and the modern period has another — the rise of the nation-state, capitalist economic relations, empirical science. But the third condition presents a difficulty. Italy was exhibiting features conventionally described as 'modern' — commercial capitalism, secular art, humanist scholarship — from the fourteenth century onward, well before 1500. Russia, by contrast, retained feudal land tenure, the dominance of the Church, and an autocratic political structure deep into the eighteenth century. The periodisation does not apply uniformly across Europe. Yet historians would not for that reason abandon it; it captures a broad tendency, an approximate and uneven transformation, and its value lies precisely in its capacity to identify the general direction of change even when particular regions are ahead or behind. The definition demands geographical uniformity where historical reality supplies only uneven approximation.

definition: A human community C constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) C has developed a system of writing, (ii) C has constructed monumental architecture, and (iii) C maintains a complex division of labour involving specialised occupations.
conditions: C has developed a system of writing
C has constructed monumental architecture
C maintains a complex division of labour with specialised occupations
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire at its height in the fifteenth century. The Inca maintained a vast and sophisticated administrative apparatus, constructed monumental architecture of extraordinary quality — Sacsayhuaman, Machu Picchu — and sustained a complex division of labour. Yet the Inca did not develop a system of writing in the conventional sense; their information was recorded through quipu, a system of knotted strings that encoded numerical and possibly narrative data but is not a writing system in the usual understanding. Condition (i) is not met, yet no historian would deny that the Inca possessed a civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition mistakes a culturally specific criterion — alphabetic or logographic writing — for a universal requirement, failing to recognise that other systems of information recording may serve the same organisational functions.
passage: It is often proposed that a civilisation is distinguished by the possession of writing, monumental architecture, and a complex division of labour. These criteria are abstracted from the familiar cases — Mesopotamia, Egypt, China, the Indus Valley — where all three features are conspicuously present. The proposal has the merit of specificity: it names concrete, identifiable markers rather than relying upon vague notions of cultural achievement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this set of criteria is provided by the Inca Empire. At its height in the fifteenth century, the Inca state administered a territory stretching along the Andes for thousands of miles. Its architecture was monumental and sophisticated — the fortress of Sacsayhuaman, the citadel of Machu Picchu, the road system that traversed the empire. Its division of labour was complex, encompassing administrative officials, agricultural specialists, metallurgists, textile workers, and soldiers. Yet the Inca did not possess a system of writing in the conventional sense. They employed the quipu — an arrangement of knotted strings that encoded numerical information and, possibly, narrative content — but this is not a writing system as the term is ordinarily understood. The first condition of the definition is not satisfied. To conclude from this that the Inca did not constitute a civilisation would be absurd. One might observe that the definition has been constructed from a set of cases that happen to share the feature of writing, and has mistaken a contingent common property for a necessary condition. The quipu served many of the organisational functions that writing serves elsewhere, and its exclusion from the category of 'writing' reflects a culturally specific understanding of that term.

definition: A civilisation C is in decline if and only if (i) C's political institutions are losing their capacity to maintain order, (ii) C's economic productivity is diminishing, and (iii) C's population is decreasing or stagnating.
conditions: Political institutions are losing their capacity to maintain order
Economic productivity is diminishing
Population is decreasing or stagnating
counterexample: Consider the late Roman Republic in the first century B.C. Political institutions were manifestly losing their capacity to maintain order — civil wars, proscriptions, and the erosion of constitutional norms were endemic. Yet economic productivity was increasing: the Mediterranean trade network was expanding, agricultural estates were growing more productive, and Roman engineering was reaching new heights. The population was growing. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not met, yet the Republic was in a state of political decline so severe that it would shortly be replaced by a fundamentally different form of government.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that decline may occur in one dimension of social life — political, economic, cultural, or demographic — independently of the others, and that a civilisation may be in genuine decline even when only one dimension is deteriorating.
passage: One might propose that a civilisation is in decline when its political institutions lose the capacity to maintain order, its economic productivity diminishes, and its population decreases or stagnates. This definition has the virtue of specificity: it names measurable indicators and avoids the vagueness of purely qualitative judgements. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the late Roman Republic. In the century before the establishment of the Principate, Rome's political institutions were in manifest disarray. The Senate's authority was flouted by military commanders, civil wars erupted repeatedly, constitutional norms were violated with impunity, and proscriptions turned political disagreement into a matter of life and death. By any reasonable assessment, the political institutions were losing their capacity to maintain order. Yet the Roman economy was flourishing. The conquest of the eastern Mediterranean had brought enormous wealth to Rome; trade networks spanned the entire sea; agricultural productivity on the great latifundia was increasing; and Roman engineering — aqueducts, roads, harbours — was advancing to unprecedented levels of sophistication. The population was growing, not declining. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are clearly not satisfied. Yet the political decline of the Republic was real and consequential — it would lead, within decades, to the end of republican government and the establishment of autocratic rule. One might observe that decline need not be uniform across all dimensions of social life, and that a civilisation may undergo genuine and severe decline in one domain while flourishing in others. The definition, by requiring that all three indicators move in concert, cannot recognise partial or asymmetric decline.

definition: A practice or belief P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a significant period, (ii) P is regarded by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past, and (iii) P has remained substantially unchanged through the period of its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted across generations over a significant period
P is regarded by practitioners as inherited from the past
P has remained substantially unchanged through its transmission
counterexample: Consider the tradition of the British monarchy. The institution has been transmitted across generations for over a thousand years, and it is regarded by its participants as having been inherited from the deep past. Yet the practice has changed enormously: the medieval monarchy bore little resemblance to the constitutional monarchy of the nineteenth century, which in turn differs from the ceremonial monarchy of today. The powers, functions, and social meaning of the institution have been continuously transformed. Condition (iii) is not met, yet few would deny that the British monarchy constitutes one of the most venerable traditions in European history.
missing_condition: The definition mistakes static persistence for the essence of tradition, failing to recognise that traditions characteristically evolve and are reinterpreted by each generation while maintaining a continuity of identity that does not require substantive unchangedness.
passage: One might propose that a tradition is a practice or belief transmitted across generations, regarded by its practitioners as inherited from the past, and substantially unchanged through the period of its transmission. The first two conditions seem uncontroversial: a tradition must have temporal depth and must be experienced by its participants as something received rather than invented. The third condition — substantive unchangedness — appears to capture the conservative character that distinguishes tradition from mere fashion or innovation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the British monarchy. The institution has been transmitted across generations for well over a millennium and is regarded by all concerned as having been inherited from the past. But it has changed profoundly. The Anglo-Saxon monarchy was an elected kingship tempered by the counsel of the Witan. The medieval monarchy was a feudal institution whose power rested on the king's personal lands and his relationship with his barons. The Tudor monarchy was an absolutist enterprise. The constitutional monarchy that emerged after 1688 was a partnership with Parliament. The present monarchy is largely ceremonial. The powers, functions, rituals, and social meaning of the institution have been continuously transformed. It has not remained substantially unchanged. Yet to deny that the British monarchy is a tradition would be to empty the word of its most natural application. One might observe that traditions characteristically evolve — that each generation reinterprets, adapts, and transforms what it has inherited, and that this process of creative reinterpretation is itself part of what makes a tradition a living practice rather than a dead relic.

definition: An account A constitutes a historical narrative if and only if (i) A presents events in temporal sequence, (ii) A connects the events by relations of cause and consequence, and (iii) A is based upon primary sources that provide evidence for the events described.
conditions: A presents events in temporal sequence
A connects events by cause and consequence
A is based upon primary sources
counterexample: Consider Fernand Braudel's account of the Mediterranean world in the age of Philip II. Braudel's work is universally regarded as one of the masterpieces of historical narrative. Yet large portions of it do not present events in temporal sequence at all; the first part of the work describes the nearly unchanging geographical and climatic conditions of the Mediterranean basin, treating them not as a sequence of events but as a quasi-permanent structure within which events occur. The connections are not primarily causal in the event-to-event sense but structural — geography shapes economy, economy shapes society. The definition's first condition is violated in substantial portions of the work, yet the work is paradigmatically historical narrative.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate narratives that operate at different temporal scales, including those that describe enduring structures and conditions rather than sequences of events, and that connect phenomena by structural rather than event-to-event causal relations.
passage: One might propose that a historical narrative presents events in temporal sequence, connects them by relations of cause and consequence, and is based upon primary sources. This definition captures the familiar model of narrative history in which one event leads to another in a chain of causes and effects — the assassination leads to the war, the war leads to the treaty, the treaty leads to the peace. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this model is Fernand Braudel's great work on the Mediterranean in the age of Philip II. Braudel's study is universally recognised as a masterpiece of historical writing. But its first and longest section does not present events in temporal sequence at all. It describes the geography of the Mediterranean — the mountains, the plains, the sea routes, the climate — as a set of enduring conditions that change only over centuries or millennia. These conditions are not events; they do not succeed one another in a temporal sequence. The connections Braudel draws are structural rather than causal in the event-to-event sense: the geography shapes the patterns of trade, the patterns of trade shape the distribution of wealth, the distribution of wealth shapes the character of political power. The definition, by insisting upon temporal sequence and event-to-event causation, cannot accommodate this mode of historical writing. One might observe that the most ambitious historical narratives characteristically operate at multiple temporal scales, combining the narration of events with the analysis of enduring structures, and that a definition which admits only the former is too narrow for the full range of historical practice.

definition: A statement S about a past period is anachronistic if and only if (i) S attributes to agents in the past a concept, practice, or artefact that did not exist in their time, (ii) the attribution distorts the understanding of the agents' actions or circumstances, and (iii) the attribution results from the historian's failure to attend to the differences between past and present.
conditions: S attributes to past agents a concept, practice, or artefact not available in their time
The attribution distorts understanding of the agents' actions
The attribution results from the historian's failure to attend to past-present differences
counterexample: Consider a historian who describes the Roman road system as an 'infrastructure project.' The concept of infrastructure, as a category of public investment subject to cost-benefit analysis, did not exist in Roman thought; the roads were built for military conquest and administrative control. The attribution of the modern concept arguably distorts understanding by suggesting an economic rationality that was not operative. Yet the same historian may use the term deliberately and self-consciously, fully aware that it is an anachronism, employing it as a heuristic device to make Roman practice intelligible to a modern audience. Condition (iii) — that the attribution results from the historian's failure to attend to differences — is not met, yet the statement is, in one clear sense, anachronistic.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between inadvertent anachronism, which is a genuine historiographical error, and deliberate or heuristic anachronism, which may be a legitimate and self-conscious tool of historical explanation.
passage: One might propose that a statement about the past is anachronistic when it attributes to past agents a concept or practice unavailable in their time, thereby distorting understanding, as a result of the historian's failure to recognise the differences between past and present. This definition captures the core of what is objectionable about careless anachronism: the historian projects present-day categories onto the past without recognising that they do not fit. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns the deliberate use of anachronistic language by careful historians. When a historian describes the Roman road system as an 'infrastructure project,' the term is anachronistic — the Romans had no concept of infrastructure as a category of public investment. The attribution arguably distorts understanding by importing connotations of economic planning that were alien to Roman practice. But the historian may employ the term in full awareness of its anachronistic character, using it as a heuristic device to render Roman practice intelligible to a modern reader. She is not failing to attend to the differences between past and present; she is exploiting a modern concept precisely in order to highlight those differences. The definition treats all anachronism as error — as the result of the historian's inattention. But the deliberate and self-conscious use of anachronistic categories is a recognised and sometimes indispensable tool of historical exposition. One should hesitate to classify it alongside the careless attribution that the definition is designed to censure.

definition: A historical figure X leaves a legacy in a domain D if and only if (i) X's actions or ideas produce lasting effects in D, (ii) the effects are attributable to X rather than to other agents or broader structural forces, and (iii) the effects are regarded by subsequent generations as significant.
conditions: X's actions or ideas produce lasting effects in D
The effects are attributable to X rather than to other agents or structural forces
Subsequent generations regard the effects as significant
counterexample: Consider the legacy of Christopher Columbus. His voyages produced lasting effects of enormous magnitude — the Columbian Exchange, the colonisation of the Americas, the transformation of global demographics. Subsequent generations have regarded these effects as among the most significant in human history. Yet the effects are not clearly attributable to Columbus rather than to broader structural forces: the technological and economic conditions that made transatlantic voyaging possible would almost certainly have produced the same result, through some other explorer, within a few years. Condition (ii) is doubtful, yet Columbus is universally credited with a legacy of world-historical importance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that a figure may leave a legacy by being the proximate or catalysing agent of changes that broader structural forces made all but inevitable, without the effects being attributable to that figure in the strong sense the definition requires.
passage: One might propose that a historical figure leaves a legacy when his or her actions produce lasting effects in a domain, those effects are attributable to the figure rather than to other agents or structural forces, and subsequent generations regard them as significant. This seems a reasonable set of requirements: it insists that the effects be real, that they be traceable to the individual, and that they matter. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of these requirements is the legacy of Columbus. The lasting effects of his voyages are beyond dispute: the encounter between the Old World and the New transformed demographics, ecology, economics, and politics on a global scale. Subsequent generations have universally regarded these effects as among the most consequential in human history. But the second condition is problematic. The maritime technology, the commercial ambitions, and the geopolitical pressures that produced the voyages of discovery were not unique to Columbus. Portugal and Spain were both actively seeking westward and southward routes; Cabot, the Cabral expedition, and numerous other ventures followed hard upon Columbus's heels. It is difficult to argue that the effects — the colonisation of the Americas, the Columbian Exchange — are attributable to Columbus rather than to the structural forces that made transatlantic exploration all but inevitable. Had Columbus not sailed in 1492, someone else would have sailed within a very few years, and the lasting effects would have been substantially the same. Yet Columbus is credited with the legacy. The definition, by requiring individual attributability, cannot accommodate cases in which a figure's legacy consists in being the proximate agent of changes that broader forces had rendered virtually certain.

definition: An event or series of events R constitutes a revolution if and only if (i) R results in a fundamental change in the political order, (ii) R involves the overthrow of the existing governing authority by force or the credible threat of force, and (iii) R is compressed into a relatively brief period.
conditions: R results in a fundamental change in the political order
R involves the overthrow of the existing authority by force or threat of force
R is compressed into a relatively brief period
counterexample: Consider the Industrial Revolution in Britain. It resulted in a fundamental transformation of the economic, social, and ultimately political order — the shift from an agrarian to an industrial economy, the rise of the factory system, the growth of an urban working class, and the eventual extension of the franchise. Yet it did not involve the overthrow of the existing governing authority by force, and it was not compressed into a brief period; it unfolded over roughly a century. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not met, yet the event is universally described as a revolution and is regarded as one of the most consequential transformations in human history.
missing_condition: The definition is constructed from the model of political revolution and cannot accommodate transformations that are equally fundamental but economic, social, or technological in character, gradual in pace, and accomplished without violence.
passage: One might propose that a revolution is an event resulting in fundamental change in the political order, involving the overthrow of the existing governing authority by force, and compressed into a relatively brief period. This definition accommodates the French Revolution of 1789, the Russian Revolution of 1917, and many other paradigmatic cases with considerable precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the Industrial Revolution. The transformation of Britain from an agrarian to an industrial economy, beginning in the mid-eighteenth century and continuing well into the nineteenth, was a revolution by any measure of its consequences. It reshaped the political order — though slowly and without a single dramatic overthrow — by creating new classes, new forms of wealth, and new centres of political power. The Reform Act of 1832, the Chartist movement, and the eventual extension of the franchise to the working class were political consequences of economic transformation. But the Industrial Revolution did not involve the overthrow of the existing authority by force. The Hanoverian monarchy and the parliamentary system survived intact throughout the period. And it was not compressed into a brief period; the transformation unfolded over roughly a century. Two of the three conditions are unmet. Yet no one would deny that the Industrial Revolution was a revolution. The definition, built from the template of violent political upheaval, cannot accommodate the slower, more diffuse, but no less profound transformations that reshape human life from the ground up.

definition: An account N constitutes an adequate historical narrative of a sequence of events if and only if (i) N presents the events in their temporal order, (ii) N connects the events by relations of cause and effect, and (iii) N is based upon evidence drawn from primary sources.
conditions: N presents the events in their temporal order
N connects the events by relations of cause and effect
N is based upon evidence drawn from primary sources
counterexample: Consider a chronicle of the Black Death in fourteenth-century England that presents the events in exact temporal order, connects them by causal relations — the arrival of infected rats, the spread of plague through crowded towns, the collapse of labour supply — and bases every claim upon contemporary parish records and court rolls. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the narrative, by attending only to cause and effect, omits the significance of the events for those who lived through them: the spiritual terror, the breakdown of feudal obligations, the transformation of the survivors' understanding of their world.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the narrative convey the meaning or significance of the events for the historical agents, beyond their mere causal connections.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an adequate historical narrative presents events in their temporal order, connects them by relations of cause and effect, and is grounded in primary-source evidence. This account captures the historian's concern with chronology, causation, and evidential rigour. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers a chronicle of the Black Death in fourteenth-century England. Such a chronicle might present the events in precise temporal order: the arrival of plague-bearing ships at Melcombe Regis in 1348, the westward and northward spread of contagion, the peak of mortality in the summer months, the gradual abatement by 1350. It might connect these events by causal relations — the migration of infected rats, the role of crowded living conditions, the consequent collapse of the labour supply. And it might base every assertion upon contemporary parish records, court rolls, and episcopal registers. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that such a narrative, for all its causal completeness, fails to convey what the plague meant to those who endured it. The spiritual terror of a population convinced that God had turned against them, the dissolution of feudal bonds when lords could no longer command obedience, the altered understanding of mortality and divine justice — these dimensions of the event are not captured by causal explanation alone.

definition: A division of the past into periods is historically legitimate if and only if (i) each period is characterised by a distinctive set of social, political, or cultural features, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to genuine discontinuities in the historical record, and (iii) the periodisation is recognised as valid by the consensus of competent historians.
conditions: Each period is characterised by a distinctive set of social, political, or cultural features
The boundaries between periods correspond to genuine discontinuities in the historical record
The periodisation is recognised as valid by the consensus of competent historians
counterexample: Consider the conventional division between the Medieval and Renaissance periods in European history, typically placed around 1400. Each period is characterised by distinctive cultural features — scholasticism and Gothic architecture on one side, humanism and classical revival on the other. Historians have long accepted this division. Yet research has revealed that many so-called Renaissance developments had deep medieval roots: the revival of classical learning began in the twelfth century, and Gothic artistic forms persisted well into the fifteenth. The boundary corresponds not to a genuine discontinuity but to a gradual and uneven transition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the boundaries between periods represent abrupt rather than gradual transitions, leaving it unable to distinguish genuine historical discontinuities from boundaries imposed upon continuous processes by historiographical convention.
passage: One might define a legitimate periodisation of the past as a division in which each period is characterised by distinctive features, the boundaries correspond to genuine discontinuities, and the division commands the consensus of historians. This definition captures the desiderata that periodisation ought to reflect real changes rather than arbitrary divisions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the conventional boundary between the Medieval and Renaissance periods. The two periods are characterised by strikingly different cultural features — the scholastic philosophy, feudal social organisation, and Gothic art of the Middle Ages contrast sharply with the humanism, commercial society, and classical revival of the Renaissance. The periodisation has been accepted by generations of historians. Yet when one examines the boundary itself, one finds not a sharp discontinuity but a protracted and uneven transition. The recovery of classical texts had begun by the twelfth century; Gothic forms persisted in northern Europe well into the fifteenth. The so-called Renaissance did not spring into being at any determinate date. One might observe that the definition, by requiring genuine discontinuities without specifying what makes a discontinuity genuine, permits boundaries that are artefacts of historiographical convention rather than reflections of the historical process.

definition: A civilisation C has made progress over a period if and only if (i) C has achieved measurable improvements in material conditions of life, (ii) C has expanded the scope of individual liberty, and (iii) C has advanced in the production and dissemination of systematic learning.
conditions: C has achieved measurable improvements in material conditions of life
C has expanded the scope of individual liberty
C has advanced in the production and dissemination of systematic learning
counterexample: Consider the Roman Empire between the first century B.C. and the second century A.D. Material conditions improved markedly — roads, aqueducts, public baths, and a stable currency raised the standard of living across the Mediterranean. Systematic learning advanced through the work of Galen, Ptolemy, and the great jurists. Yet the expansion of empire was accompanied by the entrenchment of slavery, the suppression of republican liberties, and the concentration of power in the hands of the emperor. The scope of individual liberty, far from expanding, contracted for large segments of the population.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the three forms of progress advance together, leaving open the possibility that improvements in material conditions and learning are purchased at the cost of liberty, which one should hesitate to call genuine civilisational progress.
passage: One might define civilisational progress as the conjunction of measurable improvement in material conditions, expansion of individual liberty, and advancement in systematic learning. This definition has the merit of combining the material, political, and intellectual dimensions of human flourishing. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the Roman Empire during the first two centuries of the common era. Material conditions improved dramatically: the extensive road network, the provision of clean water through aqueducts, the stability of the currency, and the security of commerce across the Mediterranean raised the standard of living to levels not seen again in Europe for a thousand years. Systematic learning advanced as well — Galen systematised medicine, Ptolemy codified astronomy, and the Roman jurists developed a body of law of extraordinary sophistication. Yet these achievements coincided with the progressive erosion of republican liberties, the entrenchment of chattel slavery as the foundation of the economy, and the concentration of arbitrary power in the person of the emperor. One might observe that a definition which permits us to call such a civilisation progressive, when its material and intellectual achievements were purchased at the cost of liberty for millions, has failed to capture what we mean by genuine progress.

definition: An account H constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) H identifies a set of antecedent conditions sufficient to bring about E, (ii) H subsumes E under a general law or regularity of human behaviour, and (iii) H is supported by documentary evidence.
conditions: H identifies a set of antecedent conditions sufficient to bring about E
H subsumes E under a general law or regularity of human behaviour
H is supported by documentary evidence
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the French Revolution that cites antecedent conditions — fiscal crisis, harvest failure, aristocratic intransigence — subsumes the event under a general regularity about revolutions occurring when rising expectations are frustrated, and supports every claim with documentary evidence. All three conditions are met. Yet the explanation fails to capture the contingent role of particular agents and particular decisions — Louis XVI's fatal hesitation, Necker's dismissal, the storming of the Bastille on a specific day when events might easily have gone otherwise. The covering-law model imposes a deterministic structure upon events whose course depended upon individual choices that no general law could have predicted.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the role of contingency and individual agency in historical events, requiring subsumption under general laws when the event's occurrence depended upon particular decisions that were not necessitated by any regularity.
passage: A familiar account of historical explanation holds that one has explained an event when one has identified antecedent conditions sufficient to bring it about, subsumed it under a general law or regularity of human behaviour, and supported the explanation with documentary evidence. This covering-law model has the attraction of assimilating historical explanation to the pattern regarded as standard in the natural sciences. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one attempts to explain the French Revolution along these lines. One might identify the antecedent conditions — the fiscal exhaustion of the monarchy, the failure of the harvest of 1788, the refusal of the aristocracy to bear a share of the tax burden — and subsume the event under the regularity that revolutions occur when the expectations of a population, raised by a period of improvement, are abruptly frustrated. Documentary evidence for each of these claims is abundant. Yet one might observe that this explanation, for all its merits, imposes a deterministic structure upon events that were in fact radically contingent. Louis XVI might have accepted the reforms proposed by his ministers; the Bastille might not have been stormed on the fourteenth of July; the revolution might have taken a quite different course had different individuals made different choices at critical junctures. It appears that historical explanation must make room for contingency and individual agency in a way that the covering-law model does not.

definition: An event R constitutes a revolution if and only if (i) R involves the sudden and forcible overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) R is carried out by a broadly based popular movement, and (iii) R results in a fundamental transformation of the social and political structure.
conditions: R involves the sudden and forcible overthrow of an existing political order
R is carried out by a broadly based popular movement
R results in a fundamental transformation of the social and political structure
counterexample: Consider the Meiji Restoration of 1868 in Japan. It resulted in a fundamental transformation of the social and political structure — the abolition of the feudal system, the creation of a centralised state, the adoption of Western-style institutions. Yet the overthrow of the Tokugawa shogunate was carried out not by a broadly based popular movement but by a small coalition of samurai from the domains of Satsuma, Choshu, Tosa, and Hizen, acting in the name of the Emperor. The event satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but not (ii), and yet historians routinely and rightly call it a revolution.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring a broadly based popular movement, thereby excluding elite-led transformations that are nevertheless revolutionary in their scope and consequences.
passage: One might define a revolution as an event involving the sudden and forcible overthrow of an existing political order, carried out by a broadly based popular movement, and resulting in a fundamental transformation of social and political structure. This definition captures the salient features of the paradigmatic revolutions — the French Revolution of 1789, the Russian Revolution of 1917 — in which large masses of the population rose against the established order and effected a radical transformation of society. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the Meiji Restoration of 1868. The overthrow of the Tokugawa shogunate and the subsequent reconstruction of the Japanese state constituted one of the most thoroughgoing transformations in modern history: the feudal domains were abolished, a centralised bureaucratic state was created, compulsory education was introduced, and a modern army and navy were established. The transformation of social and political structure was fundamental and far-reaching. Yet the movement that brought it about was not broadly based in the popular sense. It was led by a small coalition of samurai from four domains, acting in the emperor's name and without significant participation by the peasantry or merchant classes. One might observe that a definition which excludes the Meiji Restoration from the category of revolutions has drawn its boundaries too narrowly.

definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from generation to generation over a substantial period of time, (ii) P is regarded by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past, and (iii) P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted from generation to generation over a substantial period
P is regarded by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past
P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission
counterexample: Consider the Scottish Highland tradition of wearing tartan patterns associated with particular clans. Practitioners regard this as an ancient custom inherited from their ancestors, and it has been transmitted across generations.
