Yet historical research has shown that the association of specific tartans with specific clans was largely an invention of the early nineteenth century, promoted by the Highland societies and codified for the visit of George IV to Edinburgh in 1822. The practice has not remained substantially unchanged but has been substantially created and then presented as unchanging.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the phenomenon of invented tradition, in which a practice that is presented and perceived as ancient and continuous is in fact of recent origin or has undergone radical transformation while maintaining the appearance of continuity.
passage: It is natural to define a tradition as a practice transmitted from generation to generation over a substantial period, regarded by its practitioners as inherited from the past, and substantially unchanged throughout its transmission. This definition captures the ordinary understanding of tradition as something old, continuous, and authoritative by virtue of its antiquity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the Scottish Highland tradition of clan tartans. The wearing of tartan patterns associated with particular clans is regarded by its practitioners as an ancient custom stretching back into the mists of Highland history, and it has been transmitted across several generations with apparent continuity. Yet when one examines the historical evidence, one finds that the systematic association of specific tartan patterns with specific clans dates not from time immemorial but from the early nineteenth century. The Highland societies of the Romantic era codified and in many cases invented the clan tartans, and the visit of George IV to Edinburgh in 1822 — stage-managed by Walter Scott — was a decisive moment in the crystallisation of the tradition. The practice satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but fails condition (iii), for what appears to be an ancient and unchanging custom is in fact a recent creation. One might observe that the definition, by taking the practitioners' own understanding at face value, is vulnerable to cases in which the appearance of antiquity is itself an artefact.

definition: A human society S constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) S has developed a system of writing, (ii) S has established permanent urban settlements, and (iii) S has created specialised institutions for governance, religion, and economic exchange.
conditions: S has developed a system of writing
S has established permanent urban settlements
S has created specialised institutions for governance, religion, and economic exchange
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire at its height in the fifteenth century. The Inca created an extensive road network spanning thousands of miles, built the monumental city of Cusco, established a sophisticated bureaucratic administration, maintained elaborate religious institutions, and managed a complex redistributive economy. The society possessed permanent urban settlements and specialised institutions for governance, religion, and economic exchange. Yet the Inca did not possess a system of writing in the conventional sense; they employed the quipu — a system of knotted strings — for record-keeping, which does not constitute writing as ordinarily understood. It would be perverse to deny that the Inca society constituted a civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring a system of writing, thereby excluding societies that achieved all other hallmarks of civilisational complexity through alternative systems of record-keeping and communication.
passage: One might define a civilisation as a human society that has developed a system of writing, established permanent urban settlements, and created specialised institutions for governance, religion, and economic exchange. This definition has the merit of identifying features that characterise the great civilisations of antiquity — Mesopotamia, Egypt, the Indus Valley, and China all developed writing, built cities, and created complex institutions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is furnished by the Inca Empire. At its zenith in the fifteenth century, the Inca state administered a territory stretching along the western coast of South America, connected by an elaborate road network and governed by a sophisticated bureaucracy. The capital, Cusco, was a monumental urban settlement; the institutions of governance, religion, and economic redistribution were highly specialised and complex. By any reasonable standard, the Inca achieved a level of social organisation comparable to the recognised civilisations of the Old World. Yet the Inca did not develop a system of writing in the conventional sense. Their records were kept by means of the quipu, a system of knotted strings whose full communicative capacity remains debated. One might observe that a definition which excludes the Inca from the category of civilisation on account of this single absence has mistaken one contingent feature of certain civilisations for a necessary condition of civilisation as such.

definition: A historical claim C is anachronistic if and only if (i) C attributes to a past society a concept, practice, or institution that was not available in that society, (ii) the attribution results from the historian's projection of present-day categories onto the past, and (iii) the anachronistic attribution distorts the historian's understanding of the past society.
conditions: C attributes to a past society a concept, practice, or institution not available in that society
The attribution results from the historian's projection of present-day categories onto the past
The anachronistic attribution distorts the historian's understanding of the past society
counterexample: Consider a historian who describes the Roman concept of libertas as equivalent to the modern notion of individual political freedom. The concept of libertas was indeed available in Roman society, so condition (i) is not straightforwardly met — the historian is not inventing a concept but subtly mischaracterising an existing one. Yet the modern connotations imported by the translation distort the understanding of what the Romans actually meant, which was closer to the status of a free citizen as opposed to a slave. The case involves anachronism of a more subtle kind than the definition recognises.
missing_condition: The definition fails to cover cases of semantic anachronism, in which the historian employs a term that was available in the past society but imports into it meanings, connotations, or associations that belong to the present, thereby distorting the historical concept without inventing one ex nihilo.
passage: One might define an anachronistic historical claim as one that attributes to a past society a concept, practice, or institution unavailable in that society, resulting from the projection of present-day categories, and distorting the historian's understanding. This definition captures clear cases of anachronism — it would be anachronistic, for instance, to attribute a concept of human rights to the ancient Spartans. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis involves the translation of Roman political vocabulary. A historian who describes the Roman concept of libertas as equivalent to modern individual freedom does not, strictly speaking, attribute to the Romans a concept unavailable in their society. The Romans did indeed possess the concept of libertas and employed the term extensively. Yet the modern connotations that the English word freedom carries — the Enlightenment notion of the individual's inherent right to self-determination — were not part of what the Romans meant. Libertas designated primarily the status of the free citizen as opposed to the slave, and the liberty of the Roman citizen was inseparable from his membership in the political community. One might observe that the historian has committed a subtle form of anachronism: not the attribution of a wholly absent concept but the importation of modern semantic content into an ancient term. The definition, by requiring that the concept be entirely unavailable, fails to address this pervasive and insidious form of historical distortion.

definition: A past event or movement E has left a legacy L in a subsequent society S if and only if (i) there exists a demonstrable causal chain connecting E to L, (ii) L is recognised by members of S as having originated in E, and (iii) L continues to shape the practices or institutions of S.
conditions: There exists a demonstrable causal chain connecting E to L
L is recognised by members of S as having originated in E
L continues to shape the practices or institutions of S
counterexample: Consider the legacy of Roman law in modern European legal systems. There is a demonstrable causal chain connecting Roman jurisprudence to the civil-law tradition. Roman law continues to shape legal practice — the structure of the Napoleonic Code is deeply indebted to the Digest of Justinian. Yet most practising lawyers and citizens who operate within these legal systems do not recognise their practices as having originated in Roman law; they simply regard the law as the law. Condition (ii) fails despite the reality of the causal connection and the continuing influence.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring conscious recognition of the legacy's origin, thereby excluding cases in which a past event shapes present practices through causal channels that operate below the threshold of historical awareness.
passage: One might propose that a past event has left a legacy in a subsequent society if there exists a demonstrable causal chain connecting the two, if the legacy is recognised by members of the subsequent society as having originated in the past event, and if the legacy continues to shape the practices or institutions of the society. This proposal has the merit of insisting upon both causal connection and ongoing influence. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the legacy of Roman law in modern continental Europe. The causal chain is well documented: Roman jurisprudence was preserved through the medieval period, rediscovered and studied intensively at Bologna from the eleventh century, and became the foundation of the civil-law tradition codified in the nineteenth century. Roman legal concepts — contract, property, delict — continue to structure the legal systems of France, Germany, and Italy. The legacy manifestly shapes present institutions and practices. Yet it appears doubtful that condition (ii) is satisfied. The ordinary practitioner of law in France does not recognise her daily work as a continuation of Roman practice; she regards the Code civil as the law of France, not as a legacy of Rome. One might observe that the most profound legacies are precisely those that have been so thoroughly absorbed into the fabric of a society that their historical origin is no longer apparent to those who live within them.

definition: A civilisation C is in decline during a period if and only if (i) the political institutions of C are losing their capacity to maintain order, (ii) the cultural production of C is diminishing in quality and originality, and (iii) the material prosperity of C is decreasing.
conditions: The political institutions of C are losing their capacity to maintain order
The cultural production of C is diminishing in quality and originality
The material prosperity of C is decreasing
counterexample: Consider the Eastern Roman Empire in the sixth century under Justinian. The political institutions were strained by constant warfare on multiple frontiers and by the devastating plague of 542. Material prosperity, especially after the plague, was sharply declining. Yet this was the period of some of the empire's greatest cultural achievements — the construction of Hagia Sophia, the codification of Roman law in the Corpus Juris Civilis, and the mosaic art of Ravenna. Cultural production was not diminishing but reaching new heights even as political and material decline advanced.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the possibility that the dimensions of civilisational vitality — political, cultural, and material — may move independently, so that a civilisation may be declining in some respects whilst flourishing in others.
passage: One might define civilisational decline as a period in which political institutions are losing their capacity to maintain order, cultural production is diminishing in quality and originality, and material prosperity is decreasing. This definition has the merit of combining the political, cultural, and material dimensions of civilisational health. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the Eastern Roman Empire in the sixth century. Under Justinian, the empire was beset by formidable difficulties: the wars of reconquest in Italy and North Africa strained the treasury and the army, and the great plague of 542 devastated the population and the economy. By the criteria of political stability and material prosperity, the empire was manifestly declining. Yet this was simultaneously the period of some of the most remarkable cultural achievements in the empire's thousand-year history. Hagia Sophia, completed in 537, represents one of the supreme accomplishments of human architecture. The Corpus Juris Civilis, Justinian's codification of Roman law, has shaped Western legal thought for fifteen centuries. The mosaics of Ravenna display an artistic ambition and a technical mastery that stand comparison with the finest work of any era. One might observe that the definition, by requiring all three dimensions to decline simultaneously, cannot accommodate the evident fact that political decay and cultural flourishing may coexist.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites a set of antecedent events and conditions from which E follows as a likely consequence, (ii) the antecedent events and conditions cited in A are supported by documentary evidence, and (iii) A does not invoke supernatural or miraculous interventions.
conditions: A cites a set of antecedent events and conditions from which E follows as a likely consequence
The antecedent events and conditions cited in A are supported by documentary evidence
A does not invoke supernatural or miraculous interventions
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the outbreak of the French Revolution that cites the fiscal crisis of the monarchy, the poor harvest of 1788, and the influence of Enlightenment political thought — all supported by ample documentary evidence, none involving supernatural intervention. Yet one might observe that each of these conditions also obtained, in comparable measure, in several other European states that did not experience revolution. The account tells us why revolution was possible but not why it occurred in France rather than elsewhere.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the explanation distinguish why E occurred in the particular circumstances in which it did, rather than merely citing conditions that made E possible in many places where it did not in fact occur.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a satisfactory historical explanation of an event cites antecedent events and conditions, supported by documentary evidence, from which the event follows as a likely consequence, without invoking supernatural interventions. This characterisation captures much of what historians ordinarily do: they assemble documented causes and trace the path by which events unfolded. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed from the study of the French Revolution. An historian might explain the Revolution by citing the fiscal crisis of the Bourbon monarchy, the poor harvest of 1788, and the spread of Enlightenment political ideas — all amply documented, none supernatural. The Revolution follows as a likely consequence of these factors. But one might observe that these very conditions obtained in comparable measure in several other European states during the same period, states which did not experience revolution. The explanation tells us why revolution was possible but not why it was actual in France rather than in, say, the Habsburg dominions. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the citation of likely antecedent conditions, cannot distinguish an account of why an event was probable from an account of why it occurred where and when it did.

definition: A narrative N constitutes a satisfactory historical narrative if and only if (i) N arranges events in chronological order, (ii) N includes only events for which there is reliable evidence, and (iii) N connects the events by identifying the causal relations among them.
conditions: N arranges events in chronological order
N includes only events for which there is reliable evidence
N connects the events by identifying the causal relations among them
counterexample: Consider a narrative of the political history of nineteenth-century England that arranges the Reform Acts, changes in cabinet leadership, and economic fluctuations in chronological order, includes only well-documented events, and identifies the causal relations among them. It satisfies all three conditions. Yet it omits entirely the social history of the working classes, the transformation of domestic life, and the cultural shifts that accompanied political change. The narrative is causally connected, chronological, and well-evidenced, yet it distorts history by its selection.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the narrative's selection of events be representative of the significant dimensions of the historical period in question, so that a causally connected but systematically selective account may pass for a satisfactory narrative.
passage: One might define a satisfactory historical narrative as one that arranges events in chronological order, includes only events for which reliable evidence exists, and connects them by identifying causal relations. This account captures the temporal and evidential discipline that historiography demands. Yet one should hesitate to regard it as fully adequate. Consider a narrative of nineteenth-century England that meticulously chronicles the Reform Acts, the succession of prime ministers, and the economic fluctuations of the period, arranging them chronologically, documenting each with reliable evidence, and tracing the causal links between political and economic events. All three conditions are met. Yet the narrative omits entirely the lives of the working classes, the transformation of domestic arrangements, and the cultural and intellectual shifts that accompanied political change. One might observe that this narrative, though causally connected and chronologically ordered, distorts our understanding of the period by its systematic selection. It appears that the definition, by attending only to temporal order, evidential support, and causal connection, takes no account of the representativeness of the events selected and thus permits deeply partial accounts to qualify as satisfactory.

definition: A society S has made progress between time t1 and time t2 if and only if (i) the material conditions of life for the members of S are better at t2 than at t1, (ii) the improvement is the result of deliberate human effort rather than mere accident, and (iii) the improvement is general rather than confined to a particular class or segment of S.
conditions: The material conditions of life for the members of S are better at t2 than at t1
The improvement is the result of deliberate human effort rather than mere accident
The improvement is general rather than confined to a particular class or segment of S
counterexample: Consider a society that has achieved dramatic improvements in material conditions — increased life expectancy, better nutrition, more comfortable housing — as a result of deliberate industrial effort, and in which these improvements are shared across all classes. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the industrialisation which produced these material gains also destroyed long-standing communal traditions, poisoned rivers and air, and created forms of labour that degraded the human spirit. Whether such a society has genuinely progressed seems at the very least debatable.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the improvement not be accompanied by significant losses in other dimensions of human well-being — cultural, spiritual, or environmental — so that material advance purchased at the cost of other goods may count as progress.
passage: It is tempting to define progress as a general improvement in material conditions of life brought about by deliberate human effort. This conception has deep roots in Enlightenment thought, and it captures much of what we ordinarily mean when we speak of a society advancing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition may be drawn from the history of industrialisation. Consider a society that, through deliberate and sustained effort, has dramatically improved the material lot of its members across all social classes: life expectancy has risen, nutrition has improved, housing is more comfortable. All three conditions of the definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that this same process of industrialisation has destroyed long-standing communal traditions, rendered vast tracts of the natural landscape uninhabitable, and created forms of mechanical labour that many of those subjected to them experienced as a degradation of their humanity. Whether such a society has genuinely progressed is, to say the least, a matter upon which reasonable persons may disagree. It appears that the definition, by attending only to material conditions, cannot accommodate the possibility that progress in one dimension may be purchased at the cost of regress in another.

definition: A division of historical time into a period P is legitimate if and only if (i) there exists a clearly identifiable event or set of events that marks the beginning and end of P, (ii) the events and processes that occur during P share a distinctive character that differentiates P from adjacent periods, and (iii) the periodisation is recognised by the consensus of competent historians.
conditions: There exists a clearly identifiable event or set of events that marks the beginning and end of P
The events and processes that occur during P share a distinctive character that differentiates P from adjacent periods
The periodisation is recognised by the consensus of competent historians
counterexample: Consider the 'Middle Ages' as a historical period. The consensus of competent historians recognises it, and the events within it are said to share a distinctive feudal character. Yet historians disagree profoundly about when it begins — with the fall of Rome in 476, or the deposition of Romulus Augustulus, or the Visigothic sack — and when it ends — with the fall of Constantinople in 1453, or the invention of the printing press, or the voyages of Columbus. The boundary events are far from clearly identifiable, yet the periodisation is widely accepted.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that legitimate periodisations may involve boundaries that are gradual, contested, or conventional rather than sharply identifiable, so that it demands a precision in boundary-setting that historiographical practice does not in fact possess.
passage: One might propose that a division of historical time into periods is legitimate only when clearly identifiable events mark the beginning and end of each period, the events within the period share a distinctive character, and the periodisation commands the consensus of competent historians. This proposal has an orderly appeal, for it demands both objective boundaries and professional recognition. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the most venerable of historical periods. The 'Middle Ages' is recognised by a broad consensus of historians and is held to possess a distinctive feudal character. Yet the question of when the Middle Ages begin is deeply contested: does the period commence with the sack of Rome by the Visigoths, with the deposition of the last Western emperor, or with some other event? Similarly, the question of when it ends admits of no consensus: the fall of Constantinople, the invention of printing, or the discovery of the New World have each been proposed. It appears that the boundaries of this universally recognised period are not clearly identifiable events but rather conventional and contested choices. The definition, by requiring clearly identifiable boundary events, imposes a sharpness of demarcation that the actual practice of periodisation does not sustain.

definition: An event or sequence of events R constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) R involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) R occurs rapidly relative to the pace of ordinary political change, and (iii) R is carried out by a broad popular movement rather than a small conspiratorial faction.
conditions: R involves the overthrow of an existing political order
R occurs rapidly relative to the pace of ordinary political change
R is carried out by a broad popular movement rather than a small conspiratorial faction
counterexample: Consider the Bolshevik seizure of power in October 1917. It involved the overthrow of the Provisional Government and thus satisfies the first condition. It occurred with remarkable rapidity — a matter of days — and thus satisfies the second. Yet it was carried out not by a broad popular movement but by a highly organised conspiratorial faction acting in the name of the proletariat. By the definition, it would not qualify as a genuine revolution, though it is universally recognised as one of the most consequential revolutions in modern history.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate revolutions in which a small, disciplined vanguard acts as the agent of transformation, even when the broader populace did not directly participate in the act of overthrow.
passage: A natural account of revolution holds that a genuine revolution involves the rapid overthrow of an existing political order by a broad popular movement. This characterisation accords well with the familiar image of the French Revolution, in which the masses stormed the Bastille and the old regime was swept away. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises when one considers the Bolshevik seizure of power in October 1917. The Provisional Government was overthrown — of that there can be no doubt. The overthrow occurred with extraordinary rapidity, a matter of days rather than years. Yet the agents of this transformation were not a broad popular movement but a tightly organised conspiratorial faction led by Lenin and Trotsky. The Bolsheviks acted in the name of the proletariat, but the actual seizure of the Winter Palace was the work of a relatively small number of armed men. One might observe that the October Revolution is nonetheless universally regarded as one of the most consequential revolutions in modern history. The definition, by requiring broad popular participation, cannot accommodate revolutions executed by disciplined vanguards.

definition: A human community C constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) C possesses a system of writing, (ii) C has developed a settled agricultural economy, and (iii) C exhibits a complex division of labour extending beyond mere subsistence.
conditions: C possesses a system of writing
C has developed a settled agricultural economy
C exhibits a complex division of labour extending beyond mere subsistence
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire of pre-Columbian South America. It developed a settled agricultural economy of great sophistication, exhibited a complex division of labour with specialised administrators, engineers, and artisans, yet possessed no system of writing in the conventional sense — employing instead the quipu, a system of knotted cords for record-keeping. By the definition, the Inca would not constitute a civilisation, though their political and architectural achievements rival those of any literate society.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate civilisations that achieve complex social organisation through non-written systems of communication and record-keeping, thereby imposing a culturally specific criterion that excludes genuinely advanced societies.
passage: One might propose that a civilisation is a human community that possesses a system of writing, has developed a settled agricultural economy, and exhibits a complex division of labour extending beyond mere subsistence. This definition has the merit of identifying features that are commonly associated with the great civilisations of the ancient world — Mesopotamia, Egypt, China, and Greece. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the New World. The Inca Empire of pre-Columbian South America developed a settled agricultural economy of extraordinary sophistication, terracing the Andean mountainsides and engineering irrigation systems of remarkable ingenuity. It exhibited a complex division of labour: administrators, engineers, architects, and artisans each performed specialised functions within a highly organised polity. Yet the Inca possessed no system of writing in the conventional sense. They employed the quipu — a system of knotted cords — for record-keeping, but this does not constitute writing as the definition requires. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon writing as a necessary condition, imposes a culturally specific criterion that excludes a society whose political, architectural, and agricultural achievements rival those of any literate civilisation.

definition: A society S is in decline during a period P if and only if (i) the central institutions of S are losing their capacity to fulfil their customary functions during P, (ii) the members of S widely perceive that conditions are worsening, and (iii) the cultural achievements of S during P are inferior to those of the preceding period.
conditions: The central institutions of S are losing their capacity to fulfil their customary functions during P
The members of S widely perceive that conditions are worsening
The cultural achievements of S during P are inferior to those of the preceding period
counterexample: Consider the late Roman Empire in the fourth and fifth centuries. Its central institutions — the army, the civil administration, the imperial treasury — were losing their capacity to fulfil their customary functions, and there was a widespread perception among Roman writers that conditions were deteriorating. Yet the cultural achievements of this period were by no means uniformly inferior: the works of Augustine, the great basilicas, and the codification of Roman law all belong to the era of supposed decline.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that a society may be in political or institutional decline while simultaneously producing cultural achievements of the highest order, so that decline in one dimension does not entail decline in all.
passage: One might define a society as being in decline when its central institutions are losing their functional capacity, its members widely perceive that conditions are worsening, and its cultural achievements are inferior to those of the preceding era. This definition has the virtue of combining institutional, perceptual, and cultural criteria. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the late Roman Empire. In the fourth and fifth centuries, the institutions of the Empire — the army, the civil service, the fiscal apparatus — were manifestly losing their capacity to fulfil their customary functions. Roman writers of the period frequently lamented the deterioration of public life. Yet the cultural achievements of this era were by no means uniformly inferior to those of earlier centuries. The philosophical and theological works of Augustine, the construction of great Christian basilicas, and the codification of Roman law into the Theodosian Code all belong to this period of institutional decay. One might observe that cultural flowering and institutional disintegration may proceed simultaneously. The definition, by requiring inferiority of cultural achievement as a necessary condition of decline, cannot accommodate this possibility and thus mischaracterises periods in which institutional collapse is accompanied by cultural vitality.

definition: An interpretation I of a historical text or event is anachronistic if and only if (i) I attributes to the historical agents concepts or categories that were not available in their time, (ii) the attribution is unintentional on the part of the interpreter, and (iii) the attribution distorts the meaning of the text or event.
conditions: I attributes to the historical agents concepts or categories that were not available in their time
The attribution is unintentional on the part of the interpreter
The attribution distorts the meaning of the text or event
counterexample: Consider an historian who deliberately and self-consciously applies the modern concept of 'class struggle' to the analysis of the Peasants' Revolt of 1381. The historian is fully aware that fourteenth-century English peasants did not possess the Marxian concept of class, and the attribution is entirely intentional — indeed, the historian regards the deliberate application of later categories as a legitimate methodological strategy. By the definition, this would not count as an anachronism, since the attribution is not unintentional. Yet many historians would regard it as anachronistic nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that an interpretation may be anachronistic regardless of whether the interpreter is aware of the temporal displacement, so that deliberate anachronism is excluded from the category.
passage: It is commonly held that an interpretation is anachronistic when it unintentionally attributes to historical agents concepts or categories unavailable in their time, thereby distorting the meaning of the text or event. This account captures the most obvious cases of anachronism — as when a careless historian describes a medieval monarch as pursuing 'nationalist' policies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises when one considers deliberate anachronism. Suppose an historian self-consciously applies the modern concept of 'class struggle' to the Peasants' Revolt of 1381. The historian is fully aware that fourteenth-century English peasants did not think in Marxian categories and concedes this openly. The attribution is entirely intentional — indeed, the historian defends the deliberate application of later categories as a productive methodological strategy. By the definition, this interpretation is not anachronistic, for the attribution is not unintentional. Yet one might observe that many competent historians would regard it as anachronistic all the same, for it imposes a conceptual framework upon historical agents who could not have recognised it as their own. The definition, by requiring unintentionality, excludes cases of deliberate anachronism that are no less distorting for being self-conscious.

definition: An event or movement E has a genuine historical legacy if and only if (i) E produces effects that persist beyond the generation in which E occurred, (ii) those effects are causally traceable to E, and (iii) the effects are regarded by later generations as significant.
conditions: E produces effects that persist beyond the generation in which E occurred
Those effects are causally traceable to E
The effects are regarded by later generations as significant
counterexample: Consider the introduction of lead pipes in Roman aqueducts. This innovation produced effects that persisted for centuries — chronic lead poisoning among urban populations — and these effects are causally traceable to the original engineering decision. Yet for most of the intervening period, the effects were not recognised as significant by later generations; it was only with the advent of modern toxicology that the consequences were appreciated. During the long centuries of ignorance, the definition would deny that the Roman plumbing had a genuine legacy.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate legacies whose effects persist and are causally traceable but whose significance is not recognised by the generations that experience them, so that unrecognised consequences are excluded from the notion of legacy.
passage: One might suppose that a genuine historical legacy consists in effects that persist beyond the originating generation, are causally traceable to the event or movement in question, and are regarded by later generations as significant. This proposal ties the concept of legacy to both causal continuity and retrospective recognition. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the history of Roman engineering. The introduction of lead pipes in Roman aqueducts produced effects that persisted for centuries: chronic lead poisoning affected urban populations throughout the Roman world and beyond. The effects are causally traceable to the engineering decision to use lead rather than other materials. Yet for most of the intervening period — from antiquity through the mediaeval era — the consequences were not recognised as significant by anyone. It was only with the development of modern toxicology that the health effects of the Roman plumbing system came to be appreciated. During the long centuries in which the effects went unrecognised, the definition would deny that the Roman innovation possessed a genuine legacy. It appears that the definition, by requiring that later generations regard the effects as significant, cannot accommodate legacies whose consequences are real and persistent but whose significance remains unrecognised.

definition: A practice or set of practices P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next by deliberate instruction, (ii) P has persisted for at least three generations, and (iii) the practitioners of P regard it as having been received from the past.
conditions: P has been transmitted from one generation to the next by deliberate instruction
P has persisted for at least three generations
The practitioners of P regard it as having been received from the past
counterexample: Consider the annual ceremony of 'Beating the Bounds' in certain English parishes, in which parishioners walk the boundaries of the parish and strike boundary markers with willow wands. The practitioners regard it as an ancient tradition received from the past, and it has persisted for many generations. Yet historical research reveals that in many parishes the ceremony was invented or reinvented in the nineteenth century after a lapse of centuries, often by antiquarian clergymen who fabricated continuity with a mediaeval past. The transmission was not continuous but was deliberately reconstructed to appear so.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the transmission be genuinely continuous rather than retrospectively fabricated, so that invented traditions that simulate antiquity may satisfy all three conditions.
passage: A natural account of tradition holds that a practice constitutes a tradition when it has been transmitted by deliberate instruction through at least three generations and is regarded by its practitioners as received from the past. This account has an evident appeal, for it identifies the features — transmission, persistence, and a backward-looking self-understanding — that we ordinarily associate with traditional practices. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from English parochial customs. The ceremony of 'Beating the Bounds,' in which parishioners walk the parish boundaries and strike markers with willow wands, is regarded by its practitioners as an ancient custom received from the mediaeval past, and it has persisted in various parishes for many generations. All three conditions are met. Yet historical research has shown that in many parishes the ceremony was invented or reinvented in the nineteenth century by antiquarian clergymen who wished to revive what they believed to be an ancient practice. The transmission was not genuinely continuous; it was deliberately reconstructed to create an appearance of unbroken antiquity. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the practitioners' perception of reception from the past, cannot distinguish genuine traditions from invented ones that simulate historical continuity.

definition: A civilisation C exhibits genuine historical progress if and only if (i) C shows measurable improvement in material conditions of life over time, (ii) C develops increasingly sophisticated institutions of governance and law, and (iii) C accumulates a growing body of scientific and technical achievement.
conditions: C shows measurable improvement in material conditions of life over time
C develops increasingly sophisticated institutions of governance and law
C accumulates a growing body of scientific and technical achievement
counterexample: Consider the Roman Empire during the second century. Material conditions improved through aqueducts, roads, and trade. Legal and governmental institutions grew increasingly refined — the Corpus Juris Civilis stands as one of the great achievements of legal thought. And technical achievements accumulated in engineering, architecture, and agriculture. All three conditions were met. Yet this same civilisation depended upon an enormous population of enslaved persons whose subjection grew more entrenched as the empire grew more prosperous. The progress of some was achieved at the cost of the degradation of others.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the improvements in material conditions, governance, and technical achievement extend to all members of the civilisation, so that a society in which progress is enjoyed by a privileged stratum while others endure worsening conditions may still qualify as exhibiting genuine progress.
passage: One might characterise genuine historical progress as a pattern in which a civilisation shows measurable improvement in material conditions, develops increasingly sophisticated institutions, and accumulates scientific and technical achievements. This characterisation captures what many historians have meant by progress: the advance of comfort, governance, and understanding. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from classical antiquity. The Roman Empire of the second century satisfies all three conditions admirably. Material conditions improved through the construction of aqueducts, roads, and public baths. Governance and law reached remarkable sophistication, culminating in a legal tradition that influenced European jurisprudence for centuries. Technical achievements accumulated in engineering, agriculture, and medicine. Nevertheless, one might observe that this progress was sustained by the labour of an enormous enslaved population. The refinement of law did not extend to the legal personhood of the enslaved. The material prosperity of the citizen classes was built upon the systematic degradation of others. It appears that the definition, by measuring progress in aggregate terms without attending to its distribution, permits a society to count as progressing even when a substantial portion of its inhabitants experiences worsening conditions.

definition: An account A provides a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies the intentions and decisions of the principal agents involved, (ii) A shows how those intentions led, through a chain of actions, to E, and (iii) the intentions and actions cited are supported by primary documentary sources.
conditions: A identifies the intentions and decisions of the principal agents involved
A shows how those intentions led, through a chain of actions, to E
The intentions and actions cited are supported by primary documentary sources
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the Irish Famine of the 1840s that cites the intentions of British politicians — their decisions about trade policy, their deliberations in Parliament — and traces how those decisions led to the failure of relief efforts. All three conditions are met. Yet the famine was caused primarily by a potato blight, a natural catastrophe that no political agent intended or could have prevented. The actions of politicians shaped the response, but the event itself was precipitated by factors entirely beyond the scope of intentional agency.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A account for structural, environmental, and other non-intentional factors that may be causally essential to the occurrence of E, so that an explanation confined to individual agency may be radically incomplete.
passage: A common proposal holds that a satisfactory historical explanation identifies the intentions and decisions of principal agents, traces how those intentions led to the event in question through a chain of actions, and supports the account with primary documentary sources. This proposal has the merit of grounding explanation in the deliberate actions of historical persons. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the history of Ireland. An historian might explain the Great Famine of the 1840s by identifying the intentions of British politicians — their commitment to laissez-faire economics, their reluctance to intervene in Irish markets, their specific decisions regarding the Poor Law and the export of grain. The account traces how these decisions led to the failure of famine relief and supports every claim with parliamentary records, ministerial correspondence, and cabinet memoranda. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the famine was precipitated by the arrival of Phytophthora infestans, a potato blight that no political agent intended, foresaw, or could have prevented. The decisions of politicians shaped the catastrophe's severity, but the catastrophe itself was caused by a biological event that lies entirely outside the domain of intentional action. The definition, by requiring only the identification of agents' intentions, cannot accommodate the structural, environmental, and material causes that are often decisive in historical explanation.

definition: A division of historical time into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is marked by a distinctive set of social, political, and cultural characteristics, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant transformative events, and (iii) the characteristics of one period differ qualitatively from those of the preceding and succeeding periods.
conditions: Each period is marked by a distinctive set of social, political, and cultural characteristics
The boundaries between periods correspond to significant transformative events
The characteristics of one period differ qualitatively from those of the preceding and succeeding periods
counterexample: Consider the conventional division between the 'Medieval' and 'Renaissance' periods in European history, typically placed around the fourteenth century. There is no single transformative event that marks this boundary; the features supposed to characterise the Renaissance — the revival of classical learning, the development of perspective in painting, the growth of commerce — emerged gradually and at different times in different regions. Florence in 1350 looks decidedly 'Renaissance,' while much of Northern Europe in 1450 retains characteristics conventionally deemed 'Medieval.' All three conditions appear met in theory, yet the boundary dissolves upon close examination.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the transformative events and distinctive characteristics obtain uniformly across the geographical and social scope of the period in question, so that a periodisation which holds for one region or social class but not another may count as legitimate.
passage: One might propose that a legitimate periodisation divides historical time into periods marked by distinctive social, political, and cultural characteristics, separated by boundaries corresponding to significant transformative events, with each period differing qualitatively from its neighbours. This characterisation captures the purpose of periodisation: to render the past intelligible by identifying coherent epochs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises with the conventional division between the Medieval and Renaissance periods. Historians have long placed this boundary somewhere around the fourteenth century, pointing to the revival of classical learning, the development of naturalistic art, and the growth of commercial capitalism as the distinctive characteristics of the Renaissance. Yet these features emerged at radically different times in different regions. Florence exhibited recognisably Renaissance characteristics by the mid-fourteenth century, while much of Northern Europe retained features conventionally deemed Medieval well into the fifteenth. There is no single transformative event that marks the boundary. One might observe that the definition, by requiring significant transformative events at period boundaries without specifying that the transformation be geographically and socially uniform, permits periodisations that reflect the experience of one region or social class while misrepresenting another. The 'Renaissance' is a meaningful category for certain Italian city-states but a misleading one for the peasantry of Poland.

definition: A society S constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) S possesses a system of writing, (ii) S has developed urban centres with monumental architecture, and (iii) S maintains a specialised division of labour.
conditions: S possesses a system of writing
S has developed urban centres with monumental architecture
S maintains a specialised division of labour
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire. It maintained a vast and sophisticated administrative state spanning thousands of miles. It built monumental architecture — Machu Picchu, Sacsayhuaman — of extraordinary precision. It sustained a highly specialised division of labour, including engineers, soldiers, priests, and agricultural specialists. Yet it possessed no system of writing in the conventional sense, relying instead on quipu — knotted strings used for recording numerical and possibly narrative information. Two of the three conditions are met, but the absence of conventional writing would exclude one of the most impressive polities in human history from the category of civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for functionally equivalent systems of information storage and transmission — such as quipu — that serve the purposes of writing without taking the form of inscribed symbols, so that societies with alternative recording technologies are unjustly excluded.
passage: A traditional characterisation holds that a society constitutes a civilisation if it possesses a system of writing, has developed urban centres with monumental architecture, and maintains a specialised division of labour. This characterisation has a long pedigree and captures features common to many of the societies conventionally recognised as civilisations — Mesopotamia, Egypt, China, the Indus Valley. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the Americas. The Inca Empire maintained a vast administrative state encompassing millions of people across thousands of miles of mountainous terrain. Its monumental architecture — the citadel of Machu Picchu, the fortress of Sacsayhuaman — was constructed with a precision that astonishes modern engineers. Its division of labour was highly specialised, encompassing engineers, soldiers, priests, weavers, and agricultural specialists. Yet the Inca possessed no system of writing in the conventional sense. They employed quipu — elaborate systems of knotted strings — for recording numerical data and perhaps narrative information, but these do not constitute writing as traditionally understood. One might observe that the definition, by requiring writing in its conventional form, excludes one of the most impressive polities in human history from the category of civilisation. It appears that the insistence upon writing, rather than upon the functional capacity for information storage and transmission, renders the definition unduly narrow.

definition: A civilisation C is in decline if and only if (i) C experiences a sustained contraction of its territorial extent, (ii) C suffers a deterioration in the functioning of its central political institutions, and (iii) C undergoes a loss of its capacity to defend its borders against external threats.
conditions: C experiences a sustained contraction of its territorial extent
C suffers a deterioration in the functioning of its central political institutions
C undergoes a loss of its capacity to defend its borders against external threats
counterexample: Consider the Byzantine Empire in the tenth century. It experienced no sustained territorial contraction — indeed, under the Macedonian dynasty, it reconquered territories in Syria, Crete, and southern Italy. Its central institutions functioned with remarkable efficiency. And its military capacity was formidable. Yet during this same period, Byzantine culture arguably entered a phase of increasing rigidity and conservatism, its intellectual life becoming more derivative, its artistic forms increasingly stylised. Some historians have regarded this cultural stagnation as a form of decline, despite the empire's political and military vitality.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for cultural, intellectual, or artistic decline, so that a civilisation which is politically vigorous and militarily powerful but culturally stagnant may nonetheless be classified as not in decline.
passage: One might characterise civilisational decline as a pattern in which a society experiences sustained territorial contraction, deterioration of central institutions, and loss of military capacity. This characterisation captures the most visible symptoms of decline and accords with many historical narratives. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the Byzantine Empire during the Macedonian dynasty of the tenth century. Territorially, the empire was expanding — reconquering Crete, pushing into Syria, recovering positions in southern Italy. Its central institutions — the civil service, the court, the legal apparatus — functioned with a sophistication unmatched in contemporary Europe. Its military capacity, augmented by the themes system and the professional tagmata, was formidable. All three conditions of decline are conspicuously absent. Yet some historians have argued that Byzantine civilisation was entering a phase of cultural and intellectual decline during this very period — its literary production growing more derivative, its artistic forms more rigidly stylised, its philosophical originality diminishing. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to territorial, institutional, and military indicators, cannot capture the possibility that a civilisation may decline culturally and intellectually even while flourishing politically and militarily. The definition mistakes the outward shell for the living substance.

definition: A practice P constitutes a genuine tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted continuously from one generation to the next, (ii) the practitioners of P regard it as having been handed down from the past, and (iii) P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted continuously from one generation to the next
The practitioners of P regard it as having been handed down from the past
P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission
counterexample: Consider the Scottish Highland tradition of wearing clan tartans. Practitioners regard it as an ancient custom handed down from the distant past. Yet historical research has shown that the association of specific tartan patterns with specific clans was largely an invention of the early nineteenth century, promoted by textile manufacturers and popularised by Sir Walter Scott's stage-management of George IV's visit to Edinburgh in 1822. The 'tradition' has been continuously transmitted since then and is regarded as ancient, but it has not in fact remained substantially unchanged — it was substantially created within living memory of its supposed antiquity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the historical claims implicit in the practitioners' self-understanding be accurate, so that an 'invented tradition' — one that is recent in origin but presented as ancient — may satisfy all three conditions.
passage: A natural proposal for characterising a genuine tradition holds that it is a practice transmitted continuously from one generation to the next, regarded by its practitioners as having been handed down from the past, and substantially unchanged throughout its transmission. This characterisation captures the intuitive notion of tradition as a living link to the past. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the history of Scotland. The wearing of clan-specific tartans is widely regarded as an ancient Highland tradition, transmitted continuously since time immemorial. Practitioners of the custom regard it as having been handed down from their ancestors. Yet historical investigation reveals that the association of particular tartan patterns with particular clans was largely an invention of the early nineteenth century. Textile manufacturers and romanticist antiquarians, most notably Sir Walter Scott, created and promoted these associations during the 1820s. The practice has been continuously transmitted since then — it is now well over two centuries old — and its practitioners sincerely regard it as ancient. But the historical claims embedded in that self-understanding are false. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only continuity, self-understanding, and stability, cannot distinguish a genuinely ancient tradition from one that has been recently invented but successfully presented as immemorial. The definition attends to the phenomenology of tradition but not to its actual historical pedigree.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical narrative if and only if (i) A recounts events in temporal sequence, (ii) A connects those events by causal or motivational links, and (iii) A is faithful to the documentary record.
conditions: A recounts events in temporal sequence
A connects those events by causal or motivational links
A is faithful to the documentary record
counterexample: Consider two narratives of the causes of the First World War. The first begins with the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand and traces the chain of diplomatic ultimatums leading to war. The second begins with the arms race of the 1890s and traces the accumulation of geopolitical tensions over two decades. Both recount events in temporal sequence, connect them by causal links, and are faithful to the documentary record. All three conditions are satisfied by both accounts. Yet the two narratives, selecting different events and different causal connections, tell fundamentally different stories and support different conclusions about responsibility.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the selection of events and causal connections be justified as the most explanatorily relevant, so that multiple incompatible but equally well-documented narratives may all count as satisfactory.
passage: One might characterise a satisfactory historical narrative as an account that recounts events in temporal sequence, connects them by causal or motivational links, and is faithful to the documentary record. This characterisation captures the temporal structure, explanatory ambition, and evidential grounding that we expect from historical writing. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers competing narratives of the same event. Consider two accounts of the origins of the First World War. The first begins with the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo and traces the chain of diplomatic ultimatums, mobilisation orders, and alliance obligations that led within six weeks to a continental war. The second begins with the naval arms race of the 1890s and traces the slow accumulation of imperial rivalries, militarist ideologies, and alliance systems over two decades. Both accounts recount events in temporal sequence. Both connect their events by causal links. Both are faithful to the documentary record. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the two narratives are substantially different and support incompatible conclusions about the causes and the assignment of responsibility. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only sequential ordering, causal connection, and documentary fidelity, cannot adjudicate between competing narratives that select different events and different causal pathways from the same documentary record. A more adequate account would need to specify criteria for the selection and weighting of events.

definition: An historical claim C is anachronistic if and only if (i) C attributes to a past society a concept, practice, or institution that did not exist in that society, (ii) the concept, practice, or institution in question belongs to a later period, and (iii) C fails to recognise the temporal displacement.
conditions: C attributes to a past society a concept, practice, or institution that did not exist in that society
The concept, practice, or institution in question belongs to a later period
C fails to recognise the temporal displacement
counterexample: Consider the claim that the Athenians of the fifth century B.C. practised democracy. The concept of democracy, understood as self-governance by citizens through assembly and vote, did in fact exist in fifth-century Athens — indeed, it originated there. It does not belong exclusively to a later period. And the claim does not involve any temporal displacement. All three conditions fail to be met, and the definition correctly classifies the claim as non-anachronistic. Yet suppose an historian claims that the Athenians practised democratic governance while noting explicitly that the Athenian concept of democracy excluded women, slaves, and resident foreigners from citizenship. The historian explicitly notes the temporal and conceptual distance. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Yet many historians would still regard the use of the term 'democracy' in this context as mildly anachronistic, because the modern connotations of the word inevitably colour the reader's understanding.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for cases in which a term is formally recognised as temporally distant but nevertheless carries modern connotations that distort the reader's understanding of the past society, so that a conscious but insufficiently controlled use of modern vocabulary may escape the charge of anachronism.
passage: One might define anachronism as the attribution to a past society of a concept, practice, or institution that did not exist in it, belonging instead to a later period, and without recognition of the temporal displacement. This characterisation identifies the core of what historians mean by anachronism: the projection of the present onto the past. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the study of Athenian politics. Suppose an historian writes that fifth-century Athens practised 'democracy' and carefully notes that the Athenian form differed profoundly from modern democracy — excluding women, enslaved persons, and resident foreigners from the citizen body. The historian explicitly notes the temporal and conceptual distance. Condition (iii) is thus not satisfied, and the definition would classify the claim as non-anachronistic. Yet many scholars would observe that the use of the word 'democracy,' even with qualifications, inevitably imports modern connotations — universal suffrage, individual rights, representative government — that distort the reader's understanding of the Athenian practice. The definition, by requiring only that the historian fail to recognise the displacement, cannot capture cases in which recognition is present but insufficient to counteract the distorting effects of modern vocabulary. It appears that anachronism may operate at the level of connotation as well as denotation, and the definition attends only to the latter.

definition: An historical figure F possesses a lasting legacy if and only if (i) F's actions or ideas had a demonstrable effect upon subsequent historical developments, (ii) that effect persisted beyond F's own lifetime, and (iii) F is remembered and recognised for that effect by later generations.
conditions: F's actions or ideas had a demonstrable effect upon subsequent historical developments
That effect persisted beyond F's own lifetime
F is remembered and recognised for that effect by later generations
counterexample: Consider the anonymous Chinese inventor who developed the first effective gunpowder formula during the Tang dynasty. This invention had an enormous and demonstrable effect upon subsequent military, political, and technological developments across the entire world. The effect persisted for centuries and continues to the present day. Yet the inventor is not remembered or recognised by later generations, for no record of their identity survives. Two of the three conditions are met, but the third is not, and the definition would deny this person a lasting legacy despite having altered the course of human history.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that a person may possess a lasting legacy through the enduring effects of their actions or ideas even when their individual identity has been lost to the historical record, so that anonymous but enormously influential figures are excluded.
passage: One might characterise a lasting legacy as a pattern in which an historical figure's actions or ideas demonstrably affected subsequent developments, the effect persisted beyond the figure's lifetime, and later generations remember and recognise the figure for that effect. This characterisation accords with the way historians typically speak of legacy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the history of technology. Consider the anonymous Chinese inventor — or perhaps inventors — who first developed an effective gunpowder formula during the Tang dynasty. The effect of this invention upon subsequent history is difficult to overstate: it transformed warfare, reshaped political boundaries, and altered the balance of power across continents. The effect has persisted for over a millennium and shows no sign of diminishing. Yet the inventor is entirely unknown. No record of their identity survives. Later generations cannot remember or recognise a person whose name and circumstances have been lost. The definition would thus deny this person a lasting legacy. One might observe that this conclusion is absurd — the inventor of gunpowder has shaped the modern world as profoundly as any named figure in history. The definition, by requiring recognition by later generations, conflates legacy with fame and cannot accommodate the historically decisive contributions of anonymous persons.

definition: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the forcible overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E results in a fundamental transformation of the institutions of governance, and (iii) E is accomplished within a relatively brief period.
conditions: E involves the forcible overthrow of an existing political order
E results in a fundamental transformation of the institutions of governance
E is accomplished within a relatively brief period
counterexample: Consider the Industrial Revolution. It resulted in a fundamental transformation of economic, social, and political institutions — the rise of the factory system, the emergence of new social classes, the extension of the franchise. It is universally described as a revolution. Yet it involved no forcible overthrow of a political order, and it unfolded over the better part of a century rather than within a brief period. Only condition (ii) is satisfied. The definition would exclude what is perhaps the most consequential revolution in modern history.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for non-political and non-violent revolutions — transformations that fundamentally restructure economic, social, or intellectual life without the forcible overthrow of a government — so that only political upheavals qualify.
passage: A natural proposal for characterising a genuine revolution holds that it involves the forcible overthrow of an existing political order, results in a fundamental transformation of governance, and is accomplished within a relatively brief period. This characterisation captures the essential features of paradigmatic political revolutions — the French Revolution, the Russian Revolution, the American Revolution. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the Industrial Revolution. This event — or rather, this protracted process — resulted in a transformation of human institutions as profound as any political upheaval. The factory system replaced the workshop and the farm. New social classes emerged. The distribution of political power shifted fundamentally. The material conditions of life were altered beyond recognition. Yet the Industrial Revolution involved no forcible overthrow of a political order. It unfolded not over weeks or months but over the better part of a century. Only the second condition — fundamental institutional transformation — is satisfied. One might observe that the definition, by requiring forcible overthrow and brief duration, is confined to political revolutions and cannot accommodate the economic, social, and intellectual transformations that historians have rightly described as revolutionary. It appears that the concept of revolution is broader than the definition allows.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites antecedent conditions that were necessary for the occurrence of E, (ii) A connects these conditions to E by means of a general law or regularity, and (iii) A is consistent with the available documentary evidence.
conditions: A cites antecedent conditions that were necessary for the occurrence of E
A connects these conditions to E by means of a general law or regularity
A is consistent with the available documentary evidence
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the fall of the Roman Republic that cites the necessary antecedent conditions — the expansion of Roman territory, the impoverishment of the small farmer, the rise of professional armies loyal to their commanders — connects them via the general regularity that republics whose military forces owe allegiance to individuals rather than the state are liable to collapse, and is consistent with all available documentary evidence. All three conditions are met. Yet the explanation omits the contingent decisions and character of particular individuals — Julius Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon, Pompey's strategic miscalculations — which were not entailed by any general law yet were indispensable to the particular course events took.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the explanation accommodate the role of contingent individual actions and decisions that, while not subsumable under general laws, are nevertheless essential to the particular form the historical event assumed.
passage: One might characterise a satisfactory historical explanation as an account that cites necessary antecedent conditions, connects them to the event by means of a general law, and is consistent with the documentary evidence. This characterisation assimilates historical explanation to the covering-law model familiar from the philosophy of science. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed from Roman history. Suppose one explains the fall of the Roman Republic by citing the expansion of territory, the dispossession of small farmers, and the rise of professional armies loyal to their commanders, connecting these conditions to the Republic's collapse via the regularity that polities whose military forces owe allegiance to individuals are liable to dissolution. The account is consistent with all surviving documents. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the explanation omits the contingent actions of particular individuals — Caesar's decision to cross the Rubicon, Pompey's strategic errors, the conspiracies of the Senate — which cannot be subsumed under any general law yet without which the Republic's fall would not have taken the particular form it did. The definition, by requiring connection to a general law, forces historical explanation into a mould that cannot accommodate the irreducibly contingent character of individual decision and action.

definition: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N recounts a sequence of events in chronological order, (ii) N draws only upon sources that are accepted as authentic by competent scholars, and (iii) N provides a coherent causal thread connecting the events.
conditions: N recounts a sequence of events in chronological order
N draws only upon sources that are accepted as authentic by competent scholars
N provides a coherent causal thread connecting the events
counterexample: Consider a narrative of the Reformation that recounts events in strict chronological order — the posting of Luther's theses, the Diet of Worms, the Peace of Augsburg — drawing upon impeccable documentary sources and providing a coherent causal thread in which each event follows naturally from the preceding one. All three conditions are met. Yet the narrative entirely omits the simultaneous and equally well-documented developments in the Swiss and French reformations, producing a coherent but misleadingly narrow account that presents the German experience as the whole of the Reformation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the narrative recognise and integrate relevant simultaneous developments outside its chosen causal thread, so that a narrative which is coherent but selectively incomplete may qualify as legitimate.
passage: A familiar proposal holds that a legitimate historical narrative recounts events in chronological order, draws upon authentic sources, and provides a coherent causal thread connecting the events. This characterisation captures the temporal, evidential, and explanatory virtues of good historical writing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the history of the Reformation. Consider a narrative that begins with Luther's posting of the ninety-five theses, proceeds through the Diet of Worms, the Peasants' War, and the Peace of Augsburg, drawing upon unimpeachable sources and connecting each event to the next by a coherent causal thread. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the narrative omits the simultaneous developments in Switzerland under Zwingli, in Geneva under Calvin, and in France among the Huguenots — developments equally well-documented and historically consequential. The result is a narrative that is coherent but misleadingly narrow, presenting the German experience as though it were the whole of the Reformation. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only chronological order, source authenticity, and causal coherence, cannot distinguish a narrative that is genuinely comprehensive from one that achieves coherence precisely by excluding inconvenient parallel developments.

definition: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is defined by a set of distinctive characteristics that are shared by the events and developments within it, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant transitions or ruptures, and (iii) the periodisation is applicable across the geographic region under study.
conditions: Each period is defined by a set of distinctive characteristics shared by the events and developments within it
The boundaries between periods correspond to significant transitions or ruptures
The periodisation is applicable across the geographic region under study
counterexample: Consider the standard periodisation of European history that places the boundary between the Middle Ages and the Renaissance at approximately 1400. In Italian city-states, this boundary corresponds to genuine cultural and economic transitions. But in Scandinavia, the social and cultural patterns characteristic of the medieval period persisted well into the sixteenth century. The periodisation meets all three conditions when applied to Italy but fails to capture the actual rhythm of historical change across the whole of Europe.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the periodisation accommodate regional variation in the timing of the transitions it identifies, so that a scheme which accurately marks ruptures in one region but misrepresents the pace of change in others may be applied uncritically to the entire geographic scope.
passage: One might propose that a legitimate periodisation defines each period by distinctive shared characteristics, draws boundaries at significant transitions, and is applicable across the geographic region under study. This characterisation captures the internal coherence and external applicability that good periodisations exhibit. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from European historiography. The conventional division between the Middle Ages and the Renaissance places the boundary at approximately 1400. In the Italian city-states, this boundary marks genuine transitions: the revival of classical learning, the emergence of new artistic forms, the flourishing of commercial capitalism. The periodisation satisfies all three conditions when applied to the Italian peninsula. Yet in Scandinavia, the social structures, economic patterns, and cultural forms characteristic of the medieval period persisted well into the sixteenth century. The 1400 boundary, though it marks a significant transition in one region, misrepresents the rhythm of change in another. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that the periodisation be applicable across a region without specifying that it accommodate regional variation in the timing of transitions, permits a scheme calibrated to one area's experience to be imposed upon areas where it is anachronistic.
