definition: A civilisation C has made genuine historical progress if and only if (i) C has increased its material productive capacity over time, (ii) C has expanded the range of individual liberties enjoyed by its members, and (iii) C has accumulated a growing body of systematic understanding of the natural world.
conditions: C has increased its material productive capacity over time
C has expanded the range of individual liberties enjoyed by its members
C has accumulated a growing body of systematic understanding of the natural world
counterexample: Consider Victorian Britain in the 1840s. Its material productive capacity had increased enormously through industrialisation. Individual liberties had been expanded through parliamentary reform and the abolition of slavery in the Empire. And its systematic understanding of the natural world had grown spectacularly. All three conditions were met. Yet the same industrialisation that increased productive capacity had created appalling conditions in factories and mines, consigning millions of children and adults to lives of misery far worse than those of pre-industrial rural labourers. It would be strange to call this unqualified progress.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the benefits of increased productive capacity, expanded liberties, and accumulated understanding be distributed in a manner that does not systematically impose severe hardship upon a substantial portion of the population.
passage: One might define genuine historical progress as consisting in the increase of material productive capacity, the expansion of individual liberties, and the accumulation of systematic understanding of the natural world. This tripartite definition is attractive because it combines economic, political, and intellectual dimensions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from nineteenth-century British history. Victorian Britain in the 1840s satisfied all three conditions to a remarkable degree. Industrialisation had vastly increased productive capacity. Parliamentary reform and the abolition of colonial slavery had expanded liberties. And the natural sciences were advancing at an unprecedented rate. Yet the same industrialisation that produced these gains had created conditions of appalling misery in the factories and mines of Lancashire and Yorkshire. Children as young as five laboured in darkness for sixteen hours a day. Life expectancy in industrial cities fell below that of pre-industrial villages. One might observe that the definition, by attending to aggregate measures of productivity, liberty, and understanding without requiring that their benefits be broadly shared, permits a situation in which spectacular advancement on every dimension coexists with the systematic immiseration of a large portion of the population to qualify as genuine progress.

definition: An event or series of events R constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) R results in a fundamental transformation of the political order, (ii) R is accomplished through the deliberate collective action of a mobilised populace, and (iii) R occurs over a relatively compressed period of time.
conditions: R results in a fundamental transformation of the political order
R is accomplished through the deliberate collective action of a mobilised populace
R occurs over a relatively compressed period of time
counterexample: Consider the Glorious Revolution of 1688 in England. It resulted in a fundamental transformation of the political order — the replacement of absolute monarchy by constitutional monarchy under parliamentary sovereignty. It occurred over a compressed period. Yet it was accomplished not by the deliberate collective action of a mobilised populace but by an invasion by a Dutch prince and the defection of the English elite. The populace played no significant mobilising role. Two of the three conditions are met, and the event is universally called a revolution, but the definition would exclude it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that a fundamental and rapid transformation of the political order may be accomplished through elite action, external intervention, or institutional realignment rather than through popular mobilisation, so that revolutions brought about by means other than collective popular action are excluded.
passage: A natural proposal characterises a genuine revolution as a fundamental transformation of the political order, accomplished through deliberate collective action of a mobilised populace, and occurring over a compressed period. This definition captures the dramatic and participatory character commonly associated with revolutionary change. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from English history. The Glorious Revolution of 1688 resulted in a fundamental transformation: the replacement of the Stuart absolutism with constitutional monarchy and parliamentary sovereignty. It occurred over the space of a few months. Yet it was accomplished not by the mobilisation of the English populace but by the invasion of William of Orange with a Dutch army and the strategic defection of key members of the English aristocracy and military. The people of England were largely spectators rather than participants. One might observe that the definition, by requiring deliberate collective popular action, cannot accommodate an event that is universally recognised as a revolution but was engineered from above and from abroad. The definition imposes a particular mechanism — popular mobilisation — that is not a necessary feature of all revolutionary transformations.

definition: A historical figure F has left a genuine legacy if and only if (i) F's actions or ideas have had lasting effects that persist beyond F's own lifetime, (ii) these effects are recognised and attributed to F by subsequent generations, and (iii) the effects are broadly positive in their consequences for the societies they influence.
conditions: F's actions or ideas have had lasting effects that persist beyond F's own lifetime
These effects are recognised and attributed to F by subsequent generations
The effects are broadly positive in their consequences for the societies they influence
counterexample: Consider Genghis Khan. His actions had lasting effects that persist to this day — the political boundaries of Central Asia, the genetic distribution of populations, the pathways of trade and cultural exchange across the Eurasian continent. These effects are widely recognised and attributed to him. Whether they are broadly positive depends upon one's perspective: the Mongol conquests facilitated trade and cultural exchange but also caused the deaths of millions. The definition requires us to make a judgement about broad positivity that the concept of legacy does not itself demand.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a normative requirement of broadly positive consequences that does not belong to the concept of legacy as such; it fails to recognise that a figure may leave an enormous and widely recognised legacy that is destructive, ambiguous, or morally mixed.
passage: One might define a genuine legacy as consisting in lasting effects that persist beyond a historical figure's lifetime, are recognised and attributed to that figure by subsequent generations, and are broadly positive in their consequences. This definition attempts to distinguish genuine legacies from mere historical influence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of the Mongol Empire. Genghis Khan's actions had lasting effects of extraordinary magnitude: the political configuration of Central Asia, the demographic composition of Eurasia, the establishment of trade routes linking East and West. These effects are universally recognised and attributed to him. Yet whether they are broadly positive is deeply contestable. The Mongol conquests killed tens of millions of people and devastated civilisations that had flourished for centuries. At the same time, they opened channels of communication and commerce that enriched the world. One might observe that the definition, by requiring broad positivity, cannot accommodate the plain fact that some of the most consequential legacies in human history are morally ambiguous or outright destructive. The concept of legacy concerns lasting and widely attributed influence, not moral endorsement.

definition: A practice P constitutes a genuine tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from generation to generation over an extended period, (ii) P is regarded by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past, and (iii) P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted from generation to generation over an extended period
P is regarded by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past
P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission
counterexample: Consider the Scottish Highland Games. These are regarded by participants as an ancient tradition inherited from the medieval clan system. They have been transmitted from generation to generation. Yet historians have shown that the Highland Games in their current form were largely invented in the Victorian era, shaped by Romantic ideals of Scottish identity. The games have changed substantially over time — the events, the costumes, the ceremonies bear little resemblance to any medieval practice. Yet no one would deny that the Highland Games are, in the relevant sense, a tradition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the phenomenon of traditions that are continuously adapted and reinvented while maintaining a perceived continuity with the past; it requires substantial unchangeability where the concept of tradition in fact permits and even expects gradual transformation.
passage: A natural proposal holds that a genuine tradition is a practice transmitted from generation to generation, regarded as inherited from the past, and substantially unchanged throughout its transmission. This characterisation emphasises continuity and stability as the hallmarks of tradition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from Scottish cultural history. The Highland Games are regarded by their participants as an ancient tradition inherited from the medieval clan system. They have been transmitted across generations. Yet historians have demonstrated that the Highland Games in their present form owe more to Victorian Romanticism than to any medieval practice. The specific events, the tartans worn, the ceremonies performed — all were shaped or invented in the nineteenth century. The games have changed substantially. By the third condition, they would not qualify as a genuine tradition. Yet one might observe that to deny them the status of tradition would be to defy ordinary usage and common understanding. It appears that the definition, by requiring substantial unchangeability, imposes a standard that few if any actual traditions could meet. Traditions characteristically evolve, adapt, and are reinvented, all while maintaining a perceived — though often fictive — continuity with the past.

definition: A statement S about a historical period is anachronistic if and only if (i) S attributes to the period a concept, practice, or artefact that did not exist during that period, (ii) the attribution is factually incorrect, and (iii) the error results from the uncritical projection of the historian's own categories onto the past.
conditions: S attributes to the period a concept, practice, or artefact that did not exist during that period
The attribution is factually incorrect
The error results from the uncritical projection of the historian's own categories onto the past
counterexample: Consider a historian who deliberately applies the modern concept of 'inflation' to the Roman economy of the third century, fully aware that the Romans had no such concept, in order to illuminate patterns in the historical data that would otherwise remain obscure. The attribution is factually incorrect in the sense that the Romans did not possess the concept. But the projection is not uncritical — it is a self-conscious analytical tool. The third condition is not met, and the definition would classify this as non-anachronistic. Yet many historians would say that even deliberate and self-conscious use of modern categories constitutes a form of anachronism, albeit a productive one.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that anachronism may be deliberate and analytically productive rather than always the result of uncritical error; it treats all anachronism as a defect when in fact some historical analysis depends upon the controlled application of modern categories to illuminate the past.
passage: One might define anachronism as attributing to a historical period a concept, practice, or artefact that did not exist during that period, where the attribution is factually incorrect and results from uncritical projection. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic cases of anachronism — the historian who speaks of medieval democracy or ancient nationalism without recognising that these concepts are modern constructions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition may be constructed. Consider a historian of the Roman Empire who deliberately applies the modern concept of inflation to the economic disruptions of the third century. The historian is fully aware that the Romans had no concept of inflation in the modern sense. The attribution is, strictly speaking, factually incorrect. But the projection is not uncritical — it is a self-conscious analytical device employed to illuminate patterns in price data and currency debasement that would otherwise resist systematic description. The third condition fails, and the definition would classify this usage as non-anachronistic. Yet one might observe that even deliberate and self-aware use of modern categories to describe past conditions is a form of anachronism — a productive one, to be sure, but anachronism nonetheless. The definition, by restricting anachronism to uncritical error, cannot accommodate the widespread historiographic practice of controlled anachronism.

definition: A society S constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) S has developed a system of writing, (ii) S has established permanent urban settlements, and (iii) S exhibits a division of labour that includes specialised non-agricultural occupations.
conditions: S has developed a system of writing
S has established permanent urban settlements
S exhibits a division of labour that includes specialised non-agricultural occupations
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire at the height of its power in the fifteenth century. It had established permanent urban settlements of great sophistication — Cusco, Machu Picchu, and an extensive network of roads and administrative centres. It exhibited an elaborate division of labour encompassing architecture, metallurgy, textile production, and imperial administration. Yet the Inca did not develop a system of writing in the conventional sense; they employed quipu — knotted strings — for record-keeping. By condition (i), the Inca would be denied the status of a civilisation, a conclusion that most historians would find untenable.
missing_condition: The definition treats writing as a necessary condition for civilisation, failing to recognise that complex societies may develop functionally equivalent systems of information storage and transmission — such as quipu — that serve the same organisational purposes without constituting writing in the narrow sense.
passage: One might propose that a society constitutes a civilisation if and only if it has developed a system of writing, established permanent urban settlements, and exhibits a division of labour including specialised non-agricultural occupations. This tripartite definition is a standard formulation in much historiographic discussion. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the pre-Columbian Americas. The Inca Empire at its zenith possessed permanent urban settlements of extraordinary sophistication, an elaborate road system spanning thousands of miles, and a division of labour encompassing architecture, metallurgy, textile production, and a complex imperial bureaucracy. By conditions (ii) and (iii), it qualifies magnificently. Yet the Inca did not develop a system of writing. They maintained records through quipu — intricate arrangements of knotted strings capable of encoding numerical and possibly narrative information. By condition (i), the Inca would be excluded from the category of civilisation. One might observe that this exclusion is absurd. The definition, by treating writing as a necessary condition, privileges one particular technology of information management over functionally equivalent alternatives that served the same organisational purposes.

definition: A civilisation C is in a state of decline if and only if (i) C's political institutions are losing their capacity to maintain order and enforce law, (ii) C's cultural production is diminishing in both quantity and quality relative to a previous period, and (iii) C's population is decreasing.
conditions: C's political institutions are losing their capacity to maintain order and enforce law
C's cultural production is diminishing in both quantity and quality relative to a previous period
C's population is decreasing
counterexample: Consider the late Roman Empire in the fourth century. Its political institutions were under increasing strain. Its population in the western provinces was declining. Yet the period witnessed extraordinary cultural production — the works of Augustine, Ambrose, and Jerome, the great basilicas, the magnificent mosaics of Ravenna. Cultural output was not diminishing; in certain domains it was flourishing as never before. Condition (ii) is not met, yet few historians would deny that the western Roman Empire was in a state of decline.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that decline in one dimension — political or demographic — may coexist with flourishing in another, such as cultural production; it requires simultaneous deterioration across all three dimensions when decline is in fact compatible with selective cultural vitality.
passage: A natural definition holds that a civilisation is in decline when its political institutions lose their capacity to maintain order, its cultural production diminishes, and its population decreases. This tripartite characterisation captures the intuitive association of decline with comprehensive deterioration. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the later Roman Empire. In the fourth century, the political institutions of the western Empire were manifestly weakening — the provinces were increasingly difficult to govern, the borders increasingly porous. The population of the western provinces was declining. Yet the same period produced some of the most remarkable cultural achievements in the history of Western civilisation. Augustine composed the Confessions and the City of God; Jerome translated the scriptures into Latin; Ambrose transformed Christian hymnody; the great basilicas and mosaics of Ravenna reached new heights of artistic accomplishment. Condition (ii) is plainly unsatisfied. One might observe that the definition, by requiring simultaneous deterioration in political, cultural, and demographic dimensions, cannot accommodate the historical reality that civilisations may decline politically and demographically while experiencing cultural florescence. Decline, it appears, need not be uniform across all dimensions.

definition: An account A is an adequate historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites antecedent events that are causally relevant to E, (ii) A is consistent with the available documentary evidence, and (iii) A renders E unsurprising given the conditions described.
conditions: A cites antecedent events that are causally relevant to E
A is consistent with the available documentary evidence
A renders E unsurprising given the conditions described
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the collapse of the Western Roman Empire in the fifth century that cites the pressure of barbarian invasions, the weakening of the legions, and the fiscal exhaustion of the provinces. These antecedents are causally relevant. The account is consistent with the documentary evidence. And it renders the collapse unsurprising. All three conditions are met. Yet the explanation applies with equal force to the third century, when the Empire faced similar pressures but survived. The explanation cannot account for the timing of the collapse — why the fifth century and not the third.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the explanation discriminate between the actual occurrence and similar situations in which the event did not occur — that A identify conditions specific to E's actual circumstances that distinguish them from superficially similar cases.
passage: One might propose that an adequate historical explanation cites causally relevant antecedents, is consistent with the documentary evidence, and renders the event unsurprising. This seems to capture the essential features of explanatory adequacy in historical inquiry. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from Roman history. Consider an explanation of the collapse of the Western Roman Empire in the fifth century that cites barbarian pressure on the frontiers, the declining effectiveness of the legions, and the fiscal exhaustion of the provinces. Each of these antecedents is causally relevant. The account is consistent with the surviving sources. And it renders the collapse unsurprising — given these conditions, one would expect the Empire to falter. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the very same factors were present in the third century, during the crisis of 235 to 284, when the Empire was beset by barbarian incursions, military disorder, and fiscal collapse, but nevertheless survived and was restored under Diocletian. The explanation renders the fifth-century collapse unsurprising but cannot account for why the Empire succumbed on that occasion rather than on the earlier one. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the event be rendered unsurprising, cannot distinguish an explanation that identifies the distinctive conditions of the actual case from one that fits equally well a situation in which the event did not occur.

definition: A division of time D into periods P1, P2, ..., Pn constitutes a satisfactory periodisation of a historical process if and only if (i) the boundaries between periods correspond to moments of significant change, (ii) the periods are internally coherent in the sense that the dominant features remain relatively stable within each period, and (iii) the periods are collectively exhaustive of the time-span under consideration.
conditions: The boundaries between periods correspond to moments of significant change
The periods are internally coherent in the sense that dominant features remain relatively stable within each period
The periods are collectively exhaustive of the time-span under consideration
counterexample: Consider a periodisation of European music history that places the boundary between the Baroque and Classical periods at 1750, the year of Bach's death. The boundary corresponds to a moment of significant change — the decline of Baroque compositional techniques. The periods appear internally coherent. The periods exhaust the time-span. All three conditions are met. Yet 1750 is an arbitrary marker; the stylistic transition was gradual, with galant and empfindsamer Stil works coexisting with late Baroque compositions for decades. The periodisation imposes a sharp boundary on a continuous process.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the boundaries between periods reflect genuine discontinuities rather than convenient markers imposed upon processes that are in reality gradual and continuous.
passage: One might characterise a satisfactory periodisation as one whose boundaries correspond to moments of significant change, whose periods exhibit internal coherence, and whose periods collectively exhaust the relevant time-span. This formulation captures the expectation that periods should mark real transitions and maintain internal unity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the history of European music. The conventional periodisation places the boundary between the Baroque and Classical periods at 1750, the year of Johann Sebastian Bach's death. This moment appears to mark a significant change — the decline of the Baroque contrapuntal style. The periods seem internally coherent: Baroque music is characterised by basso continuo and elaborate ornamentation, Classical music by homophonic clarity and formal balance. The periods exhaust the time-span. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that 1750 is a conventional marker rather than a genuine discontinuity. The galant style was flourishing well before 1750. Bach's sons were composing in a new idiom while their father still lived. Baroque techniques persisted in church music long after 1750. The transition was gradual and multidimensional, not sharp. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that boundaries correspond to moments of significant change, cannot distinguish periodisations that reflect real discontinuities from those that impose convenient but arbitrary markers upon essentially continuous processes.

definition: An account N constitutes a satisfactory historical narrative of a sequence of events if and only if (i) N presents the events in their correct temporal order, (ii) N connects successive events by causal or motivational links, and (iii) N is accurate with respect to the facts it reports.
conditions: N presents the events in their correct temporal order
N connects successive events by causal or motivational links
N is accurate with respect to the facts it reports
counterexample: Consider a narrative of the Dreyfus Affair that presents every event in correct temporal order, connects each event to the next by plausible causal and motivational links, and reports no inaccurate facts. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the narrative omits entirely the role of anti-Semitism in the prosecution and the forging of documents by military officers, focusing instead on procedural irregularities. The narrative is accurate in what it says but profoundly misleading in what it omits; it presents a sanitised account that obscures the central dynamics of the affair.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the narrative include all events and factors that are essential to rendering the sequence intelligible — that it not systematically omit elements whose absence distorts the significance of the events it does report.
passage: One might define a satisfactory historical narrative as one that presents events in correct temporal order, connects them by causal or motivational links, and is factually accurate. This characterisation captures the expectations of chronological fidelity, explanatory connection, and truthfulness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from the Dreyfus Affair. Suppose a narrative of the affair presents every event in its proper order — the accusation, the court-martial, the imprisonment, the campaign for revision, the eventual exoneration. Suppose each event is connected to the next by plausible causal links: the accusation led to the trial, the trial to the imprisonment, the imprisonment to public agitation, and so forth. And suppose every fact the narrative reports is accurate. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the narrative omits entirely the role of anti-Semitic sentiment in the initial prosecution, the deliberate forging of documents by officers of the general staff, and the profound divisions the affair opened in French society. The account is accurate in everything it says. But one might observe that its omissions render it profoundly misleading. Without the anti-Semitic context, the prosecution appears as a mere procedural error. Without the forgeries, the military's resistance to revision seems merely bureaucratic. The narrative is truthful but distorted by the systematic absence of essential elements.

definition: A society S has made progress over a period T if and only if (i) S possesses greater material resources at the end of T than at the beginning, (ii) the average well-being of S's members has increased over T, and (iii) the institutions of S have become more complex and differentiated over T.
conditions: S possesses greater material resources at the end of T than at the beginning
The average well-being of S's members has increased over T
The institutions of S have become more complex and differentiated over T
counterexample: Consider the Roman Empire between the first and fifth centuries. Material resources arguably increased for a time before declining catastrophically. But suppose we restrict attention to a shorter period — say, the late Republic to the early Principate — when material resources demonstrably increased, average well-being plausibly improved with the cessation of civil wars, and institutions grew more complex through the creation of the imperial bureaucracy. All three conditions are met. Yet the institutional complexity of the Principate included the apparatus of imperial despotism — the informer networks, the proscription lists, the erosion of republican liberties. Whether this represents progress is far from clear.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the changes in question be improvements by some evaluative standard that goes beyond mere accumulation and differentiation — that the increased complexity and resources serve goods that the members of the society would, upon reflection, recognise as genuine improvements.
passage: One might characterise social progress as the conjunction of increased material resources, improved average well-being, and greater institutional complexity over a given period. This tripartite account aims to capture the material, human, and structural dimensions of advancement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from Roman history. Consider the period from the late Republic to the early Principate. Material resources increased as the Mediterranean world was unified under Roman administration. Average well-being plausibly improved with the end of the devastating civil wars that had convulsed Roman society. Institutions became more complex and differentiated through the creation of provincial governorships, a standing civil service, and an elaborate fiscal apparatus. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the institutional complexity of the Principate included features that are difficult to regard as progressive: networks of informers, the routine proscription of political opponents, the concentration of power in a single ruler unconstrained by republican checks. The institutional complexity served, in large part, the ends of autocratic control. It appears that the definition, by counting institutional complexity as inherently progressive, cannot distinguish complexity that serves desirable ends from complexity that serves oppressive ones. One should hesitate to say that a society has progressed merely because its mechanisms of control have grown more elaborate.

definition: An event E constitutes a revolution if and only if (i) E involves a rapid and fundamental change in the political or social order, (ii) E is brought about by collective action from below, and (iii) E results in a new institutional arrangement that differs qualitatively from the one it replaced.
conditions: E involves a rapid and fundamental change in the political or social order
E is brought about by collective action from below
E results in a new institutional arrangement that differs qualitatively from the one it replaced
counterexample: Consider the Meiji Restoration of 1868 in Japan. It involved a rapid and fundamental change in the political order — the dismantling of the Tokugawa shogunate and the restoration of imperial rule. It resulted in a qualitatively new institutional arrangement: a centralised state modelled on Western lines, with a modern army, a constitution, and an industrial policy. All three conditions appear met. Yet the Meiji Restoration was not brought about by collective action from below in the usual sense; it was engineered by a coalition of samurai from the western domains, acting in the name of the Emperor — an elite coup that mobilised popular sentiment but was not driven by it. If one counts this as collective action from below, the condition is too permissive; if one does not, the definition excludes a paradigmatic case of political revolution.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate revolutions engineered from above or by elite factions, thereby either excluding genuine cases of revolutionary transformation or requiring an unduly elastic interpretation of collective action from below.
passage: One might define a revolution as a rapid and fundamental change in the political or social order, brought about by collective action from below, resulting in a qualitatively new institutional arrangement. This characterisation captures the drama, popular agency, and structural novelty that we associate with revolutionary change. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from Japanese history. The Meiji Restoration of 1868 involved a rapid and fundamental transformation: the Tokugawa shogunate, which had governed Japan for over two hundred and fifty years, was swept aside and replaced by a centralised imperial state. The resulting institutional arrangement was qualitatively new — a modern bureaucracy, a conscript army, a parliament, and an industrial economy replaced the feudal order. Two conditions are plainly satisfied. Yet the transformation was not brought about by collective action from below in any ordinary sense. It was engineered by a coalition of disaffected samurai from the Satsuma, Choshu, Tosa, and Hizen domains, acting in the name of the young Emperor Meiji. Popular participation was limited and largely instrumental. One might observe that the definition, by requiring collective action from below, either excludes what is by any reasonable standard a genuine revolution or stretches the notion of action from below to the point of vacuity.

definition: A civilisation C is in a state of decline during a period T if and only if (i) C's military capacity diminishes over T, (ii) C's economic productivity decreases over T, and (iii) C's cultural output becomes derivative or imitative rather than original during T.
conditions: C's military capacity diminishes over T
C's economic productivity decreases over T
C's cultural output becomes derivative or imitative rather than original during T
counterexample: Consider the Dutch Republic in the eighteenth century. Its military capacity diminished relative to Britain and France. Its economic productivity arguably stagnated or declined from the heights of the Golden Age. Yet its cultural output in the eighteenth century included significant contributions to philosophy, science, and the book trade; the Republic remained one of Europe's leading centres of intellectual life. A rigid application of the third condition would deny this constituted decline, yet by most measures the Republic was plainly in relative decline.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that decline may be uneven across dimensions — that a civilisation may exhibit decline in military and economic capacity while maintaining or even advancing its cultural achievements, and that the concept of decline does not require simultaneous deterioration in all specified respects.
passage: One might characterise civilisational decline as a period during which military capacity diminishes, economic productivity decreases, and cultural output becomes derivative. This tripartite account aims to capture the military, material, and cultural dimensions of decay. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of the Dutch Republic. In the eighteenth century, the Republic experienced a marked decline in military power — it could no longer field the armies or navies that had sustained its independence in the seventeenth century. Its economic productivity fell from the extraordinary levels of the Golden Age, when Amsterdam had been the commercial capital of Europe. Two conditions are plainly met. Yet Dutch cultural output in the eighteenth century resists easy characterisation as derivative. The Republic remained a major centre of publishing and scholarship. Significant works of philosophy, natural history, and jurisprudence continued to issue from Dutch presses. One might observe that the definition, by requiring simultaneous deterioration in all three dimensions, cannot accommodate the historically common phenomenon of uneven decline, in which a civilisation decays along some dimensions while maintaining or even advancing along others. It appears that decline is not an all-or-nothing affair but admits of the kind of unevenness that the three conditions, taken conjunctively, cannot capture.

definition: A practice or body of customs X constitutes a genuine tradition if and only if (i) X has been transmitted across at least three generations, (ii) the practitioners of X regard it as inherited from the past, and (iii) X has remained substantially unchanged throughout the period of its transmission.
conditions: X has been transmitted across at least three generations
The practitioners of X regard it as inherited from the past
X has remained substantially unchanged throughout the period of its transmission
counterexample: Consider the Scottish Highland games, which practitioners regard as an ancient tradition inherited from the deep Celtic past. The games have been transmitted across many generations. The practitioners sincerely regard them as inherited. Yet many elements of the Highland games — including the wearing of clan tartans and the tossing of the caber as a competitive event — were substantially invented or reinvented in the nineteenth century by romantic antiquarians. The tradition has changed markedly over its history; its present form is largely a Victorian construction. If it has remained substantially unchanged, the condition is met only for the most recent period, not for the imagined continuity with the ancient past that the practitioners believe in.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the practitioners' beliefs about the antiquity and continuity of X be accurate — that X actually possess the historical continuity the practitioners attribute to it, rather than being a recent construction clothed in the appearance of antiquity.
passage: One might define a genuine tradition as a practice transmitted across at least three generations, regarded by its practitioners as inherited from the past, and substantially unchanged throughout the period of transmission. This characterisation captures the intergenerational continuity, subjective recognition, and constancy that we associate with tradition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the history of Scottish cultural practices. The Highland games are widely regarded by their practitioners as an ancient tradition inherited from the Celtic past, and they have been transmitted across many generations. The practitioners sincerely believe the games to be a continuation of immemorial custom. These conditions appear met. Yet historical inquiry reveals that many elements of the Highland games — the wearing of distinctive clan tartans, the competitive tossing of the caber, the elaborate ceremonial opening — were substantially invented or reinvented by romantic antiquarians in the nineteenth century. The tradition, in its present form, is largely a Victorian creation. The practitioners' belief in its deep antiquity is sincere but mistaken. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that practitioners regard the practice as inherited and that it remain substantially unchanged, cannot distinguish a practice that is genuinely continuous with its supposed origins from one that has been recently constructed but successfully clothed in the appearance of antiquity.

definition: A historical figure F has left a legacy L if and only if (i) F's actions or ideas have had enduring effects that persist beyond F's lifetime, (ii) the effects are recognised by subsequent generations as traceable to F, and (iii) the effects are regarded positively by those who invoke L.
conditions: F's actions or ideas have had enduring effects that persist beyond F's lifetime
The effects are recognised by subsequent generations as traceable to F
The effects are regarded positively by those who invoke L
counterexample: Consider the legacy of Genghis Khan. His actions had enduring effects — the political unification of Central Asia, the opening of trade routes, the genetic imprint upon millions. These effects are recognised as traceable to him. Yet whether these effects are regarded positively depends entirely upon the community of evaluation: Mongolian nationalists revere him, while the populations of cities he destroyed view his legacy with horror. The third condition is met for some communities and not for others, rendering the definition indeterminate.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the fact that a single figure's legacy may be evaluated differently by different communities — it wrongly assumes that the evaluative attitude toward the legacy is uniform, when in practice legacies are contested and their positive or negative character is a matter of perspective.
passage: One might propose that a historical figure has left a legacy when the figure's actions or ideas have had enduring effects, those effects are recognised as traceable to the figure, and the effects are regarded positively by those who invoke them. This characterisation aims to capture the durability, attributability, and value of historical influence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the history of the Mongol Empire. Genghis Khan's actions had enduring effects that persist centuries beyond his lifetime — the political reorganisation of Central Asia, the establishment of trade routes linking East and West, the genetic legacy detectable in modern populations. These effects are recognised by subsequent generations as traceable to him. The first two conditions are plainly met. Yet the third condition — positive regard — yields contradictory verdicts depending upon the community of evaluation. Mongolian nationalists revere Genghis Khan as the founder of their nation. The descendants of the populations of Samarkand, Baghdad, and Merv, cities that suffered devastating destruction, view his legacy with horror. One might observe that the definition, by requiring positive regard without specifying whose regard is relevant, cannot accommodate the pervasive historical phenomenon of contested legacies — cases in which the same enduring effects are celebrated by some communities and condemned by others.

definition: A human community C constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) C has developed a system of writing, (ii) C maintains urban centres with a substantial non-agricultural population, and (iii) C possesses a centralised political authority governing a defined territory.
conditions: C has developed a system of writing
C maintains urban centres with a substantial non-agricultural population
C possesses a centralised political authority governing a defined territory
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire at the height of its power in the fifteenth century. It maintained vast urban centres such as Cusco, with a substantial non-agricultural population of administrators, artisans, and priests. It possessed a highly centralised political authority — the Sapa Inca — governing a territory stretching along the Andes for thousands of miles. Two conditions are plainly met. Yet the Inca did not possess a system of writing in the conventional sense; they employed the quipu, a system of knotted cords, for record-keeping and communication. If the quipu counts as writing, the first condition is trivially satisfied; if it does not, the definition excludes one of the most sophisticated polities in human history from the category of civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate sophisticated systems of record-keeping, administration, and cultural transmission that do not take the form of conventional writing — it privileges one particular technology of information storage over functionally equivalent alternatives.
passage: One might characterise a civilisation as a human community that has developed writing, maintains urban centres with a non-agricultural population, and possesses centralised political authority over a defined territory. This tripartite account captures the cultural, demographic, and political features traditionally associated with civilisation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the pre-Columbian Americas. The Inca Empire at its zenith in the fifteenth century maintained urban centres of great sophistication — Cusco alone supported a substantial population of administrators, artisans, soldiers, and religious specialists. The political authority of the Sapa Inca extended over a territory stretching more than two thousand five hundred miles along the Andes, governed through an elaborate system of provincial administration. Two conditions are amply satisfied. Yet the Inca did not develop a system of writing in the conventional sense. They employed the quipu — a system of knotted cords of varying colour, length, and arrangement — for the recording of information and the administration of the empire. Whether the quipu constitutes writing is a matter of ongoing scholarly debate. One might observe that the definition, by requiring writing as a necessary condition, either excludes the Inca Empire from the category of civilisation — a result that most historians would regard as absurd — or requires an interpretation of writing so broad as to deprive the condition of analytical force.

definition: A historical interpretation I is anachronistic if and only if (i) I attributes to historical agents concepts, motives, or categories that were not available in their time, (ii) the attribution distorts the meaning of the agents' actions, and (iii) the interpreter fails to recognise the temporal displacement involved.
conditions: I attributes to historical agents concepts, motives, or categories that were not available in their time
The attribution distorts the meaning of the agents' actions
The interpreter fails to recognise the temporal displacement involved
counterexample: Consider a historian who deliberately and self-consciously applies the modern concept of inflation to analyse the price revolution of the sixteenth century, explicitly acknowledging that sixteenth-century actors did not possess this concept and that its application is an interpretive device rather than a description of their understanding. The historian attributes a concept unavailable to the agents. One might argue the attribution distorts the agents' own understanding of their economic situation. Yet the historian fully recognises the temporal displacement involved. The third condition is not met. Thus the definition would not classify this as anachronistic, even though the first two conditions — the attribution and the distortion — are present and arguably sufficient to warrant the charge of anachronism in the interpretation of meaning.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between anachronism in the interpretation of agents' meanings and the legitimate use of later concepts as analytical tools — it wrongly makes the interpreter's self-awareness the decisive factor, when the real issue is whether the attribution is presented as a claim about the agents' own understanding.
passage: One might define anachronism in historical interpretation as the attribution to historical agents of concepts or categories unavailable in their time, where this attribution distorts the meaning of their actions and the interpreter fails to recognise the temporal displacement. This tripartite characterisation aims to separate innocent uses of modern terminology from genuinely distorting misreadings. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises from economic history. Suppose a historian analyses the sixteenth-century price revolution using the modern concept of inflation. The historian explicitly acknowledges that sixteenth-century merchants and rulers did not think in terms of inflation, that the concept belongs to a later theoretical framework, and that its application is a deliberate interpretive device rather than a description of how the actors understood their situation. The historian attributes a concept unavailable to the agents — the first condition is met. One might argue that the attribution distorts the agents' self-understanding — the second condition is arguably met. But the historian fully recognises the temporal displacement — the third condition is not satisfied. The definition therefore classifies the interpretation as non-anachronistic. Yet one might observe that the distortion of the agents' meaning persists regardless of the interpreter's self-awareness. The issue is not whether the historian knows that the concept is later but whether the concept is being presented as an account of how the agents understood their own actions. It appears that the definition, by making self-awareness the decisive condition, cannot distinguish between the legitimate analytical use of later concepts and their illegitimate projection onto agents' meanings.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies antecedent conditions from which E can be expected to follow, (ii) the antecedent conditions cited in A are supported by documentary evidence, and (iii) A presents the antecedent conditions in their correct temporal sequence.
conditions: A identifies antecedent conditions from which E can be expected to follow
The antecedent conditions cited in A are supported by documentary evidence
A presents the antecedent conditions in their correct temporal sequence
counterexample: Consider an historian who explains the outbreak of a particular war by citing the preceding harvest failure, the rise in bread prices, and the consequent popular unrest — all documented and presented in correct temporal order. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the war was actually precipitated by a diplomatic insult delivered in secret between two monarchs, a cause entirely unrelated to the economic conditions. The explanation cites real, documented, temporally ordered antecedents from which war might be expected, yet misidentifies the actual cause.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the antecedent conditions cited in A be causally connected to E — that they constitute the factors that actually brought about the event, rather than merely preceding it in a plausible but causally inert sequence.
passage: One might propose that a satisfactory historical explanation identifies documented antecedent conditions from which the event can be expected to follow, presented in correct temporal order. This formulation demands empirical grounding and chronological accuracy, which are surely desirable features of historical accounts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the historiography of war. Suppose an historian, seeking to explain the outbreak of a particular eighteenth-century conflict, cites a sequence of economic misfortunes: a disastrous harvest, a sharp rise in the price of bread, widespread popular unrest, and finally the outbreak of hostilities. Each of these antecedent conditions is amply documented. They are presented in their correct temporal order. And from such conditions one might well expect a war to follow. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet suppose the war was in fact precipitated by a secret diplomatic insult exchanged between two monarchs at a private audience — an insult that has since come to light through the discovery of previously unknown correspondence. The economic distress, though real and temporally antecedent, played no causal role in the outbreak. One might observe that the explanation, though it meets every stated condition, misidentifies the actual cause of the event. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the antecedent conditions be documented, temporally ordered, and such that the event could be expected to follow, cannot distinguish explanations that identify the genuine causes from those that cite plausible but causally inert antecedents.

definition: A transition from one historical period to another constitutes genuine historical progress if and only if (i) the later period exhibits greater material prosperity than the earlier, (ii) the later period possesses more advanced technology than the earlier, and (iii) the later period has a larger population than the earlier.
conditions: The later period exhibits greater material prosperity than the earlier
The later period possesses more advanced technology than the earlier
The later period has a larger population than the earlier
counterexample: Consider the transition from a small agrarian republic to a large industrial empire. The empire is wealthier, possesses more advanced technology, and has a vastly larger population. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the empire sustains its prosperity through slave labour, its technology is devoted chiefly to instruments of oppression, and its population growth is the result of conquest rather than voluntary increase. We should hesitate to call this transition an instance of genuine progress, for the human cost and moral regression seem to overwhelm the material and technological gains.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the gains in prosperity, technology, and population be accompanied by a corresponding advancement in moral or political conditions — that the benefits be distributed in a manner consistent with the dignity and welfare of the persons who compose the society.
passage: One might characterise genuine historical progress as a transition in which the later period exceeds the earlier in material prosperity, technological advancement, and population. This characterisation captures the quantitative improvements that many historians have taken as indicators of progressive development. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from comparative political history. Suppose a small agrarian republic, possessed of modest wealth and simple technology, is conquered and absorbed by a large industrial empire. The empire is manifestly wealthier: its cities are grander, its commerce more extensive, its treasury more ample. Its technology is more advanced: it commands the production of iron on an industrial scale, constructs elaborate infrastructure, and equips formidable armies. Its population is vastly larger. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the empire sustains its material prosperity through the systematic use of slave labour, devotes its advanced technology chiefly to the manufacture of instruments of coercion, and owes its population growth to military conquest rather than to any improvement in the conditions of human life. The small republic, for all its comparative poverty, afforded its citizens a measure of liberty and self-governance that the empire has extinguished. It appears that the definition, by measuring progress exclusively in material, technological, and demographic terms, cannot distinguish transitions that represent genuine advancement in the human condition from those that achieve quantitative gains at the cost of moral and political regression.

definition: A division of the historical continuum into distinct periods is well-founded if and only if (i) each period is characterised by a set of features that obtain throughout its duration, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant changes in those features, and (iii) the features used to characterise the periods are drawn from the political life of the societies in question.
conditions: Each period is characterised by a set of features that obtain throughout its duration
The boundaries between periods correspond to significant changes in those features
The features used to characterise the periods are drawn from the political life of the societies in question
counterexample: Consider a periodisation of European history that divides it according to the form of government: monarchy, republic, constitutional monarchy. Suppose the features are drawn from political life, they obtain throughout each designated period, and the boundaries correspond to significant political changes. All three conditions are met. Yet this periodisation ignores the fact that during certain of these politically uniform periods, profound transformations occurred in economic organisation, religious life, and intellectual culture — transformations that arguably define the character of the age more fundamentally than the form of government.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the features used for periodisation capture the most historically significant dimensions of change — that the chosen features not exclude other aspects of social, economic, or cultural life whose transformations are equally or more fundamental to the character of the periods in question.
passage: One might propose that a well-founded periodisation divides the historical continuum into segments characterised by features drawn from political life, where these features obtain throughout each period and change significantly at the boundaries. This formulation ensures internal coherence, clear demarcation, and empirical grounding in the observable political institutions of the societies under study. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from European historiography. Suppose one divides European history into periods defined by the prevailing form of government — an age of absolute monarchy, followed by an age of republican experimentation, followed by an age of constitutional monarchy. The political features do obtain throughout each designated period. The boundaries correspond to significant political transitions — revolutions, restorations, constitutional settlements. The features are plainly drawn from political life. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this periodisation obscures transformations of the first importance. During the age of absolute monarchy, the economic organisation of European society underwent a fundamental shift from feudal agriculture to mercantile commerce. During the age of republican experimentation, a revolution in natural philosophy transformed the intellectual landscape. These economic and intellectual changes are arguably more significant to the character of the respective ages than the form of government. It appears that the definition, by restricting the features of periodisation to political life, cannot distinguish periodisations that capture the most historically significant dimensions of change from those that privilege one dimension while ignoring others of equal or greater importance.

definition: An account A constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) A recounts a sequence of events drawn from the historical record, (ii) the events recounted in A are connected by temporal succession, and (iii) A culminates in an outcome that is presented as the natural terminus of the sequence.
conditions: A recounts a sequence of events drawn from the historical record
The events recounted in A are connected by temporal succession
A culminates in an outcome that is presented as the natural terminus of the sequence
counterexample: Consider a narrative that recounts, in strict temporal order, the construction of a cathedral in the twelfth century, a famine in the thirteenth century, and a naval battle in the fifteenth century, presenting the battle as the natural culmination of the preceding events. The events are drawn from the historical record. They are connected by temporal succession. The narrative presents the battle as the terminus of the sequence. All three conditions are met. Yet the narrative is not legitimate, for the events are causally and thematically unrelated; the appearance of a coherent story has been manufactured by the historian's selection and arrangement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the events recounted in A be connected by causal, thematic, or explanatory relations rather than by mere temporal succession — that the narrative possess genuine coherence arising from the relationships among the events, not merely from their arrangement in chronological order.
passage: One might define a legitimate historical narrative as an account that recounts a temporally successive sequence of events drawn from the historical record and culminates in an outcome presented as the natural terminus of the sequence. This formulation demands factual basis, chronological order, and narrative closure. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by juxtaposition. Suppose an historian composes a narrative that begins with the construction of a great cathedral in the twelfth century, proceeds to a severe famine in the thirteenth century, and concludes with a naval battle in the fifteenth century. The historian presents the naval battle as the natural culmination of the preceding events. Each event is drawn from the historical record. The events are arranged in strict temporal succession. And the narrative does culminate in a presented terminus. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this is not a legitimate historical narrative. The construction of a cathedral, a famine, and a naval battle — however accurately reported — are causally and thematically unrelated. The appearance of narrative coherence is an artefact of the historian's selection and arrangement, not a reflection of genuine connections among the events. It appears that the definition, by requiring only temporal succession and a presented culmination, cannot distinguish narratives whose coherence arises from genuine causal or thematic relations among the events from those whose apparent unity is manufactured by selective chronological juxtaposition.

definition: A society S constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) S has developed a system of writing, (ii) S has constructed permanent urban settlements, and (iii) S maintains a division of labour that includes specialised occupations beyond subsistence agriculture.
conditions: S has developed a system of writing
S has constructed permanent urban settlements
S maintains a division of labour that includes specialised occupations beyond subsistence agriculture
counterexample: Consider the Inca empire, which constructed magnificent urban centres such as Cusco and Machu Picchu, maintained a highly elaborate division of labour including specialised administrators, engineers, and artisans, yet never developed a system of writing in the conventional sense, relying instead on the quipu — a system of knotted cords for recording information. The Inca satisfy conditions (ii) and (iii) but fail to satisfy condition (i) as stated. Yet the Inca empire is universally regarded as a civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the function served by writing — the recording, transmission, and organisation of complex information — may be fulfilled by alternative symbolic or material systems, so that the requirement ought to be not for writing specifically but for some effective means of recording and transmitting complex information.
passage: One might propose that a civilisation is a society that has developed a system of writing, constructed permanent urban settlements, and maintains a division of labour including specialised occupations. This characterisation captures features that have often been regarded as the hallmarks of civilised life, distinguishing complex societies from simpler ones. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of the Americas. The Inca empire of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries constructed magnificent urban centres — Cusco, with its precisely fitted stonework, and the mountain citadel of Machu Picchu are among the most remarkable achievements of pre-Columbian architecture. The empire maintained an extraordinarily elaborate division of labour, with specialised administrators, engineers, architects, artisans, and agricultural managers overseeing a complex economy that stretched across thousands of miles. Yet the Inca never developed a system of writing in the conventional sense of the term. They recorded and transmitted information by means of the quipu, a system of knotted and coloured cords whose interpretation required trained specialists. The Inca satisfy conditions (ii) and (iii) of the proposed definition but not condition (i). Yet one might observe that the Inca empire is universally recognised as a civilisation, and rightly so. Its administrative complexity, monumental architecture, and economic sophistication are comparable to those of any literate society of the same period. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon writing rather than upon the broader function that writing serves — the recording and transmission of complex information — excludes societies that have achieved that function by alternative means.

definition: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political authority, (ii) E is accomplished through the use of force or the credible threat of force, and (iii) E results in the establishment of a new political order fundamentally different from the old.
conditions: E involves the overthrow of an existing political authority
E is accomplished through the use of force or the credible threat of force
E results in the establishment of a new political order fundamentally different from the old
counterexample: Consider a military coup in which a general overthrows the president by force and establishes a new regime with a different constitution, a different legislature, and a different system of administration. The existing authority is overthrown. Force is employed. A new political order, fundamentally different from the old, is established. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this a genuine revolution, for the coup merely substitutes one authoritarian ruler for another without engaging the participation of the populace or addressing the underlying social and economic conditions; it is a palace revolution rather than a genuine transformation of society.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that E involve broad popular participation or mobilisation — that the transformation extend beyond the mere substitution of one ruling group for another and engage the wider society in the reshaping of political and social arrangements.
passage: One might define a genuine revolution as an event involving the forcible overthrow of an existing political authority and the establishment of a fundamentally new political order. This characterisation captures the violence and transformative character that we associate with revolutionary episodes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the familiar phenomenon of the military coup. Suppose a general, commanding the loyalty of the army, deposes the reigning president by force. A new constitution is promulgated. A new legislature is convened. A new system of administration is installed. The old political order is replaced by one that is, in its formal structure, fundamentally different. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met: the existing authority has been overthrown, force has been employed, and a new order has been established. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should hesitate to call this event a genuine revolution. The populace has played no part in the transformation. The social and economic conditions that prevailed under the old regime remain entirely unchanged. The new order differs from the old only in the identity and institutional arrangements of the ruling group, while the fundamental relationships between the governors and the governed are unaltered. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the overthrow of authority, the use of force, and the establishment of a new order, cannot distinguish genuine revolutions — which engage broad popular participation and transform the social fabric — from palace coups that merely rearrange the personnel of power.

definition: A practice P constitutes a genuine historical tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a significant period, (ii) P is recognised by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past, and (iii) P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted from one generation to the next over a significant period
P is recognised by its practitioners as having been inherited from the past
P has remained substantially unchanged throughout its transmission
counterexample: Consider a ceremony invented in 1920 by a patriotic society, designed to look ancient, and passed down through three generations. Its practitioners sincerely believe they have inherited it from the distant past. The ceremony has remained unchanged since its invention. All three conditions are met. Yet the tradition is spurious — it was fabricated a mere century ago and given a false pedigree; the practitioners' belief in its antiquity is mistaken.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the historical pedigree attributed to P by its practitioners be genuine — that P actually originate in the historical past to which it is attributed, rather than being a modern invention furnished with a fictitious ancestry.
passage: One might propose that a genuine historical tradition is a practice transmitted across generations over a significant period, recognised by its practitioners as inherited from the past, and substantially unchanged throughout its transmission. This characterisation captures the intergenerational continuity, self-awareness, and stability that we associate with traditional practices. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of invented traditions. Suppose a patriotic society in the year 1920 devises a solemn ceremony — complete with archaic costumes, prescribed gestures, and a fixed liturgy — designed to appear as though it dates from the medieval period. The ceremony is transmitted faithfully from the founding generation to the next, and thence to the next again. Each generation of practitioners sincerely believes the ceremony to have been inherited from the distant past; they have been told so by their predecessors. And the ceremony has remained substantially unchanged since its invention. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this is not a genuine historical tradition. It was fabricated in living memory and furnished with a false pedigree. The practitioners' belief in its antiquity, however sincere, is mistaken. It appears that the definition, by requiring only transmission, recognition, and stability, cannot distinguish genuine traditions — whose historical pedigree is authentic — from invented traditions that satisfy all the stated conditions while possessing a fabricated ancestry.

definition: A society S is in a state of historical decline if and only if (i) S exhibits a sustained decrease in economic output over a significant period, (ii) S suffers a contraction in the territory it controls, and (iii) S experiences a weakening of its military capacity relative to its rivals.
conditions: S exhibits a sustained decrease in economic output over a significant period
S suffers a contraction in the territory it controls
S experiences a weakening of its military capacity relative to its rivals
counterexample: Consider a large empire that, over the course of a century, deliberately relinquishes distant and unprofitable provinces, contracts its military to focus on defence of the core territory, and thereby suffers a decrease in total economic output. The economy of the core, however, flourishes as never before; literacy rises, public health improves, the arts prosper, and the citizens enjoy a standard of living far superior to that of the imperial period. All three conditions of decline are met. Yet we should hesitate to call this a period of decline, for the society has exchanged territorial and military overextension for a marked improvement in the quality of life of its citizens.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the decreases in economic output, territory, and military capacity be accompanied by a deterioration in the well-being, cultural vitality, or institutional health of the society — that the contraction represent a genuine loss rather than a beneficial retrenchment.
passage: One might characterise historical decline as a sustained decrease in a society's economic output, a contraction of its territory, and a weakening of its military capacity relative to its rivals. This formulation captures the material and strategic indicators that historians have often associated with the decline of great powers. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of imperial retrenchment. Suppose a large empire, recognising that its distant provinces are costly to maintain and difficult to defend, deliberately relinquishes them over the course of a century. The total economic output of the empire decreases, since the output of the abandoned provinces is no longer counted. The territory contracts significantly. The military, no longer required to garrison far-flung outposts, is reduced in size, and its capacity relative to certain rising neighbours diminishes. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that the core of the empire flourishes as never before. Freed from the burden of imperial overextension, the central provinces experience a remarkable improvement in public health, literacy, and the arts. The standard of living enjoyed by the citizens rises substantially. It appears that the definition, by measuring decline exclusively in terms of economic output, territory, and military capacity, cannot distinguish genuine decline — in which the society deteriorates in the quality of its common life — from a beneficial retrenchment in which quantitative contraction is accompanied by qualitative improvement.

definition: An historical interpretation I is anachronistic if and only if (i) I attributes to the agents of a past period a concept C, (ii) C was not available in the intellectual milieu of that period, and (iii) the attribution of C distorts our understanding of the agents' actions or beliefs.
conditions: I attributes to the agents of a past period a concept C
C was not available in the intellectual milieu of that period
The attribution of C distorts our understanding of the agents' actions or beliefs
counterexample: Consider an historian who attributes to a medieval monk the concept of charity, arguing that the monk's distribution of alms to the poor was motivated by charitable feeling. The concept of charity was certainly available in the medieval intellectual milieu. Thus condition (ii) is not met, and the interpretation is not anachronistic by this definition. Yet suppose the historian means by charity the specifically modern, secular notion of philanthropic social welfare, which was not available in the medieval period. If the historian uses the word in its medieval sense, the interpretation is not anachronistic; if in its modern sense, it is. The definition cannot distinguish these two cases because it treats the availability of a concept as a simple yes-or-no matter, when in fact concepts undergo significant transformation across periods.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the analysis attend to the specific sense or content of the concept attributed — that it distinguish between a concept's being available in name and its being available with the particular content the interpreter assigns to it, since the same term may carry profoundly different meanings in different periods.
passage: One might define an anachronistic interpretation as one that attributes to past agents a concept unavailable in their intellectual milieu, where the attribution distorts our understanding. This formulation aims to guard against the projection of later ideas onto earlier periods. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of charitable practice. Consider an historian who attributes to a twelfth-century Benedictine monk the concept of charity, citing the monk's well-documented practice of distributing alms to the poor. The concept of charity was certainly present in the medieval intellectual milieu — the Latin caritas was a central theological virtue. Thus condition (ii) appears unsatisfied, and the interpretation seems safe from the charge of anachronism. Yet suppose the historian intends by charity not the medieval theological virtue of caritas, directed towards God and neighbour as an expression of divine love, but rather the specifically modern and secular notion of philanthropic social welfare — the systematic amelioration of poverty through organised institutional action. This modern conception was not available in the twelfth century. The historian's use of the word charity, though the term existed in the medieval period, carries a content that belongs to a later age. One might observe that the definition, by treating the availability of a concept as a simple matter of whether the term exists in a period, cannot distinguish cases in which the term is used in its original sense from cases in which it is used in a later sense that significantly transforms its content. It appears that the analysis must attend not merely to the presence of a concept but to the specific content with which it is deployed.
