definition: A past event or institution X has left a genuine historical legacy in a later period if and only if (i) features of the later period resemble features of X, (ii) the agents of the later period are aware of X, and (iii) the agents of the later period explicitly invoke X as a model or precedent.
conditions: Features of the later period resemble features of X
The agents of the later period are aware of X
The agents of the later period explicitly invoke X as a model or precedent
counterexample: Consider a modern republic whose constitution bears a strong structural resemblance to that of a particular ancient republic. The modern founders were aware of the ancient republic and explicitly invoked it as a model during their constitutional deliberations. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose historical research reveals that the structural resemblances are accidental — the modern founders, despite invoking the ancient republic as an inspiration, actually drew their specific constitutional provisions from an entirely different and more recent source, and the resemblances to the ancient model are coincidental. The ancient republic's legacy is claimed but not genuine.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that there be a genuine causal connection between X and the later features — that the resemblances be produced by the influence of X rather than by independent processes that happen to yield similar results, and that the invocation of X be more than merely rhetorical.
passage: One might propose that a past event or institution has left a genuine legacy in a later period when features of the later period resemble it, the later agents are aware of it, and they explicitly invoke it as a model. This characterisation captures the resemblance, awareness, and self-conscious appropriation that we associate with the transmission of historical legacies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from constitutional history. Suppose a modern republic adopts a constitution whose structure — a bicameral legislature, an elected executive, a system of checks upon each branch — bears a striking resemblance to that of a particular ancient republic. The founders of the modern republic were well aware of the ancient precedent and repeatedly invoked it during their deliberations as an inspiration and model. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet suppose that careful historical investigation reveals the resemblances to be accidental. The modern founders, for all their rhetorical invocations, actually drew their specific provisions from an entirely different and more recent source — the constitution of a neighbouring state. The structural resemblances to the ancient republic are coincidental, and the invocations were exercises in persuasive rhetoric rather than descriptions of actual intellectual debts. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only resemblance, awareness, and invocation, cannot distinguish genuine legacies — in which the past causally shapes the present — from cases in which resemblance is accidental and invocation is merely rhetorical.

definition: A society S exhibits historical progress over a period if and only if (i) S shows measurable improvement in material conditions of life, (ii) S shows expansion in the range of opportunities available to its members, and (iii) these improvements are sustained over the period in question.
conditions: S shows measurable improvement in material conditions of life
S shows expansion in the range of opportunities available to its members
These improvements are sustained over the period in question
counterexample: Consider a colonial society in which the ruling class experiences measurable improvements in material comfort — better housing, greater access to imported goods, improved medical care — and an expanding range of opportunities, including new forms of commerce, education, and political participation. These improvements are sustained over several decades. All three conditions are satisfied for the society taken as a whole, since the measured indicators of material well-being and opportunity do improve on average. Yet the improvements accrue exclusively to the colonial elite, while the indigenous population suffers declining material conditions, loss of traditional livelihoods, and systematic exclusion from the new opportunities. We should hesitate to say that the society as a whole has made progress.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the improvements be distributed across the membership of the society in a manner that does not involve the systematic exclusion or degradation of any substantial portion of its members.
passage: One might propose that a society exhibits historical progress when it shows sustained measurable improvements in material conditions and an expansion in available opportunities. This characterisation captures the material and practical dimensions of what we ordinarily mean by social advancement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the history of colonial societies. Consider a colony in which the ruling class enjoys steadily improving material conditions over several decades: better housing, greater access to imported goods, improved medical care. The range of opportunities available to members of the ruling class also expands: new forms of commerce emerge, educational institutions are established, and political participation broadens. These improvements are sustained throughout the period. If one measures the society's material conditions and opportunities by aggregating across the population, the averages show marked improvement, so all three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the improvements accrue exclusively to the colonial elite. The indigenous population, comprising the majority of the society, suffers declining material conditions: traditional lands are seized, customary livelihoods are disrupted, and access to the new forms of commerce and education is systematically denied. The average improvement masks a profound asymmetry in which the advancement of one group is purchased at the expense of another. It appears that the definition, by requiring only aggregate improvement and sustained expansion of opportunities, fails to demand that progress be distributed in a manner that does not involve the systematic exclusion or degradation of a substantial portion of the society's members. Without such a distributive requirement, what passes for progress may be indistinguishable from the enrichment of a privileged few at the cost of the many.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies a set of antecedent conditions that preceded E, (ii) A cites a general principle connecting conditions of that type with events of the type of E, and (iii) the antecedent conditions cited in A are supported by documentary evidence.
conditions: A identifies a set of antecedent conditions that preceded E
A cites a general principle connecting conditions of that type with events of the type of E
The antecedent conditions cited in A are supported by documentary evidence
counterexample: Suppose a historian explains the outbreak of a particular peasant revolt by identifying the antecedent conditions of rising grain prices and increased taxation, citing the general principle that economic hardship of this kind regularly produces popular unrest, and supporting these claims with documentary evidence from tax records and market accounts. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the revolt in question was actually triggered by a specific rumour — a false report that the king had been overthrown — which emboldened the peasants to act, and that without this rumour the economic hardship alone would not have produced revolt at that time. The explanation identifies genuine background conditions but misses the proximate cause.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the antecedent conditions cited in A include the causally decisive factors — the conditions without which E would not have occurred at the time and in the manner it did — rather than merely citing background conditions that are generally associated with events of that type.
passage: One might propose that a satisfactory historical explanation identifies antecedent conditions supported by documentary evidence and connects them to the event via a general principle. This characterisation captures the evidential and nomological dimensions that historians and philosophers of history have often demanded of good explanatory accounts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the explanation of popular uprisings. Suppose a historian seeks to explain a particular peasant revolt that occurred in a rural province in the eighteenth century. She identifies the antecedent conditions of rising grain prices and newly imposed taxation, citing tax records and market accounts as documentary evidence. She invokes the general principle, well confirmed by historical precedent, that severe economic hardship of this kind regularly produces popular unrest. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. The antecedent conditions are documented, a general principle is cited, and the conditions precede the event. Nevertheless, one might observe that the revolt in question was actually triggered by a specific false rumour — a report, circulated among the peasantry, that the king had been overthrown and that the local authorities no longer possessed legitimate power. It was this rumour, not the economic hardship as such, that emboldened the peasants to take up arms when they did. Without the rumour, the hardship might have continued for years without producing revolt. The explanation identifies genuine background conditions but fails to isolate the causally decisive factor — the proximate trigger without which the event would not have occurred at that time. It appears that the definition, by requiring only documented antecedent conditions and a general connecting principle, fails to demand the identification of the conditions that were actually decisive in bringing about the event.

definition: A division of history into periods P1, P2, ... Pn is adequate if and only if (i) each period is characterised by a distinctive set of features that differentiate it from adjacent periods, (ii) the transitions between periods correspond to significant changes in those features, and (iii) the periodisation covers the entire temporal span under consideration without gaps or overlaps.
conditions: Each period is characterised by a distinctive set of features that differentiate it from adjacent periods
The transitions between periods correspond to significant changes in those features
The periodisation covers the entire temporal span under consideration without gaps or overlaps
counterexample: Consider a periodisation of European history that divides it into an Age of Agriculture (before 1750), an Age of Industry (1750–1950), and an Age of Information (1950–present), based on the dominant mode of economic production. Each period has distinctive economic features. The transitions correspond to significant shifts in productive technology. The periodisation covers the entire span without gaps or overlaps. All three conditions are met. Yet this periodisation obscures the fact that vast regions of Europe remained predominantly agricultural well into the twentieth century, and that the transition to industrial production occurred at markedly different times in different countries. The periodisation imposes a false uniformity on a process that was in reality highly uneven.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the periods reflect the actual distribution and timing of the characterising features across the relevant populations and regions — that the periodisation not impose a spurious temporal uniformity upon processes that unfold at different rates in different parts of the domain.
passage: One might propose that an adequate periodisation divides history into segments each characterised by distinctive features, with transitions corresponding to significant changes in those features, and covering the entire temporal span without gaps or overlaps. This formulation captures the differentiating, transitional, and comprehensive requirements that we associate with useful historical periodisation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the periodisation of European economic history. Suppose one divides European history into an Age of Agriculture, extending to roughly 1750; an Age of Industry, from 1750 to 1950; and an Age of Information, from 1950 to the present. Each period is characterised by a distinctive dominant mode of economic production. The transitions correspond to significant technological shifts: the mechanisation of production and the subsequent rise of computing. The periodisation covers the entire span of European history without gaps or overlaps. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this periodisation imposes a false uniformity upon a process that was in reality profoundly uneven. Large regions of southern and eastern Europe remained predominantly agricultural well into the twentieth century. The transition to industrial production occurred decades or even centuries later in some countries than in others. The neat boundary at 1750 reflects the experience of England far more than that of Spain or Romania. It appears that the definition, by requiring only distinctive features, significant transitions, and temporal completeness, fails to demand that the periodisation reflect the actual distribution and timing of the characterising features across the relevant domain. Without such a requirement, the periodisation may impose a spurious uniformity that obscures more than it reveals.

definition: A community C constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) C possesses a system of writing, (ii) C maintains complex political institutions with a defined hierarchy of authority, and (iii) C supports specialised occupational roles beyond subsistence agriculture.
conditions: C possesses a system of writing
C maintains complex political institutions with a defined hierarchy of authority
C supports specialised occupational roles beyond subsistence agriculture
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire prior to the Spanish conquest. The Inca maintained extraordinarily complex political institutions with a defined hierarchy extending from the Sapa Inca through regional governors to local administrators. The empire supported highly specialised occupational roles: engineers, astronomers, textile artisans, and administrators. Yet the Inca did not possess writing in the conventional sense; their system of record-keeping relied upon quipu — knotted strings — which, however sophisticated, is not a system of writing by most standard definitions. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is not. Yet we should not hesitate to call the Inca Empire a civilisation.
missing_condition: The definition errs in making the possession of a writing system a necessary condition, thereby excluding societies that achieve equivalent levels of social complexity, political organisation, and cultural sophistication through alternative systems of information storage and transmission.
passage: One might define a civilisation as a community possessing a system of writing, maintaining complex political institutions with a defined hierarchy of authority, and supporting specialised occupational roles beyond subsistence agriculture. This characterisation captures the literate, politically organised, and economically differentiated character that we commonly associate with civilised societies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the pre-Columbian Americas. The Inca Empire, prior to the Spanish conquest, maintained political institutions of extraordinary complexity: a hierarchy extending from the Sapa Inca at its apex through four regional governors, provincial administrators, and local headmen, all coordinated by an elaborate system of tribute, labour obligations, and census-taking. The empire supported a remarkable array of specialised occupational roles — engineers who constructed roads and bridges across some of the most difficult terrain on earth, astronomers who tracked celestial cycles, textile artisans who produced fabrics of astonishing intricacy, and administrators who managed the logistics of an empire spanning thousands of miles. Conditions (ii) and (iii) of the proposed definition are amply satisfied. Yet the Inca did not possess writing in the conventional sense. Their system of record-keeping employed quipu — knotted strings of varying colour, length, and knot-type — a device of considerable sophistication but not a system of writing by standard definitions. One might observe that to deny the Inca the designation of civilisation on this ground would be to mistake a particular technology for a necessary condition, when the underlying function — the storage and transmission of complex information — was achieved by alternative means. The definition, it appears, errs in elevating one particular medium of information management to the status of a defining requirement.

definition: A society S is in a state of decline over a period if and only if (i) S shows a measurable deterioration in its institutional capacity to maintain order and provide collective goods, (ii) S shows a contraction in cultural production relative to earlier periods, and (iii) these deteriorations are not the result of external conquest or natural catastrophe.
conditions: S shows a measurable deterioration in its institutional capacity to maintain order and provide collective goods
S shows a contraction in cultural production relative to earlier periods
These deteriorations are not the result of external conquest or natural catastrophe
counterexample: Consider a medieval Italian city-state in which the old republican institutions lose their capacity to maintain order, as rival factions seize control and traditional collective goods — public festivals, communal granaries, civic defence — fall into disrepair. Cultural production in the traditional forms — ecclesiastical art, Latin verse, scholastic disputation — contracts markedly. These deteriorations are not the result of external conquest or natural catastrophe; they arise from internal political fragmentation. All three conditions are met. Yet the same period witnesses an extraordinary flourishing of new forms of cultural production: vernacular literature, secular painting, and humanistic scholarship that will come to be regarded as the beginnings of a cultural renaissance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the deterioration in cultural production be assessed across all forms of cultural activity, including emergent ones, rather than only across the forms characteristic of the earlier period — thereby distinguishing genuine decline from the transformation of a society's cultural life.
passage: One might propose that a society is in decline when it shows measurable deterioration in institutional capacity, contraction in cultural production, and these deteriorations are not attributable to external conquest or natural catastrophe. This characterisation captures the internal, self-generated nature of decline and distinguishes it from destruction imposed from without. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of the Italian city-states. Consider a medieval Italian commune in which the old republican institutions gradually lose their capacity to maintain order. Rival factions seize power in succession; the communal granary falls into disuse; civic festivals are abandoned; public defence is neglected. Cultural production in the traditional forms — ecclesiastical art, Latin verse, scholastic disputation — contracts markedly. These deteriorations are entirely internal in origin, arising from political fragmentation and social conflict rather than from external conquest or natural disaster. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the very same period witnesses an extraordinary flourishing of new forms of cultural activity. Vernacular literature emerges; secular painting develops new techniques and new subject matter; humanistic scholarship begins to displace the older scholastic tradition. What appears as a contraction of cultural production is in reality a transformation: old forms wither, but new forms arise that will come to be regarded as among the greatest achievements of European civilisation. It appears that the definition, by measuring cultural production against the forms characteristic of an earlier period, mistakes cultural transformation for cultural decline. Without a requirement that the assessment encompass emergent as well as traditional forms, the definition cannot distinguish genuine decline from the painful but productive metamorphosis of a society's creative life.

definition: A historical narrative N is adequate if and only if (i) every event described in N is supported by reliable evidence, (ii) the events are arranged in correct chronological order, and (iii) N provides a coherent account in which each event is connected to the others by intelligible transitions.
conditions: Every event described in N is supported by reliable evidence
The events are arranged in correct chronological order
N provides a coherent account in which each event is connected to the others by intelligible transitions
counterexample: Suppose a historian constructs a narrative of the French Revolution that describes only the major military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations, omitting the social upheaval, the ideological transformations, and the experience of ordinary citizens. Every event described is well documented. The chronological order is scrupulously correct. And the transitions between events are perfectly coherent: one campaign leads to the next, one negotiation precipitates another. All three conditions are met. Yet the narrative is manifestly inadequate, for it systematically omits the very dimensions of the Revolution — popular mobilisation, ideological conflict, institutional transformation — that are essential to understanding what the Revolution was.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that N include events representative of the full range of causally and thematically significant dimensions of the historical process being narrated — that the selection of events not systematically exclude aspects essential to an adequate understanding of the phenomenon.
passage: One might propose that a historical narrative is adequate when every event it describes is supported by reliable evidence, the events are arranged in correct chronological order, and the account is coherent with intelligible transitions. This characterisation captures the evidential, temporal, and logical requirements that we associate with responsible historical writing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the historiography of the French Revolution. Suppose a historian constructs a narrative of the Revolution that confines itself entirely to the major military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations: the fall of the Bastille as a military event, the wars of the First Coalition, the Treaty of Campo Formio, and so on. Every event described is meticulously documented from primary sources. The chronological order is scrupulously correct. And the transitions between events are perfectly intelligible: one military reverse precipitates a diplomatic initiative, which in turn provokes a new campaign. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that this narrative is manifestly inadequate as an account of the French Revolution. It systematically omits the social upheaval, the ideological transformations, the experience of ordinary citizens, the dismantling of feudal institutions, and the radical reconception of political authority that together constitute what the Revolution was. The narrative is accurate in what it describes but profoundly misleading in what it omits. It appears that the definition, by requiring only evidence, chronology, and coherence, fails to demand that the selection of events encompass the full range of causally and thematically significant dimensions of the historical phenomenon. Without such a requirement, a narrative may be accurate, orderly, and coherent while presenting a fundamentally distorted picture.

definition: A practice P constitutes a genuine historical tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted across at least three generations within a community, (ii) P is recognised by members of the community as having been inherited from their predecessors, and (iii) P retains a recognisable continuity of form throughout its transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted across at least three generations within a community
P is recognised by members of the community as having been inherited from their predecessors
P retains a recognisable continuity of form throughout its transmission
counterexample: Suppose a community in the nineteenth century invents a harvest festival, complete with costumes, songs, and rituals, and attributes it to ancient ancestors. The festival is transmitted faithfully for four generations. Each generation recognises it as inherited. And the form of the festival remains substantially unchanged. All three conditions are met. Yet the festival was invented in the nineteenth century; its attribution to ancient ancestors is a fabrication. The practice is what one might call an invented tradition — a recent creation disguised as an immemorial custom.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the historical claims embedded in the community's recognition of P be accurate — that P actually originate in the manner and at the time that its practitioners believe, rather than being a recent invention retrospectively attributed to a fictitious antiquity.
passage: One might define a genuine historical tradition as a practice transmitted across at least three generations, recognised by its practitioners as inherited from their predecessors, and retaining a recognisable continuity of form. This characterisation captures the temporal depth, communal self-understanding, and formal continuity that we associate with authentic traditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of invented traditions. Suppose a community in the early nineteenth century creates a harvest festival, devising costumes, songs, and rituals for the occasion, and attributes the entire celebration to ancient ancestors who, it is claimed, performed the same rites a thousand years before. The festival is transmitted faithfully for four generations. Each generation recognises the practice as inherited from its predecessors, and the community takes pride in the supposed antiquity of its customs. The form of the festival — the costumes, the songs, the order of ritual — remains substantially unchanged throughout its transmission. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this is not a genuine historical tradition in the sense the definition means to capture. The festival was invented barely a century ago, and its attribution to ancient ancestors is a fabrication, whether deliberate or the product of gradual mythologising. The practice is what has been called an invented tradition: a recent creation retrospectively clothed in the garments of immemorial antiquity. It appears that the definition, by requiring only generational transmission, communal recognition, and formal continuity, fails to demand that the historical claims embedded in the community's self-understanding be actually true — that the practice genuinely originate in the manner its practitioners believe.

definition: A historical interpretation I is free from anachronism if and only if (i) I employs only concepts that were available to the agents whose actions are being interpreted, (ii) I does not attribute motives or intentions that presuppose knowledge of subsequent events, and (iii) I describes the material conditions of the period accurately.
conditions: I employs only concepts that were available to the agents whose actions are being interpreted
I does not attribute motives or intentions that presuppose knowledge of subsequent events
I describes the material conditions of the period accurately
counterexample: Suppose a historian interprets a medieval monk's decision to copy classical manuscripts as motivated by a love of learning and a desire to preserve knowledge for future scholars. The historian employs only concepts available to a medieval monk — love of learning, preservation, scholarly devotion. No foreknowledge of subsequent events is attributed; the monk is not said to anticipate the Renaissance. The material conditions are described accurately. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the interpretation may be anachronistic in a subtler way: the monk may have copied the manuscripts as a form of devotional discipline, understanding the act as prayer rather than as scholarship, and the concept of preserving knowledge for secular posterity may be a modern imposition upon a fundamentally religious practice.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the concepts employed in I not merely have been available to the agents but be used with the meaning and significance that the agents themselves would have attached to them — that the interpretation capture the agents' own framework of understanding rather than deploying familiar terms with modern connotations.
passage: One might propose that a historical interpretation is free from anachronism when it employs only concepts available to the agents in question, does not attribute motives presupposing foreknowledge, and describes material conditions accurately. This characterisation captures the temporal, motivational, and factual dimensions of the prohibition against anachronism. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the interpretation of medieval monastic culture. Suppose a historian interprets a twelfth-century monk's decision to copy classical manuscripts as motivated by a love of learning and a desire to preserve knowledge for future scholars. The concepts employed — love of learning, preservation, scholarly devotion — were certainly available to a twelfth-century monk; these are not modern inventions. The historian does not attribute foreknowledge of subsequent events: the monk is not said to have anticipated the Renaissance or the Enlightenment. The material conditions of the scriptorium are described with accuracy. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the interpretation is anachronistic in a way the definition fails to detect. The monk may have understood his copying as a form of devotional discipline — an act of prayer and submission to divine will — rather than as an exercise in the preservation of secular knowledge. The concept of preserving knowledge for posterity, though available in some form, may have carried connotations for the monk quite different from those the modern historian attaches to it. The historian has employed concepts that were available but has invested them with a significance that belongs to a later age. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the availability of concepts, the absence of attributed foreknowledge, and material accuracy, fails to demand that the concepts be used with the meaning the agents themselves would have attached to them.

definition: An event E leaves a historical legacy if and only if (i) E produces effects that persist beyond the generation in which it occurred, (ii) these effects are recognised by subsequent generations as traceable to E, and (iii) the effects are of sufficient magnitude to have shaped the course of events in the period following E.
conditions: E produces effects that persist beyond the generation in which it occurred
These effects are recognised by subsequent generations as traceable to E
The effects are of sufficient magnitude to have shaped the course of events in the period following E
counterexample: Consider the widespread but historically unfounded belief that a medieval battle between two minor lords was the decisive event that determined the political boundaries of a region for centuries. The belief persists across multiple generations. It is universally recognised as traceable to the battle. And it has shaped subsequent events, since political movements have invoked the battle to justify territorial claims. All three conditions are met. Yet historical research reveals that the political boundaries were actually determined by an entirely unrelated treaty negotiated decades later, and the battle had no lasting military or political significance whatsoever. The supposed legacy is an artefact of collective misattribution.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the effects attributed to E actually be caused by E — that the causal connection between the event and the persisting effects be genuine rather than a product of collective misattribution or mythologised memory.
passage: One might define a historical legacy as obtaining when an event produces effects that persist beyond its own generation, these effects are recognised as traceable to the event, and they are of sufficient magnitude to shape subsequent events. This characterisation captures the durability, traceability, and significance that we associate with genuine historical legacies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from the historiography of contested territories. Consider a medieval battle between two minor lords that is widely believed to have determined the political boundaries of a region for centuries. The belief in this legacy persists across many generations: schoolchildren are taught it, political orators invoke it, and boundary disputes are argued with reference to the battle. The effects are universally recognised as traceable to this event. And the effects are of considerable magnitude: political movements have risen and fallen on the strength of claims rooted in the supposed outcome of the battle. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, suppose that careful archival research reveals that the political boundaries were in fact established by an entirely unrelated treaty negotiated several decades after the battle, and that the battle itself had no lasting military or political consequences. The supposed legacy is an artefact of collective misattribution — a myth that has acquired causal efficacy through belief rather than through any genuine historical connection. It appears that the definition, by requiring only persistent and recognised effects of sufficient magnitude, fails to demand that the causal connection between the event and its supposed effects be genuine. Without this requirement, myths and misattributions qualify as legacies on the same footing as authentic historical consequences.

definition: An event or series of events R constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) R involves a rapid and fundamental change in the political order of a society, (ii) R is brought about by collective action on the part of a substantial portion of the population, and (iii) R results in the establishment of a new political regime that differs in kind from its predecessor.
conditions: R involves a rapid and fundamental change in the political order of a society
R is brought about by collective action on the part of a substantial portion of the population
R results in the establishment of a new political regime that differs in kind from its predecessor
counterexample: Consider a palace coup in which a group of military officers seizes power overnight, replaces a monarchy with a republic, and institutes a constitution establishing democratic elections and civil liberties. The change is rapid and fundamental. The officers acted collectively and enjoyed broad popular support, so a substantial portion of the population may be said to have participated or at least acquiesced. And the new regime — a democratic republic — differs in kind from the monarchy it replaced. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the same military clique retains effective control behind the democratic facade, the constitution is systematically subverted, and the old power structure persists under new names. We should hesitate to call this a genuine revolution.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the new political order established by R represent a genuine transformation in the distribution and exercise of power, rather than merely a change in the formal or nominal character of the regime that leaves the actual structure of authority substantially intact.
passage: One might propose that a genuine revolution involves a rapid and fundamental change in the political order, is brought about by collective action of a substantial portion of the population, and results in a new regime that differs in kind from its predecessor. This characterisation captures the speed, popular basis, and transformative character that we associate with revolutionary change. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the annals of military coups. Suppose a group of military officers seizes power overnight, deposes the reigning monarch, and proclaims a republic. A constitution is promulgated establishing democratic elections, civil liberties, and the separation of powers. The change is rapid — accomplished within a week. It is fundamental, replacing a hereditary monarchy with a constitutional republic. The officers enjoy considerable popular support, and mass demonstrations accompany the proclamation of the new order, so the condition of collective participation is met. The new regime — a democratic republic — differs in kind from the old monarchy. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the same military clique that orchestrated the coup retains effective control of the government. The democratic constitution is systematically subverted: elections are managed, opposition is suppressed through informal channels, and the old networks of patronage and authority persist under new titles. The change in political form is genuine, but the actual distribution and exercise of power remain substantially unaltered. It appears that the definition, by requiring only a rapid change in political order, popular involvement, and a formally different new regime, fails to demand that the revolution produce a genuine transformation in the structure of authority rather than merely a change in its outward appearance.

definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies the antecedent events and conditions that temporally preceded E, (ii) A shows that E was a probable or expected outcome given those antecedent conditions, and (iii) the antecedent events and conditions cited in A are supported by documentary or archaeological evidence.
conditions: A identifies the antecedent events and conditions that temporally preceded E
A shows that E was a probable or expected outcome given those antecedent conditions
The antecedent events and conditions cited in A are supported by documentary or archaeological evidence
counterexample: Suppose a historian explains the fall of a particular medieval kingdom by citing the well-documented antecedent conditions of famine, military overextension, and dynastic dispute, and shows that the kingdom's collapse was a probable outcome given these pressures. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose a neighbouring kingdom experienced the same combination of famine, military overextension, and dynastic dispute at the same period and did not fall. The explanation identifies genuine antecedent conditions but fails to account for why one kingdom succumbed while the other, under apparently similar pressures, survived.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A identify the specific causal mechanisms by which the antecedent conditions produced E in this particular case, thereby distinguishing genuine causal explanations from accounts that merely cite background conditions common to both the occurrence and the non-occurrence of similar events.
passage: One might propose that a satisfactory historical explanation identifies well-evidenced antecedent events and conditions and shows that the event to be explained was a probable outcome given those conditions. This characterisation captures the temporal ordering, probabilistic adequacy, and evidential grounding that we expect of historical explanations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from medieval European history. Suppose a historian seeks to explain the fall of a particular kingdom in the thirteenth century by citing three well-documented antecedent conditions: a prolonged famine that weakened the population, a costly military campaign that depleted the treasury, and a succession dispute that divided the nobility. The historian demonstrates that, given these conditions, the kingdom's collapse was a probable outcome. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that a neighbouring kingdom experienced the same combination of famine, military overextension, and dynastic quarrel during the same period and did not collapse. The antecedent conditions cited by the historian are genuine features of the situation, but they do not, by themselves, explain why this particular kingdom fell while its neighbour survived under apparently similar pressures. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the identification of antecedent conditions, probabilistic adequacy, and evidential support, fails to demand that the explanation identify the specific causal mechanisms — perhaps the decisive intervention of a foreign power, or the particular vulnerability of a specific institutional structure — by which the antecedent conditions produced the event in this particular case.

definition: A civilisation C has made historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) C has increased its material productive capacity during P, (ii) the range of technical and intellectual achievements available to C has expanded during P, and (iii) the population of C has grown during P.
conditions: C has increased its material productive capacity during P
The range of technical and intellectual achievements available to C has expanded during P
The population of C has grown during P
counterexample: Consider the Roman Empire during the second century, when material productive capacity was immense, technical achievements in engineering, law, and administration were unparalleled, and the population was large and growing. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this period also saw the consolidation of slavery on a vast scale, the systematic suppression of political liberty for the majority of the population, and the use of advanced engineering to construct instruments of public cruelty. One might question whether a civilisation that increases its productive capacity and expands its technical range while deepening human degradation has made genuine progress.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the increases in productive capacity and technical achievement be accompanied by improvements in the moral, political, or humanitarian conditions of the population, so that progress is not attributed to civilisations that advance materially while regressing ethically.
passage: One might define historical progress as the increase of material productive capacity, the expansion of technical and intellectual achievements, and the growth of population over a given period. This characterisation captures the quantitative, cumulative, and demographic aspects that are often cited as hallmarks of civilisational advancement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of the Roman Empire. During the second century, Rome's material productive capacity was immense: the empire produced vast quantities of grain, metals, and manufactured goods. The range of technical and intellectual achievements available to the civilisation had expanded: Roman engineering, legal codification, and administrative organisation were without parallel in the ancient world. And the population of the empire was large and growing. All three conditions of the proposed definition are manifestly satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say without qualification that Rome had made historical progress during this period. The expansion of productive capacity rested in large part upon the institution of slavery, maintained on a vast scale. The technical achievements of Roman engineering were employed not only in the construction of aqueducts and roads but also in the building of amphitheatres designed for the public infliction of suffering. Political liberty, for the great majority of the population, was wholly absent. It appears that the definition, by attending only to productive capacity, technical range, and demographic growth, fails to require that these material gains be accompanied by any improvement in the moral or humanitarian conditions of the population. Progress, one might conclude, demands more than material expansion.

definition: A historical narrative N is adequate if and only if (i) N presents events in their correct chronological sequence, (ii) N includes all events that are causally relevant to the outcome being narrated, and (iii) N is consistent with all available documentary evidence.
conditions: N presents events in their correct chronological sequence
N includes all events that are causally relevant to the outcome being narrated
N is consistent with all available documentary evidence
counterexample: Suppose a historian narrates the sequence of events leading to a diplomatic crisis between two nations. The events are presented in correct chronological order. Every causally relevant event — the provocative speech, the troop mobilisation, the failed negotiation, the ultimatum — is included. The narrative is consistent with all available documentary evidence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the historian narrates the crisis entirely from the perspective of one nation's archives, presenting the other nation's actions as unprovoked aggressions while omitting any sympathetic reconstruction of the other side's reasoning. The narrative satisfies every stated condition but distorts the historical reality by imposing a single interpretive perspective.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that N consider and integrate multiple perspectives, so that the narrative does not distort the historical reality by presenting events exclusively from one vantage point while neglecting the interpretive frameworks and motivations of other significant actors.
passage: One might define an adequate historical narrative as one that presents events in their correct chronological sequence, includes all causally relevant events, and is consistent with the available documentary evidence. This characterisation captures the temporal ordering, causal completeness, and evidential fidelity that we expect of responsible historical writing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the narration of diplomatic crises. Suppose a historian undertakes to narrate the sequence of events leading to a major diplomatic rupture between two nations. The events are presented in their correct chronological order. Every causally relevant occurrence is included: the provocative speech, the troop mobilisation, the failed negotiation, the ultimatum. And the narrative is consistent with all available documentary evidence. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this narrative as adequate if it is constructed entirely from the archives of one nation, presenting that nation's actions as measured and defensive while depicting the other nation's conduct as aggressive and unprovoked. The historian has included all causally relevant events and has not contradicted any document, yet the narrative imposes a single interpretive perspective that distorts the historical reality. It appears that the definition, by requiring only chronological accuracy, causal completeness, and documentary consistency, fails to demand that the narrative integrate multiple perspectives, so that the motivations and reasoning of all significant actors are given due consideration.

definition: A division of history into periods is justified if and only if (i) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant changes in the political, social, or cultural conditions of the civilisation in question, (ii) the features that characterise each period are relatively stable and uniform within that period, and (iii) the periodisation is based upon a comprehensive survey of the available evidence.
conditions: The boundaries between periods correspond to significant changes in political, social, or cultural conditions
The features that characterise each period are relatively stable and uniform within that period
The periodisation is based upon a comprehensive survey of the available evidence
counterexample: Suppose a historian divides the history of a large empire into three periods: a period of expansion, a period of consolidation, and a period of decline. The boundaries correspond to significant political and military changes. The characterising features are relatively stable within each period. And the periodisation is based upon a comprehensive survey of the evidence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the periodisation attends only to the political and military fortunes of the imperial centre, while ignoring the fact that during the so-called period of decline, the empire's provincial cultures were experiencing an unprecedented flourishing of art, commerce, and learning. The periodisation is justified by the political criterion but misleading when applied to cultural or economic history.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the periodisation be specified with respect to a clearly identified dimension of historical experience, so that a scheme justified for political history is not uncritically extended to cultural, economic, or intellectual history where different temporal rhythms may obtain.
passage: One might propose that a justified periodisation of history is one in which the boundaries correspond to significant changes in conditions, the features characterising each period are relatively stable within it, and the scheme rests upon a comprehensive survey of the evidence. This characterisation captures the sensitivity to change, internal coherence, and evidential thoroughness that we expect of responsible periodisation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the historiography of empires. Suppose a historian divides the history of a large empire into three periods: expansion, consolidation, and decline. The boundaries correspond to significant changes in political and military conditions: the cessation of territorial conquest, the establishment of stable frontiers, and the onset of territorial losses. Within each period, the characterising features are relatively stable. And the periodisation is based upon a comprehensive survey of the available political and military evidence. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that this periodisation, though adequate for the political and military dimension, is positively misleading when applied to the cultural and economic life of the empire. During the so-called period of decline, the provinces may have experienced an unprecedented flourishing of art, commerce, and intellectual activity. The periodisation imposes the temporal rhythm of political history upon dimensions of experience that follow quite different rhythms. It appears that the definition, by requiring only correspondence to significant changes, internal stability, and comprehensive evidence, fails to demand that the periodisation be explicitly relativised to a specified dimension of historical experience.

definition: A society S qualifies as a civilisation if and only if (i) S possesses a system of writing, (ii) S has developed specialised institutions for governance, religion, and economic exchange, and (iii) S supports a population concentrated in urban settlements.
conditions: S possesses a system of writing
S has developed specialised institutions for governance, religion, and economic exchange
S supports a population concentrated in urban settlements
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire, which governed a vast territory with millions of subjects, maintained elaborate institutions for governance, religion, and economic redistribution, and supported a population concentrated in substantial urban centres. Two of the three conditions are clearly satisfied. Yet the Inca lacked a system of writing in any conventional sense, employing the quipu — a system of knotted cords — for record-keeping. If we insist upon writing as a necessary condition, we must deny the Inca the status of a civilisation, a conclusion that appears manifestly inadequate given the sophistication and scale of Inca social organisation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that complex record-keeping systems other than writing — such as the quipu — may serve the same organisational functions as written language, and that the possession of writing is therefore too narrow a criterion for the communicative and administrative capacities characteristic of civilisation.
passage: One might define a civilisation as a society possessing a system of writing, specialised institutions for governance, religion, and economic exchange, and a population concentrated in urban settlements. This characterisation captures the literate, institutionally complex, and urban character that we commonly associate with the great civilisations of antiquity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the Americas. The Inca Empire, at its height in the fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries, governed a territory stretching thousands of miles along the western coast of South America. It maintained elaborate institutions of governance, with a hierarchical administration, a system of state-controlled economic redistribution, and a complex religious order. Its population was concentrated in substantial urban centres. Two of the three conditions are manifestly satisfied. Yet the Inca lacked a system of writing in the conventional sense. Their primary means of record-keeping was the quipu, a system of knotted cords encoding numerical and possibly narrative information. If we insist upon writing as a necessary condition for civilisation, we are compelled to deny the Inca that designation — a conclusion that appears manifestly inadequate given the extraordinary sophistication and scale of their political, economic, and religious organisation. It appears that the definition, by requiring writing as a necessary condition, draws the boundary of civilisation too narrowly, failing to acknowledge that complex record-keeping systems other than alphabetic or logographic writing may serve the same organisational and communicative functions.

definition: A practice P constitutes a genuine historical tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted continuously from one generation to the next over a substantial period, (ii) P is recognised by its practitioners as having ancient origins, and (iii) P retains a stable core of meaning and form throughout the period of transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted continuously from one generation to the next over a substantial period
P is recognised by its practitioners as having ancient origins
P retains a stable core of meaning and form throughout the period of transmission
counterexample: Consider the Scottish Highland games, which are recognised by their practitioners as having ancient Celtic origins, have been transmitted across generations, and appear to retain a stable core of athletic competitions and ceremonial form. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet historical investigation reveals that many features of the Highland games as practised today were invented or substantially reinvented in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, often by Lowland Scots or English romantics. The continuity is partly illusory; the tradition, as understood by its participants, attributes an antiquity to practices that are in fact of comparatively recent origin.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the practitioners' belief in the ancient origins of P be historically accurate, so that a practice invented or substantially reinvented in recent times cannot qualify as a genuine tradition merely because its practitioners sincerely believe it to be ancient.
passage: One might define a genuine historical tradition as a practice transmitted continuously across generations, recognised by its practitioners as having ancient origins, and retaining a stable core of meaning and form. This characterisation captures the continuity, self-understanding, and formal stability that we associate with the most venerable traditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from Scottish cultural history. The Highland games are recognised by their participants as having ancient Celtic origins, they have been transmitted from one generation to the next over a substantial period, and they appear to retain a stable core of athletic competitions and ceremonial pageantry. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that historical investigation reveals a more complicated picture. Many of the practices now associated with the Highland games were invented or substantially reinvented in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, often by Lowland Scots or English romantics inspired by the fashionable cult of Highland antiquity. The participants' belief in the ancient origins of these practices, though sincere, is historically inaccurate. It appears that the definition, by accepting the practitioners' own understanding of antiquity at face value, fails to distinguish between genuine continuity from ancient times and the retrospective attribution of antiquity to practices of comparatively recent origin. Without the requirement that the belief in ancient origins be historically warranted, the definition cannot exclude invented traditions from the category of genuine ones.

definition: A civilisation C is in a state of decline during a period P if and only if (i) the central political authority of C has weakened during P, (ii) the military capacity of C has diminished during P, and (iii) the territory controlled by C has contracted during P.
conditions: The central political authority of C has weakened during P
The military capacity of C has diminished during P
The territory controlled by C has contracted during P
counterexample: Consider the Byzantine Empire during the period from the loss of its western provinces in the fifth century to the reign of Justinian in the sixth century. During portions of this interval the central political authority was relatively weak, the military capacity had diminished from its former peak, and the territory had contracted significantly. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this period also witnessed the codification of Roman law in the Corpus Juris Civilis, a magnificent programme of architectural construction including the Hagia Sophia, and a flourishing of theological and philosophical scholarship. To describe the civilisation as being in decline during this period captures only the political and military dimensions while ignoring the extraordinary cultural vitality.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that decline be assessed across all significant dimensions of civilisational life — cultural, intellectual, economic, and artistic — rather than solely in terms of political authority, military capacity, and territorial extent, so that a civilisation experiencing cultural flourishing is not mischaracterised as being in decline.
passage: One might define civilisational decline as a period during which the central political authority has weakened, the military capacity has diminished, and the territory has contracted. This characterisation captures the political, military, and territorial dimensions that are often taken as the most visible indicators of decline. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of the Byzantine Empire. During the period between the loss of the western provinces in the fifth century and the reign of Justinian in the sixth, the empire's central authority was relatively weak in comparison with the unified rule of earlier centuries, its military capacity had diminished from its former peak, and its territory had contracted significantly. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to describe the civilisation as being in unqualified decline during this period. It was precisely during these years that the foundations were laid for the codification of Roman law in the Corpus Juris Civilis, that a magnificent programme of architectural construction was undertaken culminating in the Hagia Sophia, and that theological and philosophical scholarship flourished in Constantinople and other centres. The political and military indicators point downward; the cultural and intellectual indicators point upward. It appears that the definition, by restricting its criteria to political authority, military capacity, and territorial extent, fails to assess decline across the full range of civilisational achievement, and thereby mischaracterises periods of extraordinary cultural vitality as periods of decline.

definition: An event or series of events R constitutes a genuine historical revolution if and only if (i) R involves the rapid and forcible overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) R results in the establishment of fundamentally new political institutions, and (iii) R is driven by a broad-based popular movement rather than a narrow elite.
conditions: R involves the rapid and forcible overthrow of an existing political order
R results in the establishment of fundamentally new political institutions
R is driven by a broad-based popular movement rather than a narrow elite
counterexample: Consider the English Glorious Revolution of 1688, which is widely regarded as one of the most consequential revolutions in modern political history. The existing political order was overturned with remarkable rapidity, and fundamentally new political institutions — including parliamentary sovereignty and the Bill of Rights — were established. Yet the Glorious Revolution was not driven by a broad-based popular movement; it was orchestrated by a narrow political and religious elite who invited a foreign prince to assume the throne. The broader populace was largely passive. Two of the three conditions are satisfied, but the third is not, even though the event is paradigmatically revolutionary.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate revolutions accomplished through elite initiative rather than popular uprising, thereby excluding from the category of genuine revolution events that effect fundamental and rapid institutional transformation without broad-based popular participation.
passage: One might define a genuine historical revolution as one that involves the rapid and forcible overthrow of an existing political order, results in the establishment of fundamentally new political institutions, and is driven by a broad-based popular movement. This characterisation captures the violence, institutional novelty, and popular agency that we associate with the great revolutions of modern history. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from English history. The Glorious Revolution of 1688 is widely regarded as one of the most consequential political upheavals of the modern era. The existing political order was overturned with remarkable rapidity when James II fled and William of Orange assumed the throne. Fundamentally new political institutions were established, including parliamentary sovereignty and the English Bill of Rights, which transformed the relationship between crown and parliament for centuries to come. Two of the three conditions are manifestly satisfied. Yet the Glorious Revolution was not driven by a broad-based popular movement. It was orchestrated by a narrow political and religious elite — a group of Whig and Tory nobles who issued an invitation to William — and the broader populace was largely passive during the transfer of power. The third condition is not met. Nevertheless, to deny the Glorious Revolution the status of a genuine revolution appears absurd. It appears that the definition, by requiring broad popular participation, draws the category too narrowly, excluding events that achieve fundamental and rapid institutional transformation through elite initiative.

definition: A historical figure F has left a positive legacy if and only if (i) F's actions produced lasting institutional, cultural, or intellectual effects that persist beyond F's lifetime, (ii) those effects are generally regarded as beneficial by subsequent generations, and (iii) F intended to produce those beneficial effects.
conditions: F's actions produced lasting institutional, cultural, or intellectual effects that persist beyond F's lifetime
Those effects are generally regarded as beneficial by subsequent generations
F intended to produce those beneficial effects
counterexample: Consider a medieval monarch who, motivated entirely by the desire to consolidate personal power and enrich the royal treasury, established a system of royal courts to adjudicate disputes that had previously been settled by local strongmen through violence. The monarch's intention was purely self-interested: centralised courts extended royal authority and generated revenue. Yet the institutional effect — a system of impartial adjudication available to common subjects — persisted for centuries and was regarded by subsequent generations as one of the foundations of the rule of law. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third is not, since the beneficial effects were entirely unintended.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that historically significant and beneficial legacies may be produced by actors whose intentions were entirely self-interested, and that requiring the intention to produce beneficial effects excludes cases in which some of the most consequential positive legacies arose as unintended consequences of actions motivated by quite different purposes.
passage: One might propose that a historical figure has left a positive legacy if his or her actions produced lasting beneficial effects, those effects are regarded as beneficial by subsequent generations, and the figure intended to produce those effects. This characterisation captures the durability, positive reception, and purposiveness that we associate with the most admired legacies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from medieval political history. Suppose a monarch, motivated entirely by the desire to consolidate personal power and enrich the royal treasury, establishes a system of royal courts to adjudicate disputes throughout the realm. The monarch's intention is purely self-interested: centralised courts extend royal authority into regions previously dominated by local barons and generate a reliable stream of judicial fees. Yet the institutional effect persists for centuries after the monarch's death, evolving into a system of impartial adjudication available to common subjects and generally regarded by subsequent generations as one of the foundations of the rule of law. The first two conditions are manifestly satisfied: the effects are lasting and beneficial. Yet the third is not, for the monarch never intended to establish the rule of law or to benefit common subjects. It appears that the definition, by requiring beneficial intention, excludes some of the most consequential positive legacies in history — those that arose as unintended consequences of actions motivated by quite different purposes.

definition: An interpretation I of a historical text or practice is anachronistic if and only if (i) I applies a concept or category to the text or practice that was not available to its original authors or practitioners, (ii) the concept or category in question was developed after the period in which the text or practice originated, and (iii) the application of the concept distorts the original meaning or significance of the text or practice.
conditions: I applies a concept or category that was not available to the original authors or practitioners
The concept or category was developed after the period in which the text or practice originated
The application of the concept distorts the original meaning or significance of the text or practice
counterexample: Suppose a historian interprets Aristotle's discussion of the polis by applying the concept of the nation-state, which was developed many centuries after Aristotle. The concept was unavailable to Aristotle, it postdates his work by well over a millennium, and its application distorts his meaning. All three conditions are satisfied, and the interpretation is rightly called anachronistic. Now suppose a different historian applies the concept of social class — also a post-Aristotelian concept — to Aristotle's discussion of the various orders of citizens. The concept postdates the text, and it was unavailable to Aristotle. Yet its application may illuminate features of Aristotle's analysis that are genuinely present in the text, drawing out structural relations that Aristotle described but lacked a name for. In this case the third condition may not be satisfied, but the definition gives us no resources for distinguishing fruitful anachronism from distorting anachronism.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between anachronistic applications that distort and those that illuminate, and thereby provides no criterion for determining when the application of a later concept to an earlier text, though technically anachronistic, may be hermeneutically valuable rather than misleading.
passage: One might define an anachronistic interpretation as one that applies a concept unavailable to the original authors, developed after the relevant period, in a manner that distorts the original meaning. This characterisation captures the temporal displacement, conceptual imposition, and interpretive distortion that we regard as the hallmarks of anachronism in historical scholarship. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the interpretation of ancient political thought. Suppose a historian applies the concept of the nation-state to Aristotle's discussion of the polis. The concept was unavailable to Aristotle, was developed many centuries later, and its application distorts his meaning by importing assumptions about sovereignty, territory, and citizenship that are foreign to the ancient context. The interpretation is rightly judged anachronistic. Now suppose a different historian applies the concept of social class to Aristotle's discussion of the various orders of citizens in the polis. The concept of social class, as articulated in modern social theory, was similarly unavailable to Aristotle and postdates his work by over two millennia. Yet its application may illuminate features of Aristotle's analysis that are genuinely present in the text — structural relations among groups defined by their economic function and political standing — without distorting the original meaning. The definition, by requiring distortion as a necessary condition, correctly excludes this second case from the category of anachronism. But it gives us no resources for explaining why one retrospective application distorts while the other illuminates, for it attends only to the question of distortion without specifying the conditions under which a later concept may be fruitfully applied to earlier material.

definition: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.
conditions: The material standard of living of the majority has risen during P
The range of civil liberties has expanded during P
The capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P
counterexample: Suppose a society over the course of two centuries experiences a dramatic rise in the material standard of living for the majority of its members, a significant expansion of civil liberties, and a vast increase in its capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge through universities, printing, and public libraries. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet during the same period, the society develops industrial capacities that cause severe and potentially irreversible damage to the natural environment upon which future generations depend. The gains in material welfare, liberty, and knowledge are purchased at the cost of ecological degradation that threatens the sustainability of those very gains.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the improvements be sustainable and not purchased at the cost of harms to future generations or to the natural conditions upon which continued human flourishing depends, so that a society may satisfy all conditions of progress while undermining the foundations of its own future welfare.
passage: One might propose that genuine historical progress consists in the conjunction of rising material welfare, expanding civil liberties, and increasing capacity for the production and dissemination of knowledge. This characterisation captures the economic, political, and intellectual dimensions that we most commonly associate with the idea of progress. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of industrial civilisation. Suppose a society, over the course of two centuries, experiences a dramatic rise in the material standard of living for the majority of its members: food is more abundant, housing is more secure, and disease is less prevalent. Simultaneously, the range of civil liberties expands: the franchise is extended, censorship is reduced, and legal protections for individuals are strengthened. And the society's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge increases enormously through the establishment of universities, the development of printing, and the creation of public libraries. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to pronounce this progress unqualified, for during the same period the society develops industrial capacities that inflict severe and potentially irreversible damage upon the natural environment. The gains in welfare, liberty, and knowledge are purchased at the cost of ecological degradation that threatens the very sustainability of those gains. It appears that the definition, by requiring only improvements in material welfare, civil liberties, and knowledge, fails to demand that these improvements be sustainable and not achieved at the expense of future generations. Without such a requirement, a society may satisfy every condition of progress while systematically undermining the foundations of its own continued flourishing.

definition: An account A constitutes an adequate historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies the proximate causes that brought E about, (ii) A situates E within the broader social, economic, and political context of the period, and (iii) A draws upon documentary evidence that is contemporaneous with E.
conditions: A identifies the proximate causes that brought E about
A situates E within the broader social economic and political context
A draws upon documentary evidence contemporaneous with E
counterexample: Suppose a historian seeks to explain the collapse of a particular medieval monastery. She identifies the proximate causes: a dispute between the abbot and the local bishop, the withdrawal of royal patronage, and a series of poor harvests. She situates the collapse within the broader context of declining feudal authority, shifting patterns of piety, and economic contraction. And she draws upon charters, letters, and account rolls that are contemporaneous with the events in question. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the historian's account relies entirely upon documents produced by the literate clerical elite and entirely overlooks the perspectives and actions of the lay servants and tenant farmers whose labour sustained the monastery and whose withdrawal of cooperation was, arguably, the decisive factor in its collapse.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the documentary evidence be representative of the diverse social groups involved in the event, so that an explanation drawing upon contemporaneous evidence from only one social stratum may satisfy all conditions while systematically excluding the perspectives of those whose agency was crucial to the outcome.
passage: One might define an adequate historical explanation as an account that identifies the proximate causes of an event, situates the event within its broader social, economic, and political context, and draws upon documentary evidence contemporaneous with the event. This characterisation captures the causal, contextual, and evidential requirements that we expect of rigorous historical scholarship. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of medieval institutional decline. Suppose a historian explains the collapse of a particular monastery by identifying the proximate causes: a dispute between the abbot and the local bishop, the withdrawal of royal patronage, and a succession of poor harvests. She situates the collapse within the broader context of declining feudal authority, shifting patterns of piety, and regional economic contraction. And she draws upon charters, letters, and account rolls that are contemporaneous with the events in question. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met.
