Nevertheless, one might observe that her documentary sources are exclusively the products of the literate clerical elite. The perspectives and actions of the lay servants and tenant farmers, whose labour sustained the monastery and whose withdrawal of cooperation may have been the decisive factor in its collapse, are entirely absent from the account. It appears that the definition, by requiring only contemporaneous documentary evidence without stipulating its representativeness, permits an explanation that is causally proximate, contextually situated, and evidentially grounded, yet systematically blind to the agency of those social groups whose records have not survived or were never produced.

definition: A division of historical time into periods is adequate if and only if (i) each period is demarcated by identifiable events or transitions that mark a significant change in the character of the society under study, (ii) the periods are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive of the time span in question, and (iii) the periodisation is widely accepted among historians of the relevant field.
conditions: Each period is demarcated by events or transitions marking significant change
The periods are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive of the time span
The periodisation is widely accepted among historians of the relevant field
counterexample: Suppose a periodisation of European history divides the time from the fifth century to the present into the periods commonly designated as the Dark Ages, the Medieval, the Renaissance, the Early Modern, and the Modern. Each period is demarcated by identifiable events marking significant change; the periods are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive; and the periodisation is widely accepted. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this periodisation imposes upon the histories of non-Western societies a framework that reflects specifically European developments. Applied to the history of China or the Islamic world, the same boundaries mark no significant transitions, and the periodisation distorts rather than illuminates the character of those civilisations.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the periodisation be appropriate to the particular civilisation or region whose history is being divided, so that a scheme adequate for one society may be imposed upon another for which its boundaries mark no significant transitions, thereby satisfying all conditions while distorting the historical phenomena it purports to organise.
passage: One might propose that an adequate periodisation of history is one in which each period is demarcated by events marking significant change, the periods are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive, and the scheme is widely accepted among historians. This characterisation captures the transitional, logical, and professional dimensions of sound periodisation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the comparative study of civilisations. Suppose the familiar periodisation of European history into the Dark Ages, the Medieval, the Renaissance, the Early Modern, and the Modern is applied not only to Europe but to the history of human civilisation as a whole. Each period is demarcated by identifiable events: the fall of Rome, the recovery of classical learning, the voyages of exploration, the French Revolution. The periods are mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive of the time span from the fifth century to the present. And the periodisation is widely accepted among Western historians. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this periodisation as adequate when applied to the history of China or the Islamic world, for the European boundaries mark no significant transitions in those civilisations. The fall of Rome is of no particular moment in Chinese history; the European Renaissance corresponds to no analogous event in the Islamic world. It appears that the definition, by requiring only significant boundaries, logical structure, and professional acceptance, fails to demand that the periodisation be appropriate to the particular civilisation or region under study. Without such a requirement, a scheme adequate for one society may be uncritically imposed upon another, distorting the very phenomena it is meant to organise.

definition: A human society S constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) S possesses a system of writing capable of recording laws, commerce, and literature, (ii) S has developed permanent urban settlements with monumental architecture, and (iii) S exhibits a division of labour that supports a class of individuals devoted to intellectual, artistic, or religious pursuits.
conditions: S possesses a system of writing capable of recording laws commerce and literature
S has developed permanent urban settlements with monumental architecture
S exhibits a division of labour supporting a class devoted to intellectual artistic or religious pursuits
counterexample: Suppose one considers the Inca Empire at its height. It developed permanent urban settlements of impressive scale and monumental architecture, including Cusco and Machu Picchu. It exhibited a sophisticated division of labour that supported a priestly and administrative class devoted to intellectual and religious pursuits. Yet the Inca did not possess writing in the conventional sense; their principal recording system, the quipu, consisted of knotted cords rather than inscribed symbols. By the proposed definition, the Inca Empire would fail to qualify as a civilisation, despite its administrative complexity, engineering achievements, and cultural sophistication being comparable to those of societies that do satisfy all three conditions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the functions served by writing, such as recording, organising, and transmitting information, may be fulfilled by non-written systems of comparable complexity, so that a society which discharges all the informational functions of writing through alternative means is excluded from the category of civilisation on purely formal grounds.
passage: One might define a civilisation as a human society possessing a system of writing, permanent urban settlements with monumental architecture, and a division of labour supporting a class devoted to intellectual, artistic, or religious pursuits. This characterisation captures the literate, urban, and culturally differentiated character that we associate with the civilisations of Mesopotamia, Egypt, and classical Greece. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the Americas. Consider the Inca Empire at its height. It developed permanent urban settlements of impressive scale and monumental architecture, including the great city of Cusco and the mountain complex of Machu Picchu. It exhibited a sophisticated division of labour that supported a priestly and administrative class devoted to religious ceremony, astronomical observation, and the organisation of a vast empire. Yet the Inca did not possess writing in the conventional sense. Their principal system of recording, the quipu, consisted of knotted cords rather than inscribed symbols. By the proposed definition, the Inca Empire fails to qualify as a civilisation, despite its administrative complexity, engineering achievements, and cultural sophistication being manifestly comparable to those of societies that satisfy all three conditions. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon writing as a necessary condition, fails to recognise that the informational functions served by writing may be discharged by alternative systems of comparable complexity. Without this recognition, the analysis excludes from the category of civilisation societies that meet every substantive criterion save one that is ultimately formal.

definition: A historical account A constitutes a faithful narrative of a sequence of events if and only if (i) A presents the events in their actual chronological order, (ii) A includes all events that are causally relevant to the outcome, and (iii) A does not attribute to historical actors intentions or motives for which there is no documentary evidence.
conditions: A presents the events in their actual chronological order
A includes all events that are causally relevant to the outcome
A does not attribute intentions or motives without documentary evidence
counterexample: Suppose a historian constructs a narrative of a political revolution by presenting every event in its actual chronological order, including all causally relevant events supported by extensive documentary evidence, and scrupulously refraining from attributing any intention or motive for which there is no written record. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the narrative, by its selection and arrangement of events, creates a strong impression of historical inevitability: each event seems to lead inexorably to the next, and the revolution appears to have been the only possible outcome. In truth, the outcome was deeply contingent, and at several junctures events might easily have taken a different course. The narrative, while faithful in every particular, conveys a false sense of necessity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the narrative accurately represent the contingency of the historical process, so that an account which presents all causally relevant events in chronological order without unwarranted attributions of motive may nonetheless create a misleading impression of inevitability by its very structure and selection.
passage: One might propose that a faithful historical narrative presents events in their actual chronological order, includes all causally relevant events, and refrains from attributing intentions or motives unsupported by documentary evidence. This characterisation captures the temporal accuracy, causal completeness, and evidential restraint that we associate with responsible historical writing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the narration of political revolutions. Suppose a historian constructs a narrative of a great revolution by presenting every relevant event in its actual chronological order. She includes all events that are causally relevant to the outcome, drawing upon extensive documentary evidence. And she scrupulously refrains from attributing any intention or motive to historical actors for which there is no written record. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the narrative, by its selection and arrangement, creates a powerful impression of historical inevitability. Each event appears to lead inexorably to the next, and the revolution seems to have been the only possible outcome. In truth, the outcome was deeply contingent, and at several critical junctures events might easily have taken a different course. It appears that the definition, by requiring only chronological accuracy, causal completeness, and evidential restraint regarding motives, fails to demand that the narrative accurately represent the contingency of the historical process. Without such a requirement, a narrative that is faithful in every particular may nonetheless convey a profoundly misleading sense of inevitability.

definition: A civilisation C is in a state of decline during a period P if and only if (i) the political institutions of C lose their capacity to maintain order and administer justice during P, (ii) the economic productivity of C diminishes significantly during P, and (iii) the cultural and intellectual output of C deteriorates in quality and quantity during P.
conditions: The political institutions lose their capacity to maintain order and administer justice
The economic productivity diminishes significantly
The cultural and intellectual output deteriorates in quality and quantity
counterexample: Suppose one considers the late Roman Empire in the West during the fourth and fifth centuries. The political institutions lose their capacity to maintain order; economic productivity diminishes as trade routes are disrupted and agricultural lands are abandoned; and, by many measures, the quantity of literary and philosophical production declines. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet during this same period, Christian theology and ecclesiastical literature flourish with remarkable vigour: Augustine writes the City of God and the Confessions, Jerome completes his translation of the Scriptures, and the great ecumenical councils produce documents of enduring intellectual significance. The civilisation's cultural output is not uniformly deteriorating; it is being transformed and redirected rather than simply declining.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the decline be uniform across all dimensions of cultural and intellectual life, so that a civilisation in which political and economic decline coincide with a creative transformation or redirection of cultural energies may satisfy all conditions while in fact exhibiting not decline but metamorphosis.
passage: One might define the decline of a civilisation as a period in which its political institutions lose their capacity to maintain order, its economic productivity diminishes, and its cultural and intellectual output deteriorates in quality and quantity. This characterisation captures the political, economic, and cultural dimensions that we naturally associate with civilisational decline. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the late Roman Empire in the West. During the fourth and fifth centuries, the political institutions of Rome lose their capacity to maintain order and administer justice across the empire. Economic productivity diminishes as trade routes are disrupted and agricultural lands are abandoned. And by many measures the quantity of literary and philosophical production declines. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that during this very period, Christian theology and ecclesiastical literature flourish with remarkable vigour. Augustine composes works of profound philosophical depth; Jerome completes his great translation of the Scriptures; and the ecumenical councils produce documents of enduring intellectual significance. The cultural output of the civilisation is not uniformly deteriorating; it is being transformed and redirected. It appears that the definition, by treating cultural decline as a single undifferentiated phenomenon, fails to accommodate the possibility that political and economic decline may coincide with a creative transformation of cultural energies. Without a requirement of uniformity across all dimensions of cultural life, the analysis conflates metamorphosis with decline.

definition: An event or series of events R constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) R involves the forcible overthrow of an existing political authority, (ii) R results in a fundamental transformation of the political and social order, and (iii) R is carried out by a broad popular movement rather than by a narrow elite.
conditions: R involves the forcible overthrow of an existing political authority
R results in a fundamental transformation of the political and social order
R is carried out by a broad popular movement rather than by a narrow elite
counterexample: Suppose one considers the Meiji Restoration in Japan. It involved the forcible overthrow of the Tokugawa shogunate, a political authority that had governed for over two centuries. It resulted in a fundamental transformation of the political and social order: the feudal system was dismantled, a modern centralised state was established, and sweeping reforms were introduced in education, industry, and law. Yet the Meiji Restoration was carried out not by a broad popular movement but by a coalition of disaffected samurai from a small number of domains, acting in concert with court nobles. The third condition is not satisfied, and by the proposed definition the Meiji Restoration fails to qualify as a revolution, despite its transformative consequences being as profound as those of any event commonly so designated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that fundamental political and social transformation may be achieved by a narrow but strategically situated elite whose actions transform the lives of the entire population, so that the requirement of a broad popular movement excludes events whose revolutionary character is demonstrated by their consequences rather than by the social breadth of their agents.
passage: One might propose that a genuine revolution involves the forcible overthrow of an existing political authority, results in a fundamental transformation of the political and social order, and is carried out by a broad popular movement. This characterisation captures the violent, transformative, and democratic dimensions that we associate with the paradigmatic revolutions of modern history. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from Japanese history. Consider the Meiji Restoration. It involved the forcible overthrow of the Tokugawa shogunate, which had governed Japan for more than two centuries. It resulted in a fundamental transformation of the political and social order: the feudal system was dismantled, a modern centralised state was established, and sweeping reforms were introduced in education, industry, and law. Yet the Meiji Restoration was not carried out by a broad popular movement. It was the work of a coalition of disaffected samurai from a small number of domains, acting in concert with court nobles. By the proposed definition, the Meiji Restoration fails to qualify as a revolution, despite its transformative consequences being as profound as those of any event commonly so designated. It appears that the definition, by requiring broad popular participation, fails to recognise that fundamental transformation may be achieved by a narrow but strategically situated elite whose actions reshape the entire social order. Without this recognition, the analysis excludes events whose revolutionary character is manifest in their consequences rather than in the social composition of their agents.

definition: A practice or belief B constitutes a genuine tradition if and only if (i) B has been continuously transmitted from generation to generation over a substantial period, (ii) B is regarded by its practitioners as having ancient origins, and (iii) B has remained substantially unchanged in form and content throughout the period of its transmission.
conditions: B has been continuously transmitted from generation to generation over a substantial period
B is regarded by its practitioners as having ancient origins
B has remained substantially unchanged in form and content
counterexample: Suppose one considers the Highland clan tartan system in Scotland. The practice of associating specific tartan patterns with particular clans has been continuously transmitted from generation to generation since at least the early nineteenth century. It is regarded by its practitioners as having ancient origins stretching back to the medieval period. And the association of particular patterns with particular clans has remained substantially unchanged since the system was codified. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet historical scholarship has shown that the system of clan tartans was largely invented in the early nineteenth century, prompted by the romanticisation of Highland culture and the visit of George IV to Edinburgh in 1822. The practice satisfies every condition of the definition yet is not, in any meaningful historical sense, the ancient tradition it purports to be.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the attributed antiquity of the practice correspond to its actual historical origins, so that a recently invented practice may satisfy all conditions by being continuously transmitted, believed to be ancient, and unchanging in form, while in fact being a fabrication of relatively recent date.
passage: One might define a genuine tradition as a practice or belief that has been continuously transmitted from generation to generation, is regarded by its practitioners as having ancient origins, and has remained substantially unchanged in form and content. This characterisation captures the continuity, perceived antiquity, and formal stability that we associate with the idea of tradition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the cultural history of Scotland. Consider the Highland clan tartan system, in which specific patterns of woven cloth are associated with particular clans. This practice has been continuously transmitted from generation to generation since at least the early nineteenth century. It is regarded by its practitioners as having ancient origins, stretching back to the medieval period or beyond. And the association of particular patterns with particular clans has remained substantially unchanged since the system was codified. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, historical scholarship has shown that the system of clan tartans was largely invented in the early nineteenth century, prompted by the romanticisation of Highland culture and the state visit of George IV to Edinburgh in 1822. The practice is not, in any meaningful historical sense, the ancient tradition it purports to be. It appears that the definition, by accepting the practitioners' own belief in ancient origins rather than requiring that this belief be historically accurate, fails to distinguish genuine traditions of great antiquity from recently invented practices that acquire the appearance of venerability through continuous transmission and collective conviction.

definition: An historical figure F leaves a lasting legacy if and only if (i) F's actions or ideas produced consequences that persisted beyond F's own lifetime, (ii) those consequences are recognised and attributed to F by subsequent generations, and (iii) the consequences are evaluated positively by the community that inherits them.
conditions: F's actions or ideas produced consequences that persisted beyond F's lifetime
Those consequences are recognised and attributed to F by subsequent generations
The consequences are evaluated positively by the inheriting community
counterexample: Suppose a colonial administrator in the nineteenth century establishes a system of land tenure in a territory under his governance. The system persists for generations after his death. Subsequent generations recognise and attribute the system to this administrator. And the settler community that inherits the system evaluates it positively as a foundation of property rights and economic stability. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the same system dispossesses the indigenous population, concentrating land in the hands of settlers and creating inequalities that persist for over a century. The community that evaluates the legacy positively is not the only community affected; the indigenous population, which bears the brunt of the system's consequences, evaluates it as an instrument of injustice.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the evaluation of the consequences take into account the perspectives of all communities significantly affected by them, so that a legacy may satisfy all conditions when assessed from the perspective of a favoured group while being assessed very differently by those who suffered its effects.
passage: One might propose that an historical figure leaves a lasting legacy when the consequences of the figure's actions persist beyond the figure's lifetime, are recognised and attributed to the figure by subsequent generations, and are positively evaluated by the inheriting community. This characterisation captures the durability, recognition, and esteem that we associate with the notion of a lasting legacy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from colonial history. Suppose a colonial administrator in the nineteenth century establishes a system of land tenure in a territory under his governance. The system persists for generations after his death, shaping the distribution of property and the structure of rural society. Subsequent generations recognise the system and attribute it to this administrator by name. And the settler community that inherits the system evaluates it positively as a foundation of secure property rights and economic stability. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the same system dispossesses the indigenous population, concentrating land in the hands of settlers and creating inequalities that persist for more than a century. The community that evaluates the legacy positively is not the only community affected. The indigenous population evaluates the same legacy as an instrument of dispossession and injustice. It appears that the definition, by requiring only positive evaluation by the inheriting community, fails to demand that the evaluation encompass the perspectives of all communities significantly affected by the legacy. Without such a requirement, the analysis permits a legacy to be counted as lasting and positive when assessed from the perspective of a favoured group, while being regarded as destructive by those who bore its costs.

definition: A historical interpretation I is free of anachronism if and only if (i) I does not attribute to historical actors concepts that were unavailable in their period, (ii) I does not judge the actions of historical actors by moral standards that did not exist in their time, and (iii) I employs only terminology that was in use during the period under study.
conditions: I does not attribute to historical actors concepts unavailable in their period
I does not judge actions by moral standards that did not exist in their time
I employs only terminology in use during the period under study
counterexample: Suppose a historian writes about the governance of a medieval city-state using only terminology that was in use during the period, avoids attributing modern political concepts to the actors, and refrains from judging their actions by later moral standards. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the historian, by restricting herself entirely to the conceptual vocabulary of the period, is unable to identify patterns, structures, or causal mechanisms that the actors themselves could not have recognised. She cannot describe the economy of the city-state as feudal, for the concept of feudalism was not available to its inhabitants; she cannot identify demographic trends, for the concept of demography had not yet been formulated. The interpretation satisfies every condition of the definition yet is impoverished to the point of being uninformative.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that some degree of conceptual anachronism is methodologically indispensable, for the historian must employ analytical categories not available to the historical actors in order to identify patterns and structures that were invisible from within the period, so that a rigid prohibition on all anachronistic terminology renders historical understanding impossible.
passage: One might define freedom from anachronism as a condition in which the historical interpretation does not attribute concepts unavailable in the relevant period, does not judge actions by later moral standards, and employs only the terminology in use at the time. This characterisation captures the sensitivity to historical context that we rightly demand of responsible scholarship. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of medieval governance. Suppose a historian writes about the administration of a medieval city-state, using only the terminology available in the period, avoiding the attribution of modern political concepts, and refraining from moral judgements drawn from later standards. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the historian, by restricting herself entirely to the conceptual vocabulary of the period, renders herself unable to identify patterns, structures, and causal mechanisms that the actors themselves could not have recognised. She cannot describe the economy of the city-state as feudal, for the concept of feudalism was not available to its inhabitants. She cannot identify demographic trends, for the concept of demography had not been formulated. The interpretation is so faithful to the period's own self-understanding that it cannot rise above it. It appears that the definition, by prohibiting all conceptual and terminological anachronism, fails to recognise that some degree of anachronistic analysis is methodologically indispensable. Without the use of analytical categories not available to the historical actors, the historian cannot identify the structures and patterns that constitute the very substance of historical understanding.

definition: A text T constitutes a canonical work of an intellectual tradition if and only if (i) T has been continuously studied and commented upon within the tradition over a substantial period, (ii) T has shaped the vocabulary and conceptual framework employed by subsequent practitioners in the tradition, and (iii) T is regarded by a consensus of practitioners as indispensable to the tradition's self-understanding.
conditions: T has been continuously studied and commented upon over a substantial period
T has shaped the vocabulary and conceptual framework of subsequent practitioners
T is regarded by consensus as indispensable to the tradition
counterexample: Suppose a philosophical treatise written in the second century has been continuously studied and commented upon for nearly two millennia. It has shaped the vocabulary and conceptual framework of generations of scholars. And it is regarded by the consensus of practitioners as indispensable to the tradition's self-understanding. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the treatise has been preserved and transmitted only through translations and paraphrases, and the original text has been lost. The versions that practitioners study differ from one another in significant respects, and the vocabulary that has shaped the tradition derives partly from the original author and partly from the interpolations and interpretive choices of successive translators. The canonical status of the work rests upon a textual tradition that may not faithfully represent the original.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the text studied and commented upon bear a determinate and faithful relationship to its original form, so that a work may satisfy all conditions of canonicity while the text that practitioners actually study is a composite of the original and the interpretive contributions of successive transmitters.
passage: One might define a canonical work of an intellectual tradition as a text that has been continuously studied over a substantial period, has shaped the vocabulary and conceptual framework of subsequent practitioners, and is regarded by consensus as indispensable to the tradition's self-understanding. This characterisation captures the durability, conceptual influence, and communal authority that we associate with canonical status. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the transmission of ancient philosophical texts. Suppose a treatise written in the second century has been studied and commented upon for nearly two millennia. It has shaped the vocabulary and conceptual framework of generations of scholars. And it is regarded by the consensus of practitioners as indispensable. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the treatise has been preserved only through translations and paraphrases; the original text has been lost. The versions that practitioners study differ from one another in significant respects, and the vocabulary that has shaped the tradition derives partly from the original author and partly from the interpolations of successive translators. It appears that the definition, by requiring only continuous study, conceptual influence, and consensual regard, fails to demand that the text bear a determinate and faithful relationship to its original form. Without such a requirement, the canonicity of a work may rest upon a textual tradition that represents not the original author's thought alone but a composite of the original and the interpretive contributions of centuries of transmission.

definition: A civilisation has made progress if and only if (i) its material conditions of life have improved over a given period, (ii) its members enjoy greater individual liberty than before, and (iii) its arts and sciences have advanced beyond their earlier state.
conditions: Material conditions of life have improved over the period
Members enjoy greater individual liberty than before
Arts and sciences have advanced beyond their earlier state
counterexample: Consider a case in which a society industrialises rapidly, vastly improving material conditions, extending political freedoms to previously disenfranchised groups, and producing remarkable advances in the natural sciences. Yet this same process leads to the destruction of traditional communal bonds, widespread spiritual disillusionment, and a pervasive sense of purposelessness among its members. One might say that all three conditions of progress are met, yet it appears doubtful that a society suffering a profound crisis of meaning has truly progressed in the fullest sense.
missing_condition: Progress must include the preservation or enhancement of the social and spiritual well-being of the community, not merely material, political, and intellectual advancement.
passage: The notion of progress has occupied a central place in the philosophy of history since at least the eighteenth century. A natural analysis holds that a civilisation has progressed when its material conditions have improved, its members enjoy greater liberty, and its intellectual and artistic achievements have advanced. One might observe that this tripartite criterion accords with much of what the Enlightenment thinkers had in mind. Yet we should hesitate to accept it without qualification. Consider a case in which a society undergoes rapid industrialisation. Poverty is dramatically reduced, political franchise is extended to groups hitherto excluded, and the natural sciences achieve breakthroughs of the first order. By our analysis, this is a clear instance of progress. Suppose, however, that this same process of industrialisation dissolves the traditional communal bonds that had given meaning to the lives of ordinary persons. Families are scattered, religious observance declines not through enlightened choice but through dislocation, and a pervasive sense of purposelessness settles upon the population. The society is richer, freer, and more learned, yet its members report a diminished sense of well-being and connectedness. It appears that something important has been lost, and that to call this state of affairs simply progressive is to ignore a dimension of human flourishing that the analysis fails to capture. We are led to conclude that genuine progress must encompass the spiritual and communal life of a people, and not merely their material comfort, political liberty, and intellectual accomplishment.

definition: A historical explanation of an event is adequate if and only if (i) it identifies the agent or agents whose actions brought the event about, (ii) it specifies the intentions or motives of those agents, and (iii) it shows how those actions led to the event in question.
conditions: It identifies the agent or agents whose actions brought the event about
It specifies the intentions or motives of those agents
It shows how those actions led to the event in question
counterexample: Suppose that a historian explains the Irish Famine of the 1840s by identifying the British administrators who made policy decisions, specifying their intentions regarding free-market ideology and poor relief, and tracing how their actions led to mass starvation. This account satisfies all three conditions. Yet it omits the potato blight itself, a natural event without any agent, without which no amount of administrative decision-making would have produced the famine. The explanation is inadequate because it ignores non-agential structural and natural causes.
missing_condition: The explanation must also account for impersonal structural, economic, and natural conditions that contributed to the event, not merely the actions and intentions of identifiable agents.
passage: It is a question of enduring interest whether historical events can be explained solely by reference to the actions and intentions of individual agents. An influential analysis holds that a historical explanation is adequate when it identifies the relevant agents, specifies their motives, and shows the causal chain from their actions to the event. One might observe that this intentionalist approach captures much of what we find illuminating in narrative history. Yet we should hesitate to regard it as sufficient. Consider a case in which a historian seeks to explain the great Irish famine of the eighteen-forties. She identifies the British administrators who shaped relief policy, specifies their motivations, which were bound up with prevailing doctrines of political economy, and traces how their decisions led to the distribution failures that caused mass starvation. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. Nevertheless, it appears that this explanation is seriously incomplete, for it omits the potato blight, a natural catastrophe that struck without human intention or agency. Without the blight, the administrative decisions in question would not have produced famine. The historian who confines herself to agents and their motives misses a crucial structural and natural dimension of the event. We are led to conclude that an adequate historical explanation must also take account of impersonal conditions, whether ecological, economic, or demographic, that shape the context within which human agents act. The analysis as stated is insufficiently broad.

definition: A division of history into periods is valid if and only if (i) each period is characterised by a distinctive set of social, cultural, or political features, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant transformative events, and (iii) the periodisation applies consistently across the geographical region under study.
conditions: Each period is characterised by a distinctive set of features
Boundaries between periods correspond to significant transformative events
The periodisation applies consistently across the geographical region under study
counterexample: Consider a case in which the conventional division between the 'mediaeval' and 'modern' periods is placed at 1453, the fall of Constantinople. This event is transformative for the Eastern Mediterranean, and distinctive features can be identified for each period, with the periodisation applying consistently across Europe. Yet for sub-Saharan Africa or East Asia, this boundary has no significance whatsoever; the features characterising European periods do not obtain, and the event marking the boundary is irrelevant. The periodisation satisfies the conditions for one region but is presented as though it captures a universal historical structure.
missing_condition: The periodisation must be explicitly acknowledged as applying only to a specified cultural or geographical context, or else it must demonstrate cross-cultural validity, rather than illicitly universalising a regionally specific scheme.
passage: The division of the past into periods is among the most familiar operations of the historian, yet its philosophical basis is seldom examined with sufficient care. One might propose that a periodisation is valid when each period possesses distinctive features, the boundaries correspond to transformative events, and the scheme applies consistently within the region under study. This analysis appears to capture the essential desiderata. Yet we should hesitate to accept it without noting a serious difficulty. Consider a case in which the boundary between the mediaeval and modern ages is placed at the fall of Constantinople in 1453. This is undeniably a transformative event for the Eastern Mediterranean and for the Latin West, which received an influx of Greek scholars. Each period exhibits distinctive characteristics, and the scheme may be applied with tolerable consistency across the European continent. It appears, however, that this periodisation is profoundly misleading when extended beyond its region of origin. For the civilisations of sub-Saharan Africa, of China, or of the Americas, the fall of Constantinople marks no transformation whatever, and the features designated as mediaeval or modern in Europe have no counterparts. The periodisation meets the stated conditions for one region yet is frequently presented as if it described a universal structure of historical development. We are led to conclude that the analysis must either restrict its scope explicitly or else demand that a valid periodisation demonstrate genuine cross-cultural applicability.

definition: A society constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) it has developed a system of writing, (ii) it has established permanent urban settlements, and (iii) it has created a specialised division of labour.
conditions: It has developed a system of writing
It has established permanent urban settlements
It has created a specialised division of labour
counterexample: Suppose that the Inca empire possessed vast permanent cities, an elaborate division of labour encompassing administrators, soldiers, artisans, and farmers, and a sophisticated system of governance spanning thousands of miles. Yet the Inca did not possess writing in the conventional sense, relying instead on the quipu, a system of knotted strings, for record-keeping. By the proposed analysis, Inca society fails to qualify as a civilisation, a conclusion that appears plainly absurd given the complexity and achievement of their social organisation.
missing_condition: The analysis must recognise that information-recording systems need not take the form of writing in the narrow sense, or else it must abandon writing as a necessary condition and replace it with a broader requirement concerning the capacity for complex record-keeping and intergenerational transmission of information.
passage: The concept of civilisation has long served as a central category in the philosophy of history, yet its definition remains contested. A familiar analysis holds that a society is a civilisation when it has developed writing, established permanent urban settlements, and created a specialised division of labour. One might observe that this set of conditions captures much of what is meant when historians speak of the earliest civilisations of Mesopotamia and Egypt. Yet we should hesitate to suppose that these conditions are necessary in every case. Consider a case in which one examines the Inca empire of South America. Here was a society that governed a territory stretching some two thousand miles, organised vast public works, maintained an elaborate administrative hierarchy, and supported a population in permanent cities of considerable size. The division of labour was highly specialised. Yet the Inca did not develop writing as it is conventionally understood; their principal means of record-keeping was the quipu, a system of knotted cords encoding numerical and perhaps narrative information. By the analysis under consideration, Inca society fails to qualify as a civilisation, for it lacks the first condition. This conclusion appears manifestly inadequate. A society of such complexity and achievement cannot reasonably be excluded from the category of civilisation merely because its information-recording system took a non-alphabetic form. We are led to conclude that the requirement of writing is too narrow, and that the analysis must be broadened to encompass any effective system of complex record-keeping.

definition: A civilisation is in decline if and only if (i) its political institutions are losing the capacity to maintain order, (ii) its economic productivity is diminishing, and (iii) its cultural output is decreasing in quality and originality.
conditions: Its political institutions are losing the capacity to maintain order
Its economic productivity is diminishing
Its cultural output is decreasing in quality and originality
counterexample: Consider a case in which the late Roman Empire in the West experienced political fragmentation and diminishing economic productivity, yet the period simultaneously witnessed extraordinary cultural and intellectual output, including the works of Augustine, Boethius, and the flowering of early Christian art and architecture. Cultural output was not decreasing in quality; rather, it was transforming. This case satisfies two conditions but clearly violates the third, yet many historians would still characterise the period as one of decline.
missing_condition: The analysis must allow that decline may be partial rather than total, affecting some dimensions of a civilisation while others flourish, and must not require simultaneous deterioration across all domains.
passage: The concept of historical decline has been employed by historians and philosophers since antiquity, yet it proves remarkably difficult to define with precision. A natural analysis holds that a civilisation is declining when its political institutions lose their grip, its economy contracts, and its cultural production deteriorates. One might observe that this captures the intuitive picture of a society in decay. Yet we should hesitate to suppose that all three conditions must be met simultaneously. Consider a case in which the Western Roman Empire of the fourth and fifth centuries is examined. There can be little doubt that political institutions were fracturing; the capacity of the central government to maintain order across its vast territories was visibly diminishing. Economic productivity, too, was in many regions declining, as trade routes were disrupted and agricultural output fell. It appears, however, that cultural output presents a very different picture. The late imperial period produced Augustine of Hippo, whose philosophical and theological works rank among the most influential in the Western tradition, as well as Boethius, whose Consolation of Philosophy would shape thought for a millennium. Early Christian art and architecture were flourishing with extraordinary vitality. Here, then, is a civilisation that satisfies two of the three conditions yet simultaneously experiences a cultural transformation of the highest order. We are led to conclude that decline may be partial, affecting some dimensions of a civilisation while leaving others vigorous, and that an analysis requiring deterioration on all fronts is too rigid.

definition: A practice constitutes a living tradition if and only if (i) it has been transmitted continuously from one generation to the next, (ii) its practitioners regard it as inherited from the past, and (iii) it retains its original form and meaning.
conditions: It has been transmitted continuously from one generation to the next
Its practitioners regard it as inherited from the past
It retains its original form and meaning
counterexample: Suppose that the celebration of Christmas in Northern Europe has been transmitted continuously for centuries and its practitioners regard it as an ancient tradition. Yet its form and meaning have changed dramatically: originally a Christian liturgical observance, it has absorbed pagan Yule customs, Victorian domestic sentimentality, and modern commercial practices, so that its present form bears little resemblance to its mediaeval antecedent. It satisfies the first two conditions but fails the third, yet one would not wish to say it is therefore not a living tradition.
missing_condition: A living tradition need not retain its original form and meaning; rather, it must exhibit a recognisable continuity of development in which successive modifications are understood by practitioners as elaborations or adaptations of what was received.
passage: The notion of tradition is indispensable in the philosophy of history, yet its analysis presents unexpected difficulties. One might propose that a living tradition is a practice transmitted continuously across generations, regarded by its participants as inherited, and retaining its original form and meaning. This analysis seems to capture the essential features of what we mean when we speak of tradition. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition without qualification. Consider a case in which one examines the celebration of Christmas in Northern Europe over the past several centuries. There is no question that this practice has been transmitted continuously; nor is there any doubt that its participants regard it as an inheritance from the past. However, the form and meaning of the celebration have undergone profound transformation. What began as a strictly liturgical observance marking the Nativity has incorporated elements of pagan midwinter festivals, acquired elaborate Victorian domestic rituals involving decorated trees and gift-giving, and been further overlaid with modern commercial customs. The present form of the practice would be scarcely recognisable to a mediaeval participant. It appears that the third condition is not met, yet to deny that the celebration of Christmas is a living tradition would be absurd. We are led to conclude that the analysis errs in demanding preservation of original form and meaning. What is required instead is a recognisable continuity of development, in which each generation's modifications are experienced as adaptations of what was received rather than as wholesale inventions.

definition: A historical narrative is objective if and only if (i) it recounts events as they actually occurred, (ii) it does not impose a plot structure that the events themselves do not possess, and (iii) it selects facts on the basis of their historical significance rather than the narrator's preferences.
conditions: It recounts events as they actually occurred
It does not impose a plot structure that the events themselves do not possess
It selects facts on the basis of their historical significance rather than the narrator's preferences
counterexample: Suppose that a historian writes an account of the French Revolution that is scrupulously accurate in its factual claims, refrains from imposing a dramatic arc, and selects facts by widely accepted standards of significance. Yet by beginning with the financial crisis of 1789 and ending with the fall of Robespierre, the historian has already imposed a structure, for one could equally begin with the Enlightenment or end with Napoleon. Every act of selection and boundary-setting constitutes an imposition of structure, so the second condition can never be fully satisfied.
missing_condition: The analysis must acknowledge that narrative structure is an inescapable feature of historical writing rather than a defect, and that objectivity must be reconceived in terms of the adequacy of the narrative structure to the events, not its absence.
passage: The objectivity of historical narrative has been a matter of considerable debate. One familiar analysis holds that a narrative is objective when it recounts events accurately, refrains from imposing extraneous plot structures, and selects its materials by significance rather than preference. One might observe that this seems to articulate a laudable ideal of historical writing. Yet we should hesitate to suppose that these conditions can all be simultaneously satisfied. Consider a case in which a historian composes a narrative of the French Revolution. She is meticulous in her use of primary sources, and every factual claim she makes can be verified. She consciously avoids imposing a dramatic arc of rise and fall. She selects her materials according to their importance as judged by the wider community of scholars. It appears that her narrative meets all three conditions. However, the very act of beginning her account with the financial crisis of 1789 and concluding it with the fall of Robespierre in 1794 is itself a structuring decision. Another historian might begin with the intellectual currents of the preceding decades or extend the account through the Napoleonic period. Each choice of boundary frames the events differently, investing them with a shape they do not intrinsically possess. We are thus led to conclude that the imposition of structure is not an optional feature of narrative that can be avoided by the disciplined historian; it is constitutive of narrative itself. Objectivity in historical writing must therefore be reconceived, not as the absence of narrative structure, but as the adequacy and faithfulness of the structure adopted to the events under examination.

definition: A historical interpretation is anachronistic if and only if (i) it attributes to historical agents concepts that were unavailable in their time, (ii) these concepts play a central role in the interpretation, and (iii) the agents could not have acted upon or been influenced by such concepts.
conditions: It attributes to historical agents concepts unavailable in their time
These concepts play a central role in the interpretation
The agents could not have acted upon or been influenced by such concepts
counterexample: Consider a case in which a historian interprets the enclosure movements of eighteenth-century England using the concept of 'primitive accumulation' as described by Marx in the nineteenth century. The concept was unavailable to the enclosing landlords, it plays a central role in the interpretation, and the agents could not have acted upon it. Yet the interpretation may be perfectly illuminating as an analysis of structural economic forces that operated independently of the agents' conceptual repertoire. The analysis condemns as anachronistic an interpretation that is nonetheless historically valuable.
missing_condition: The analysis must distinguish between anachronism as the illicit attribution of concepts to agents' own thinking and the legitimate retrospective use of later concepts to analyse structural forces that operated beyond any agent's awareness.
passage: The charge of anachronism is among the most serious that can be levelled against a historical interpretation, yet it proves more difficult to define than one might initially suppose. A natural analysis holds that an interpretation is anachronistic when it attributes concepts unavailable in the relevant period, when these concepts are central to the account, and when the agents in question could not have acted upon them. One might observe that this analysis correctly identifies many clear cases of anachronistic thinking. Yet we should hesitate to apply it without discrimination. Consider a case in which a historian of eighteenth-century England analyses the enclosure movements using the Marxian concept of primitive accumulation, a concept formulated in the following century. The concept was plainly unavailable to the landlords and parliamentarians who effected the enclosures; it plays a central role in the historian's interpretation; and the agents could not have been influenced by it. All three conditions are satisfied, and the interpretation is therefore, by our analysis, anachronistic. Yet the historian is not claiming that the enclosing landlords thought of themselves as engaging in primitive accumulation. Rather, she employs the concept as an analytical tool to illuminate structural economic processes that operated independently of any agent's self-understanding. It appears that the interpretation is both anachronistic by the stated criteria and historically illuminating. We are led to conclude that the analysis must distinguish between the illicit projection of concepts into the minds of past agents and the legitimate retrospective application of analytical categories to structural phenomena.

definition: A historical figure has left a legacy if and only if (i) their actions or ideas have had lasting effects beyond their lifetime, (ii) these effects are recognised and attributed to the figure by subsequent generations, and (iii) the figure intended to produce such lasting effects.
conditions: Their actions or ideas have had lasting effects beyond their lifetime
These effects are recognised and attributed to the figure by subsequent generations
The figure intended to produce such lasting effects
counterexample: Suppose that a mediaeval monk, copying manuscripts purely as an act of devotion with no thought of posterity, inadvertently preserves the only surviving copy of an ancient philosophical text that profoundly influences Renaissance thought. His actions have lasting effects, and later generations attribute the preservation to him. Yet he had no intention of producing any legacy; his sole aim was pious obedience. By the analysis, he has left no legacy, which seems an unsatisfactory conclusion given the magnitude of his unintended contribution.
missing_condition: Legacy need not require the intention of the historical figure; unintended but significant and attributed effects may equally constitute a legacy.
passage: The concept of historical legacy is frequently invoked but seldom analysed with care. One might propose that a figure leaves a legacy when their actions have lasting effects, when these effects are recognised as attributable to the figure, and when the figure intended to produce such enduring influence. This analysis has the merit of distinguishing deliberate historical contributions from mere happenstance. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition as necessary. Consider a case in which a mediaeval monk, toiling in a scriptorium, copies a manuscript of an ancient philosophical work purely as an act of devotional obedience to his abbot. He has no thought of posterity; indeed, he may not even understand the Greek text he copies. Centuries later, this copy proves to be the sole surviving text of the work in question, and it exercises a profound influence upon the thinkers of the Renaissance. Subsequent generations recognise the monk's role and attribute the preservation to his labours. Here the first two conditions are plainly met: the effects are lasting and they are attributed to the monk. Yet the third condition fails entirely, for the monk intended nothing beyond the immediate fulfilment of his monastic duties. It appears perverse to deny that he has left a legacy merely because his contribution was unintentional. We are thus led to conclude that the analysis is too restrictive in requiring intention, and that a legacy may arise from actions whose historical significance far exceeds anything their author envisaged.

definition: An event constitutes a revolution if and only if (i) it involves a rapid and fundamental change in the political order, (ii) it is carried out by a mass movement rather than a small elite, and (iii) it results in the establishment of a new form of government.
conditions: It involves rapid and fundamental change in the political order
It is carried out by a mass movement rather than a small elite
It results in the establishment of a new form of government
counterexample: Consider a case in which the so-called Glorious Revolution of 1688 in England involved the rapid removal of James II and a fundamental alteration in the constitutional settlement. It resulted in the establishment of a new governmental arrangement under William and Mary. Yet it was accomplished not by a mass movement but by a small political elite in concert with a foreign prince, with little popular mobilisation. By the analysis, it fails to qualify as a revolution, though it is universally regarded as one of the most consequential revolutions in modern history.
missing_condition: The analysis must allow that revolutions may be carried out by elites as well as by mass movements, and should focus instead on the depth and character of the institutional transformation achieved.
passage: The concept of revolution occupies a prominent place in the philosophy of history, and yet the criteria for its application remain in dispute. A common analysis holds that an event is a revolution when it brings about rapid and fundamental political change, is carried out by a mass movement, and results in a new form of government. One might observe that this captures the essential features of such paradigmatic cases as the French Revolution of 1789. Yet we should hesitate to regard it as universally applicable. Consider a case in which one examines the English revolution of 1688, commonly called the Glorious Revolution. The political order was rapidly and fundamentally altered: the reigning monarch was deposed and replaced under conditions that significantly changed the constitutional settlement, establishing parliamentary supremacy in a manner that had not previously obtained. A new governmental arrangement was undeniably the result. However, the event was brought about not by a mass movement of the populace but by a relatively small group of political and ecclesiastical leaders acting in concert with William of Orange, a foreign prince who arrived with a Dutch army. Popular participation was minimal. By our analysis, this event fails to qualify as a revolution, for it does not meet the second condition. Yet historians universally regard it as revolutionary, and its consequences for constitutional government were immense. We are led to conclude that the requirement of mass participation is too restrictive, and that the analysis should attend instead to the depth of institutional transformation, regardless of the social breadth of the agency that brings it about.
